data.title: Why Britain faces the growing risk of Spanish-style blackouts
data.description:
The great British blackout was once a threat that hung over the country during the depths of winter, when generators struggled to meet demand or storms destroyed power lines.

But today, as the net zero revolution rolls on, the greatest threat to grid stability could land on a sunny Sunday afternoon.

According to the National Energy System Operator (Neso), the body responsible for keeping the lights on, the UK power grid is at increasing risk of being overwhelmed by surges in solar power.

Underpinning this threat is the fact that too many solar panels have been connected to the grid without any way of monitoring them or controlling their output.

As a result, a typically sunny day in summer can lead to nearly 19 gigawatts (GW) of power pouring into the UK’s networks, while only 12-15GW is being consumed.

That imbalance is a recipe for disaster because significant destabilisation of the grid can cause blackouts.

Such a calamity struck Spain and Portugal last year, when a chain reaction triggered by solar farms knocked out power to 55 million people.

At the time, solar and wind generated 80pc of energy in the Iberian Peninsula, the result of renewables displacing power stations run by nuclear and gas.

While a welcome switch for supporters of green energy, the removal of traditional power stations can prove problematic – as Spain and Portugal discovered last year.

This is because of their importance in providing vital system stability, known as inertia.

Inertia is created by generators with spinning parts – such as turbines running on gas, coal or hydropower – which wind and solar do not have.

This form of kinetic energy helps to prevent sudden changes in a system’s frequency.

Therefore, when two solar farms suddenly disconnected from Spain’s grid last year, there was no steady inertia to keep the country’s power networks afloat.

In practice, Neso is confident the UK will never see such a disaster. However, it admits the challenge is growing as ever more solar farms are built in Ed Miliband’s net zero drive.

“We are confident that we have the tools required to operate the system safely and reliably,” Neso said in its Summer Outlook report.

“But there may be periods when balancing supply, demand and system needs will require us to use our full range of operational tools, including system notices.”

However, independent experts are not so sure – and we may soon find out who is right. The crunch will probably come around the late May bank holiday, or on any sunny summer Sunday.

Last year, that date saw national demand for power plummet to a record low of 11GW, while sun and wind drove up power generation to nearly 19GW. And crucially, Neso could do little to stop it.

This sense of powerlessness is new. In the past, the grid operator’s response to an excess of power was simply to switch off some power stations.

But Neso’s problem is that it can no longer see what is happening on the UK’s power networks.

Unlike before, energy is being created naturally from wind and solar farms that Neso cannot control.

Underlying Britain’s energy grid are two main power networks.

At the top is the high-voltage transmission grid, which takes electricity from power stations to cities, towns and industry.

Everything on it is closely measured and monitored from Neso’s Electricity National Control Centre in Wokingham, Berkshire.

There, controllers use a giant wall-to-wall digital map of the transmission network to match supply to demand – switching power stations on or reducing output as needed.

Below that lies the distribution network, which takes electricity from the transmission grid, reduces its voltage, and pumps it into our homes and businesses.

It was designed to be a passive power carrier, meaning it lacks most of the switching or measuring systems found on the transmission grid. Ultimately, this makes it hard to monitor or control.

However, in the rush for decarbonisation, officials have been hooking the distribution system up to hundreds of new power sources, particularly solar farms.

Almost all of those solar generators are largely invisible to Neso’s operators, and they cannot see how much power is being generated or switch it off.

This now poses a problem, according to Simon Gallagher, the chief executive of UK Network Services, a leading consultancy.

“Traditionally, most power generators were connected to the transmission system and ‘dispatched’ centrally, so Neso can instruct them to turn on, turn off, ramp up and down to balance the grid in real time,” he says.

“Big power stations with large gas generators get tuned up and down as Neso monitors changes in demand.

“But solar is almost all connected to the distribution system and Neso has no control or even visibility of what it is doing. It means there is a risk of imbalances. On a sunny summer Sunday, demand will be very low, but solar will be generating at full capacity.”

However, whether this results in a blackout similar to Spain is another matter.

Some industry experts say an energy crisis is unlikely in the UK because its grid is structured differently, but regional failures and high costs remain a threat.

“Britain’s solar surplus problem is more about balancing costs than sudden system failure,” says Timothy Santonastaso, the deputy head of European power pricing at the consultancy Argus.

Such problems should have been addressed sooner, according to Claire Coutinho, the energy secretary in the last Conservative government and now a shadow minister.

‘Bogus numbers’

While in power, Coutinho says she became so worried about the impact of excessive solar disrupting the grid that she ordered civil servants to investigate.

This involved a review of the costs associated with balancing the grid when it is too windy or sunny.

Last year, the Government spent more than £1bn on paying wind farms to switch off in so-called curtailment costs.

“I started a full systems cost of wind and solar in the department because we need to know the true costs of renewables to consumers,” she says.

“I asked officials to prove to me that renewables were cheap and then realised their numbers were bogus.”

Coutinho claims Miliband has since cancelled her review. She also says the Energy Secretary is paying too much for net zero.

A spokesman for Miliband rejected the criticisms, instead claiming that solar and wind farms would bring huge benefits. “We are reversing decades of underinvestment and delivering the biggest upgrade to the grid in decades, which will minimise constraint costs, meet the capacity needed to deliver clean power by 2030 and help bring down bills for good,” he said.

He also played down the threat of grid disruption. “The National Energy System Operator has always had to manage changes in demand between the winter and summer months. They have well-established tools to manage the grid, including during times of low demand, which will not impact supply.”

The fresh row over solar is just the latest front in the battle over green energy.

Within Labour, Miliband’s renewables rollout has delighted the party faithful and made him increasingly popular among members.

However, it has also turned energy and climate policy, an area on which there was general political agreement until two years ago, into a culture war.

Leading the charge against Labour is Reform UK, which has pledged to disconnect solar farms if it wins power at the next general election.

“These solar farms are being built for the benefit of foreign investors, but they are destroying our countryside and ruining our environment,” says Richard Tice, Reform’s energy spokesman.

“It’s my living mission to stop this madness. Britain has done its bit – we’ve cut emissions by 50pc and impoverished ourselves doing it. Now’s the time to make Britain prosperous again, and that means cheap energy, lots of it, whether from gas, nuclear or oil.”

No doubt Miliband will disagree. However, the latest warning from Neso may at least make him sit up and notice.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Trump is an utter disaster for the world’s oil and gas industry
data.description:
The bittersweet joke in energy circles is that the Nobel committee should create a new climate prize to reward Donald Trump for his outstanding services.

Nobody has done more to accelerate the world’s electrotech revolution and pull forward the irreversible collapse of the oil and gas industry – not even Greta Thunberg, Al Gore or the strategic planners of the Chinese Communist Party.

The Carlyle Group made waves last year with a report called the “New Joule Order”. The giant US investment firm argued that seaborne molecules were no longer safe in the Hobbesian new world of America First and maritime anarchy.

Jeff Currie, its energy guru, said the open trading system of the last 80 years had been a function of American and allied power, relying on “the US navy as its muscle”.

This gift to the world, akin to the role of Britain’s Royal Navy in the 19th century, had allowed a constant stream of tankers carrying oil, diesel, naphtha, and liquefied natural gas (LNG) to criss-cross the oceans with scarcely a moment’s thought about choke points, taking for granted the international dollar system that lubricated trade so efficiently.

Currie said the “quest for security” was becoming the dominant driver of electrotech, as countries rushed to insulate themselves from geopolitical risk. It has little to do with climate change and even less to do with the red herring of net zero.

But not even he expected the US to lose its strategic compass so comprehensively as to attack Iran without securing the Strait of Hormuz, cutting off 8pc of total world trade for 48 days, including supplies of fertilisers, helium needed for semiconductors and a host of critical inputs that Washington forgot to think about.

Currie says the elemental contract of the petrodollar system is, or was, that the US would keep the sea lanes open and protect oil and gas producers from all foes. In return, the petrostates would recycle their vast earnings into US Treasuries and Wall Street, letting America enjoy the exorbitant privilege of inexhaustible cheap capital. “The grand bargain has been violated,” he said.

You might think that US oil and gas companies would be celebrating the windfall profits of Trump’s war, but the mood was closer to horror at the recent CeraWeek summit in Houston, the sanctum sanctorum of the drilling industry.

Casey Merriman, from Energy Intelligence, said the mood behind the scenes was “panic and dread” rooted in an awful premonition that the fallout of war would pose “existential risks to the future of oil and gas”.

Data from Ember show that 78pc of humanity lives in countries that are net importers of oil. These states have been bleeding $1.7tn (£1.25tn) a year just to pay oil rents to petrostates or frackers in Texas, and that could double again in short order if oil futures converge upwards to reflect the $140-$150 levels seen in the physical markets.

The importers have put up with this glaring anomaly out of inertia and the well-organised resistance of vested interests. Not any longer.

“Depending on fossil fuels is dangerous,” said Lee Jae-myung, the South Korean president, confessing that matters were now so grave that he could not sleep.

He has called for a fundamental rethink of South Korea’s energy strategy. “This is an emergency situation and we must shift to renewable energy very quickly,” he said.

France is doubling investment in electrification and will ban boilers in new homes at the end of this year. Variants of this debate are going on all over the world.

The double crisis of Trump’s war so soon after Vladimir Putin’s war has shaken the world out of complacency. All can now see that America has become a predator state. They can also see that a drone costing $20,000 is enough to stop a $150m tanker dead in the water.

Kingsmill Bond, Ember’s director of energy, said this shock had hit just as electrotech comes of age and is undercutting legacy fossil fuels on pure cost for power, heat and most land transport across the majority of the world’s populated regions.

“We didn’t have an alternative in past crises. Now we do. We have a suite of completely new solutions that change everything,” he said.

“Solar power coupled with storage is down to $60 [per] MWh across the global sunbelt. It costs $160 [per] MWh right now to cover just the cost of importing variable gas in Asia. It’s game over for LNG in Asia,” he said.

The figures for total net fossil imports as a share of primary energy are revealing, and not what you might think. To pick a few, they are: Japan (84pc), South Korea (80pc), Italy (72pc), Turkey and Spain (70pc), Germany (67pc), France (47pc), India and the UK (37pc) and China (24pc).

Britain is partially shielded by North Sea oil and gas working in tandem with fast-increasing volumes of North Sea wind power. The International Monetary Fund persists in claiming that this country is more vulnerable than peers to a fossil shock. It might usefully revisit its assumptions and stop repeating arrant nonsense.

Bond said the lesson of the two Opec crises in the 1970s is that it took the second shock in 1979 to cause a lasting shift in policy. “Global final oil demand per capita peaked in 1979 and has never recovered,” he said.

The response after the 1970s was to switch to more fuel-efficient cars and break the link between oil and GDP growth, but it could only go so far. This time the wider fossil fuel system faces terminal run-off in the importing countries, swept away by the scramble for safer homemade supply, which now also happens to be cheaper. Obviously, oil and gas will still be needed for jet fuel (for a while) and as a feedstock for petrochemicals.

China is already a post-oil, post-gas, electrostate economy, rolling out more wind, solar and electric vehicles each year than the rest of the world combined – although it has not cracked solid-state batteries for EVs and may yet be outflanked by the West.

What began as a strategic insurance policy against a future US naval blockade of China’s seaborne energy imports has become a juggernaut by now. Sales of EVs and plug-in hybrids were half of the Chinese car market last year. They are running at 60pc this month.

The Chinese near-abroad is now slotting into this new joule order. EV sales are around 40pc in Vietnam and Singapore, 25pc in Thailand, 18pc in Indonesia, and rising.

Laurence Tubiana, the chief convenor of the Paris Climate Accord, said that as recently as five years ago African leaders would bridle at green colonialism, accusing the West of trying to deny the Global South a cheap energy route out of poverty.

Today the mood is radically different. Country after country has done its sums and concluded that it could leapfrog centralised, fossil-based grids and opt instead for solar micro-grids, slashing costs and freeing itself of fuel-import bondage. “They have no interest any longer in being trapped in the old energy system,” she said.

We have not felt the full impact of this crisis yet. Currie said it reminded him of the eerie insouciance in early days of the pandemic. First it hit China and shut the economy: the world reacted as if the virus had nothing to do with them. Then it hit South Korea, then Iran, then Italy, and still stock markets kept rising. It took six weeks before Wall Street suddenly buckled and crashed 35pc along with a seizure in the US Treasury market.

Trump’s war will hit home once the physical barrels stop arriving and cannot be bought for love or money, a moment scheduled for late April once the usable global buffer of 800 million barrels is exhausted.

Currie has another warning as the S&P 500 reached new highs based on nothing more than Truth Social tweets. The US oil and gas companies make up just 3pc of the equity capitalisation of the index; the other 97pc is on the wrong side of the Gulf War trade and will soon suffer the consequences of what remains the biggest energy shock of modern times.

Wars have a bad habit of spinning out of control.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Heathrow’s new car parks to cost more than Gatwick’s second runway
data.description:
From HS2 to Crossrail, British infrastructure projects lead the world in one unenviable respect: their sizeable, often runaway budgets.

The bill for big-ticket construction schemes typically starts off with eye-watering sums – and then climb to even more staggering levels as budgets are blown and costs run rampant.

Now a plan to provide 36,000 parking spaces at Heathrow airport is poised to join the pantheon of cash-hungry mega-projects after it was revealed that it will come with a staggering £2.6bn price tag.

That’s £400m more than the estimated outlay for a new runway at Gatwick and more than half the cost of Heathrow’s Terminal 5 – the largest free-standing structure in the UK.

At £72,000 per parking space, the two multi-storey car parks planned under the scheme will also be comfortably the most expensive in Britain and a contender for the costliest ever built.

Heathrow argues the price per space will be lower because only half the overall cost, about £1.3bn, is for the buildings themselves, with the rest of the spending going towards associated infrastructure such as bus facilities.

Yet the airport has faced criticism from some of the world’s largest airlines, who will ultimately pick up the bill for the world’s most expensive car park.

In a week when National Car Parks (NCP), the country’s biggest car park operator, collapsed into administration, the plan might seem an ambitious one.

However, unlike NCP, which was hit by a drop in commuting, Heathrow can rely on a captive customer base and funding rules that mean construction costs are ultimately likely to be passed on as aircraft landing fees.

Airline bosses have attacked the project as typical of Heathrow’s largesse in lavishing cash on gold-plated enhancements that they and their customers must ultimately pay for.

The airport says the car parks will be a vital component of its £49bn plan for a third runway that will lift passenger numbers from 84 million a year to as many as 150 million.

It says the sites – one next to the M4 and the other to the south near the M25 – will be needed to cope with the thousands of additional passengers driving to what is already Europe’s busiest airport each day, even if a higher proportion of people take public transport.

And it argues that the expense of the project reflects construction costs in London that are themselves amongst the highest in the world.

But Luis Gallego, the boss of British Airways (BA) owner International Airlines Group, said the plan was overpriced.

He highlighted the shortcomings of a funding model that allows Heathrow to charge airlines a regulated fee to cover building and operating costs.

“We support expansion, but not at any cost. All future spending must be carefully controlled as Heathrow already charges passengers twice as much as Amsterdam and three times as much as Paris,” he said.

Willie Walsh, the director general of the International Air Transport Association, was blunter, describing the car parks plan as “madness” and saying his mind “boggled” at the £2.6bn bill.

He said Heathrow had proposed what would have been the world’s most expensive car park when he was chief executive of BA as part of a previous runway plan that was rejected in 2010.

He said: “You can find data on what it costs to build a car park, there’s lots of evidence there, and Heathrow is just way out of line with the rest of the world.

“The problem is that they are incentivised to spend as much money as possible and to build the most expensive car park and to be proud of that.”

Heathrow has said that calling the new structures car parks does not do them justice.

Thomas Woldbye, the airport’s chief executive, has instead referred to them as “parking villages”.

The blueprint for the new runway proposes “two large parkway car parks” that would replace more than 20 existing facilities, with the northern site able to accommodate 20,000 vehicles and the southern one 16,000.

It adds: “This could support fast and efficient connections to each terminal campus via dedicated transport systems.

“These new parkways could also provide local communities with improved access to airport terminals and public transport links and help reduce congestion on local roads.”

While a leaked Heathrow briefing paper states the cost of the parkways to be £2.6bn, a spokesman said the parking areas alone will consume only about half the budget, with the rest covering the cost of associated infrastructure such as bus facilities.

Major profit centre

The car parks may be necessary to cope with an influx of new passengers, but they would also be a major profit centre, with parking fees contributing £200m in revenue last year.

Heathrow’s long-stay car parks currently charge £46.80 for the first day and £37.40 for each additional 24-hour period.

Taking an average of £40 per day, the number of spaces planned could generate more than £500m in revenue over the course of a year.

That is not including income from drop-off charges and the short-stay car parks nearer to terminals that the airport has indicated it plans to retain.

Michael O’Leary, the chief executive of Ryanair, which does not fly from Heathrow, said drop-off fees were “a scandal” across UK airports and that parking had been turned into a cash cow integral to their business models.

He said: “The more they earn from car parks the more they will dividend it out to their shareholders and then complain that they can’t make a reasonable return on the risks they were taking investing in runways in London.

“But there’s no risk at all, it’s a complete freebie, which is why pension funds now own most of these airports.”

O’Leary said the cost of runways, car parks and terminals was ultimately the result of a “broken” regulatory model that “rewards inefficiency and incentivises airports to squander gazillions”.

A study from Oxford Global Projects commissioned by Heathrow Reimagined, a lobby group formed by airlines including BA, has suggested that Heathrow’s construction costs across a variety of infrastructure including car parks are consistently among the highest in the world.

The report said the cost of the runway alone – covering cement, asphalt and lights – would come in at more than £800,000 per metre, making it one of the most expensive ever built.

Jonathan Simcock, author of The Delivery Gap, an examination of failed infrastructure projects, said companies like Heathrow have an incentive to inflate estimated costs by “including all the bells and whistles they can”.

In so doing, he said, they aim to secure regulatory approval for levels of spending – in Heathrow’s case to be funded by airlines and their customers – that will cover all eventualities and “ensure they are not left holding the baby”.

“If the estimate is inflated then the company ends up making an inappropriate profit. But if it’s undercooked the company gets into trouble, somebody has to bail them out and the country doesn’t get the infrastructure it needs,” he says.

Woldbye has said that the costings for the car parks and the wider runway project could change and that Heathrow is open to conversations about modifying its plans.

However, Simcock warned: “When the conversation is about how it can possibly cost that much, very often it turns out that it should have been, can you really do it for that?

“For very large and highly complex HS2-type things – and Heathrow is big and complex – it is rare to get nice surprises and much more common to get nasty ones.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Britain in danger of outsourcing its intellect, warns AI pioneer
data.description:
The artificial intelligence revolution threatens to erode Britain’s sovereignty, one of the country’s most experienced tech entrepreneurs has warned.

Ken Mulvany, co-founder and executive chairman of Benevolent AI, which has pioneered artificial intelligence in drug discovery, told The Telegraph that the United States and China are increasingly dominant in the crucial field.

He says: “If you look at this from the UK or Europe’s perspective, the risk is that these – China and America – are now the two cognitive powers. They’re the ones that are investing the most into this.

“I do worry about having the nation’s intellect effectively outsourced. It’s not just that, either. It’s the productivity gains.

“If for every hour of cognitive work that you do there’s a machine doing what would take you 50 hours, how do you as a nation compete with that?

“Electricity multiplied the power of muscles and AI can do the same with brains in certain scenarios. But it’s almost like you, as Britain and Europe are outsourcing national IQ to US and Chinese tech companies.

“So you’re left trying to regulate something you don’t own. And I do worry about that. I think of it as cognitive border control.”

Mulvany is better placed than most to sound the alarm. An eerily youthful 57 who betrays his Hawaiian roots with a surf-ready hairdo, he is an entrepreneur who has seen tech boom and bust more than once.

In a world increasingly haunted by visions of an AI apocalypse that would make the dotcom crash look like a minor market setback, Mulvany and Benevolent have already been tested to near-destruction.

The technology excited investors – including the star fund manager Neil Woodford long before his self-inflicted implosion – enough to snag a £1.5bn valuation way back in 2018.

This was long before the likes of Sam Altman of OpenAI and Dario Amodei of Anthropic emerged to seek thrones as the next generation of tech plutocrats.

Benevolent sought to help researchers to quickly design new drugs with computer assistance, using machine learning techniques to scour a treasure trove of science information for possibilities at an unprecedented clip. Expansion into defence and energy markets promised new horizons and riches.

It seemed to work. Benevolent burst onto the public markets long before the current AI frontrunners had attracted the attention of everyday investors, too. With the world still in the grip of the pandemic and having discovered an anti-inflammatory that was proving useful on pandemic wards, the start-up made a splashy debut.

“I wasn’t really part of it at that time,” Mulvany recalls ruefully. Having seemingly set up Benevolent for enormous success, he was already easing into semi-retirement.

As it secured a lucrative listing in Amsterdam, Benevolent won endorsement and investment from some of the pharmaceutical industry’s biggest names. AstraZeneca backed Benevolent as part of a transaction that raised hundreds of millions.

Alas, Mulvany’s big payday and exit did not set the scene for a sustainable company. A starry board and big executive hires steered Benevolent off a cliff.

The company lost almost all its value within two years, prompting Mulvany to abandon his early retirement and mount a boardroom coup to take back control last year. He had become frustrated that Benevolent had shifted from his concept of a technology company to a drug developer in its own right, with all the risk and costs associated with it.

“They brought in some luminaries,” Mulvany recalls. “Some really heavy hitters from pharma. And Benevolent morphed from a tech company to a pharma company. For me as an investor, and for some of my fellow investors, we didn’t have the appetite for that. We were technology investors.

“You had a technology shareholder base and a management and board doing biotech. All of its revenue was from its technology but it was spending a ton of money developing drugs.

“The technology cost was very low relative to drug development. So I became more of an activist shareholder at that point because it wasn’t going the way I wanted it to go and the markets reflected that too. It lost 95pc of its value.”

It’s a tale of hype meeting with financial and scientific reality that now seems to be playing out on an extreme scale as OpenAI and its rivals test the limits of pre-profit funding and investor patience. Altman alone has committed to spending more than $1tn (£750bn).

Having abandoned his retirement to mount his successful coup, Mulvany is now rebuilding Benevolent as an enterprise software company. Its new software and services are focused sharply on helping drug developers, rather than developing drugs. It’s a more commercial proposition, with a closer eye on returns for Benevolent and its big pharma customers.

“We help on the science side of things, reviewing the literature, but also how you make business decisions as a drug developer, how you expand into a particular market, how you design trials and all of that.

“People in the industry are making decisions with a fraction of the information they need.”

These are still the early days of Benevolent’s attempted comeback and Mulvany’s unexpected return from early retirement, which he had intended to spend skiing. His hopes of using the company’s targeted AI technology beyond drug discovery in fields such as defence and energy have been revived.

Benevolent, without revenue, now has the cash and investor support to pursue its new business into 2027, according to Mulvany.

Despite the mounting fears of an AI bust, he remains a believer in the technology. After a deep cull of staff, Benevolent’s coders are 50 times faster with the help of computer intelligence, for instance. But Mulvany, now an AI survivor, admits a shake-out lies ahead for those with grandiose visions.

“I saw this in the dotcom. I was there right at the bubble. But whether it’s railroads, or electricity or the internet there has always been over-investment and hype.

“So there’s going to be losers in this but the infrastructure is being put in place. So yeah, you’re seeing an AI bubble, but I’ve seen it before.”

data.title: A 1960s bungalow transformed into a dream home
data.description:
When Elizabeth Taylor stumbled across an outdated 1960s bungalow hidden away in the West Sussex village where she grew up, she saw beyond its shabby façade. In its place came a vivid vision shaped by her travels along America’s East Coast, from New England to the Florida Keys, where she had fallen for the understated charm of clapboard houses. Painted white with dormer windows and wide porches, often with a swing, these rather romantic homes were typically set close to water. The only thing linking this property to those in America was its coastal setting, a small river running through its grounds and Elizabeth’s ability to imagine a complete transformation.

At the time, Elizabeth, a wedding photographer, was living nearby in a 1930s semi – her third renovation – and had no plans to move. But when she spotted the bungalow in an estate agent’s window, her curiosity was piqued. A viewing revealed a house hidden at the end of a private road, screened by towering leylandii and, inside, a range of Sixties features such as York-stone fireplaces, vinyl finishes, Artex ceilings and veneered wardrobes. It wasn’t the prettiest of houses, but it was detached, with a big garden. “It certainly wasn’t my dream home, but it had scope to go every which way, including up,” says Elizabeth. “I could picture it surrounded by azaleas, magnolias and camellias.” Six weeks later, in 2012, she had sold her home and moved in.

Decorating in stages

As Elizabeth had a young child and a limited budget, progress was gradual. “I started with the basics, just to make it liveable,” she says. A new bathroom was top of the list – particularly after her son discovered the walk-in bath would flood the room if it was opened mid-soak. Other rooms were stripped back and refreshed. “It smelt of the Sixties for about four years, until we finally got all the wallpaper and carpet out,” Elizabeth recalls.

While these early years didn’t bring about much change, they gave her time to think. “I’d previously renovated older homes with strong architectural features to steer design choices, but it was clear this project was going to be different,” she says. “I ended up concluding that I would just have to trust my instinct.”

In 2014, her now-husband Jamie Taylor, a builder and carpenter, entered the picture, and plans began to take shape. 

Reimagining the layout

The original layout needed rethinking. “It was completely disjointed,” says Elizabeth. She and Jamie worked with an architect to develop plans that balanced ambition with budget: “I thought having lots of options was a good thing, but it just sent me down endless Pinterest rabbit holes – unable to make decisions.” Rather than extending dramatically outwards, they eventually chose to build upwards to echo the American aesthetic that she so admired. The plans included loft and garage conversions, a modest extension and the addition of a veranda. Extending the front by just 1.5 metres to accommodate a hallway and porch proved transformative. “It was a small change, but it made a huge difference,” says Elizabeth.

Inside, the footprint remained similar, but awkward spaces were opened up. The cramped kitchen-diner, garage access and an odd outhouse were combined into one generous room, now the family’s day-to-day living space. “All those little nooks disappeared and suddenly the house made sense.”

Creating a budget kitchen with character

With the layout established, creating a kitchen with personality was central to Elizabeth’s vision, but staying within budget was a challenge. “Everything I loved was far too expensive,” she says. The solution came via eBay, where she found some old-school science cabinets. The cabinet doors had flat fronts but shaker-style backs, so they were simply reversed and rehung. Elizabeth then repainted each piece by hand, preserving the original lettering and brass fittings. “At one point I was using a tiny make-up brush to get into all the details,” she recalls. Glazed cabinets from the same find were fitted above. “Everyone thought I was mad putting in so many hours, but it turned out well and was a real bargain.”

Professionally fitted new quartz worktops added a touch of luxury, and they kept costs down elsewhere by reusing a sink from the old utility room. The space was designed to be sociable as well as practical, with a sofa, a salvaged island to gather around and a large dining table. Scaffold boards were lightly hand-sanded and varnished for flooring, and much of the furniture was sourced second-hand. “By using reclaimed materials and pre-loved furniture, we’ve created real character here,” says Elizabeth, who credits this approach with helping to shape and guide her overall design direction.

A resourceful approach

The former garage became a bedroom for Elizabeth’s son, Jack (now 21), with a vaulted ceiling and a mezzanine. The space between the kitchen and garage was enclosed to form a hallway and bathroom, creating a self-contained suite. Reclaimed scaffold boards were again used for flooring and stairs, while iron spindles from a car boot sale form the balustrade. “Scaffold boards have been a real hero material,” says Elizabeth. “They’re incredibly versatile and bring a warmth and texture that new materials just don’t have.”

The loft was also converted into a spacious bedroom with a dormer bathroom, and retained its dramatic vaulted ceiling.

Throughout, resourcefulness was key: “I wanted a high-end finish that reflected my taste, but we didn’t always have the budget,” says Elizabeth. Reclaimed materials, second-hand finds and Jamie’s craftsmanship kept costs manageable: only around 5 per cent of the materials and furnishings used in the project are new. The work was often done in stages, and sometimes they had to pause and wait for the time, money or motivation to continue.

Over the years, the total cost has reached around £250,000. “We never set a strict budget,” says Elizabeth. “We just had a vision and worked towards it whenever we could.” Part of the vision was to create a seamless connection between inside and out, achieved through the use of two complementary shades of cream across both the clapboard exterior and the interior.

Transforming the garden

Their attention then turned to the overgrown garden, beginning with the removal of the front hedging. “While it kept us completely private, it wasn’t the view we wanted,” says Elizabeth. “Taking it down was the best thing we could have done.” The change revealed the riverbank and mature trees, including elegant weeping willows, creating a stronger connection to the landscape.

This new outlook helped to define the vision for the rest of the garden. An old summer house was replaced with a purpose-built photography studio made from larch wood and reclaimed Crittall windows, while a greenhouse was created from salvaged materials. Landscaping introduced structure, with new paths, defined areas and a central patio. The result is a garden that feels open yet secluded.

“It finally feels like the garden belongs to the house,” says Elizabeth; and she feels she belongs there too. The project has become a deeply personal expression of everything she loves, and it seems a home made with love is a recipe for success.

See more on Instagram at @thetaylormadehouse

data.title: Hundreds of Hollywood stars oppose Paramount’s Warner Bros takeover
data.description:
Hundreds of Hollywood stars have come out against Paramount’s takeover of Warner Bros, claiming the deal to hand control of the studio to supporters of Donald Trump will hurt democracy.

Actors, including Jane Fonda, Glenn Close and Ben Stiller, have signed their names on an open letter expressing “unequivocal opposition” to the tie-up, arguing that competition is “essential for a healthy economy and a healthy democracy”.

The letter said the takeover “would further consolidate an already concentrated media landscape”, leading to “fewer opportunities for creators” and “less choice for audiences” worldwide.

It added: “We are deeply concerned by indications of support for this merger that prioritises the interests of a small group of powerful stakeholders over the broader public good.”

More than a thousand actors, screenwriters, producers, directors and publicists signed the letter, which called for Rob Bonta, the California attorney general, to block the deal.

Signatories included: Joaquin Phoenix, star of Joker; Twilight actress Kristen Stewart; US Traitors host Alan Cumming; Bryan Cranston, Breaking Bad’s lead actor; and Slow Horses star Kristin Scott Thomas.

Directors including David Fincher, known for Fight Club and Gone Girl, and Denis Villeneuve, who is behind the Dune franchise, also signed their names on it.

Paramount agreed to buy Warner Bros for $111bn (£82bn) in February after outbidding Netflix, which had earlier struck a deal to pay $82bn for the film studio.

The takeover has fuelled fears that the billionaire Ellison family will be handed outsized control over American media. Paramount has already taken control of CBS News and Larry Ellison played a central role in the takeover of TikTok’s US operations.

Mr Ellison and his son David Ellison, who runs Paramount, are both close to Mr Trump.

RedBird Capital, the US private equity fund that mounted a takeover bid for The Telegraph and is Paramount’s second-largest shareholder, is also backing the bid.

Ms Fonda said: “A Warner Bros Discovery-Paramount merger would be one of the most destructive threats to free speech and creative expression in our history.

“This deal would place unprecedented power in the hands of a single corporation that already appears to have proven itself willing to sacrifice integrity for political favour.”

Paramount took control of CBS News in an $8bn deal last year and has installed Bari Weiss, the founder of “anti-woke” news site The Free Press, as its editor-in-chief. Mr Ellison’s Oracle Corporation was also part of the consortium that acquired TikTok’s operations.

Growing concerns in Hollywood

Senator Elizabeth Warren, a senior Democrat for Massachusetts, said in February: “A handful of Trump-aligned billionaires are trying to seize control of what you watch and charge you whatever price they want.”

The open letter said: “Increasingly, a small number of powerful entities determine what gets made – and on what terms – leaving creators and independent businesses with fewer viable paths to sustain their work.”

Mr Ellison, the 81-year-old founder of Oracle Corporation, is a long-time friend and supporter of Trump and has helped fund his political campaigns.

As well as raising concerns about free speech, the Hollywood stars who put their names to the letter warned the Warner Bros’ takeover would endanger “the professional lives of the tens of thousands of workers”.

The letter said: “Our industry is already under severe strain, in large part due to prior waves of consolidation. We have witnessed a steep decline in the number of films produced and released, alongside a narrowing of the kinds of stories that are financed and distributed.”

The UK’s competition watchdog separately said on Monday that it would investigate Paramount’s takeover of Warner Bros.

A spokesman for the Competition and Markets Authority said: “The film and TV industries contribute billions to our economy, so it’s important we assess whether deals between studios may harm competition.”

A Paramount spokesman said: “Paramount remains deeply committed to talent, and this merger strengthens both consumer choice and competition, creating greater opportunities for creators, audiences and the communities they live and work in.”

Warner Bros was contacted for comment.

data.title: GB News accuses Labour MP of spreading populism ‘conspiracy theory’
data.description:
The chairman of GB News has accused a senior Labour MP of peddling a conspiracy theory over claims the channel is a mouthpiece for populism.

Alan McCormick, a partner at GB News co-owner Legatum, has criticised Liam Byrne after he claimed the broadcaster’s investors were “volatility traders” hoping to cash in on chaos in Britain.

Writing in The Telegraph, Mr McCormick said the claim “verges on conspiracy theory”, adding: “It demonstrates an ignorance about how the media works.”

Mr Byrne, who chairs the House of Commons’s business and trade committee, has accused GB News’s backers of seeking to promote populist politicians to unleash market chaos they can profit from.

The Labour MP commissioned research for his book, Why Populists are Winning and How to Beat Them, that found more than £170m had been handed to media organisations, politicians and think tanks aligned with the populist Right over the last five years.

In a robust rebuttal, Mr McCormick dismissed claims of political bias at GB News as “nonsense” and insisted the channel provided a platform for a more diverse array of opinions than any of its rivals.

He wrote: “The money invested in GB News by its investors has also been wildly mischaracterised in various places as being politically motivated. This is simply not the case.

“GB News was founded to surface the topics the British people want to talk about and the ideas that work, taking it off the political continuum, and not aligned with any political party.”

In response, Mr Byrne told The Telegraph: “Well, he would say that, wouldn’t he?”

He added: “Ofcom know exactly what they’re supposed to be doing to impose some proper rules at GB News – they need to get on and do it.”

It marks a rare intervention by Mr McCormick, who was appointed as chairman of GB News in 2022 following the abrupt departure of founder Andrew Neil. Dubai-based hedge fund Legatum co-owns the channel alongside the hedge fund tycoon Sir Paul Marshall.

The investors have pumped more than £140m into the loss-making channel since its inception five years ago. The latest accounts for the year to March 2025 show they injected a further £17.7m as losses narrowed to £22m.

GB News has repeatedly surpassed the BBC News channel and Sky News for audience numbers while revenues have grown as the channel shrugged off an advertising boycott that dogged its early years.

But the broadcaster has been embroiled in repeated controversies over impartiality, including its use of politicians such as Nigel Farage and Sir Jacob Rees-Mogg as presenters.

The channel was fined £100,000 by regulator Ofcom in 2024 for breaking impartiality rules in a programme featuring Rishi Sunak, who was the prime minister at the time.

It has also previously been censured for airing conspiracy theories linked to Covid-19 vaccines.

However, GB News scored a major victory over the regulator last year, when the High Court overturned a ruling that the channel had breached the broadcasting code in a programme presented by Sir Jacob.

Ofcom subsequently planned to tighten its rules to block politicians from acting as presenters on any show on TV or radio, but dropped the proposals in the face of fierce opposition from GB News and Rupert Murdoch’s News UK.

The picture Liam Byrne paints verges on conspiracy theory

By Alan McCormick, the chairman of GB News

Why would anyone invest in a broadcast news channel today? In our age of streaming and our attention-based economy, combined with declining television consumption, it may not seem like a growth industry. What’s more, the “media mogul” is hardly the most universally loved of stock characters.

Yet that is where Legatum, the private investment firm in which I am a partner, happily finds itself in 2026. Alongside Sir Paul Marshall, we invested in All Perspectives Ltd, the parent company of GB News, in 2021 when the channel launched.

The main reason we invested is very simple. It’s the same reason that investors back almost any project: we believed it would be a sound commercial enterprise. And more, we believed it would meet an audience need that was unmet by the incumbent providers.

Five years later, that judgment has been thoroughly vindicated – GB News is Britain’s number-one news channel.

As of March 2026, it has beaten both the BBC and Sky in seven of the last eight months, both in average audience and audience share.

In February, it had an average audience of 99,000 viewers, up 13pc on the previous month and far surpassing the BBC’s 78,400. Revenues have increased 65pc in the last 12 months, and while the company has been loss-making, we are patient and confident that in time the business will prosper.

Far from making the most of what remains of a declining industry, GB News is at the cutting edge of media technology. GB News is a technology platform, having pioneered hardware that is now being used by the rest of the industry. Visit one of our studios and you will find a slick, automated operation which embraces new tech and AI unencumbered by legacy broadcast hardware and practices.

Why did we believe that GB News would prove to be a sound investment? There is a clue in the name of the channel’s parent company: All Perspectives. To us, it was evident that a major gap existed in the media marketplace for voices that were routinely ignored by legacy broadcasters – not fringe voices, but those of normal communities all across Britain.

The decline of broadcast media cannot be solely blamed on the rise of the internet and streaming. It is also a product of people switching off because the stories which matter to them have been ignored by broadcasters for a very long time. Viewers simply didn’t want what the established channels were selling.

This judgement has been vindicated. Viewers are tuning in to GB News in droves, largely because they finally hear their perspectives being taken seriously and given airtime. And yet they are also exposed to genuine debate, making nonsense of suggestions that the channel is a mouthpiece for a particular political agenda.

GB News guests and panels showcase far more diversity of opinion than its competitors. Rather than the carefully choreographed and contained “discussions” found on other channels, GB News puts those with opposite views into conversation with one another, and allows viewers to decide for themselves.

Some people have their own ideas about why we would make such an investment. In the course of promoting his new book on so-called populism, Liam Byrne MP has suggested that we, investors in GB News, are “volatility traders”, planning to disrupt Britain in order to profit from future chaos by making big bets on the markets.

Investors certainly do plenty of sophisticated, long-term thinking, but the picture Mr Byrne paints verges on conspiracy theory. It demonstrates an ignorance about how the media works. While there are certainly easier and speedier ways to drive returns, we view this as a very significant, long-term investment in British journalism.

The money invested in GB News has also been wildly mischaracterised in various places as being politically motivated. This is simply not the case. GB News was founded to surface the topics the British people want to talk about and the ideas that work, taking it off the political continuum, and not aligning with any party.

The investors behind GB News have always been guided by the value of competition and free enterprise, which is a fundamental British ideal. That applies to our investment in a media company as much as any other project. We sincerely value an open media landscape, one in which genuinely diverse views compete on a level playing field.

News channels should be free to present their very best broadcasting, to win viewers to their conclusions, and to generate revenue whilst they do so.

Such a liberated media landscape is what many critics of GB News say they want too. If they are as sincere about that as we are, then GB News’ success should be welcomed by its competitors and critics as much as by its rapidly growing audience.

data.title: Rolex-wearing robot chases wild boars off the streets
data.description:
A Rolex-wearing humanoid robot named Edward has become a viral sensation in Poland after he was filmed chasing a pack of wild boars down the street.

Edward Warchocki, also known as Edek, is a 1.3-metre-tall fully autonomous android.

He was bought from Chinese manufacturer Unitree by two Polish IT entrepreneurs, who have modified the technology and launched a high-profile publicity campaign.

In one video posted on the robot’s official social media account, Edward can be seen dashing through a Warsaw car park at night in pursuit of a number of wild boars.

The clip, which has racked up almost four million views on X, is accompanied by a caption reading: “I’m herding the wild boar into the forest.”

It is one of a number of viral videos of Edward. Others show him dancing and interacting with police officers and other members of the public. Collectively, the videos have gained more than 1.5 billion views online.

The backpack-wearing robot sensation has also made appearances on Polish TV and was even invited to the Polish parliament, where he met the country’s finance minister.

Based on the Unitree G1 model, which starts at $16,000 (£11,800), Edward is an advanced humanoid robot capable of performing complex motions and tasks including dancing, performing martial arts moves and playing the drums.

Edward is also equipped with a large language model that allows him to communicate with people in real time. He has an in-built scanner, which means he is fully aware of his surroundings.

Edward’s owners have said their aim is to familiarise Polish people with humanoid robots and encourage politicians to draft new laws attracting investment into the sector.

However, the android has also emerged as an AI influencer. Edward sports a diamond-set Rolex on his wrist as part of a sponsorship deal with the luxury watch brand.

Edward’s rise to fame comes as tech companies race to develop realistic humanoid robots. Hangzhou-based Unitree is gearing up for a stock market float in Shanghai that would value the company at around $2bn (£1.5bn).

Elon Musk’s Tesla is also developing an android called Optimus, with the company considering using its factory in Shanghai to begin mass production.

data.title: Former EY employee sues over sacking after Gaza genocide speech
data.description:
EY is being sued by a former employee who says she was sacked for making a speech about Gaza at her university graduation ceremony.

Cecilia Culver was hired by the “big four” accountancy firm in January 2025, but said she was unfairly dismissed just a few months later after making a controversial speech at George Washington University.

In the six-minute speech to an audience of 750 people in May, Ms Culver claimed George Washington University was profiting from a “genocide” in Gaza. She called on the institution to “divest from the apartheid state of Israel”.

“I am ashamed to know my tuition is being used to fund this genocide,” Ms Culver said in the speech. It provoked fierce backlash online, including from campaign group StopAntisemitism.

Ms Culver filed her claim against EY and five of its executives in a court in Washington, DC earlier this week, arguing that the firm’s decision to fire her breached her civil rights.

George Washington University was also listed as a defendant in the claim.

The lawsuit claimed Ms Culver was a “22-year-old economist at the beginning of a distinguished career, who gave a six-minute speech at her own graduation and who lost her job for it”.

It alleged EY fired Ms Culver to placate an “external Zionist pressure campaign” which was organised in response to her speech. It said the campaign led to more than 10,000 complaints about Ms Culver to the firm.

“When EY faced a choice between its legal obligation to protect its employee from discrimination and its desire to placate an organised campaign targeting her for the identity-based content of her expression, EY chose wrong,” the lawsuit said.

Ms Culver was hired by EY after completing an internship at the firm in the summer of 2024 at its offices in Washington, DC. During her stint at EY, Ms Culver was consistently given good feedback by her managers, the lawsuit said.

However, she was locked out of her workplace accounts less than 24 hours after making the speech and was placed on administrative leave, the lawsuit said.

EY told Ms Culver via email that the reason she was put on leave was “in connection with your speech at the George Washington University commencement”, according to the lawsuit.

Ms Culver was then terminated from EY four days later after she filed an internal discrimination complaint.

George Washington University separately issued a statement alleging she had been “dishonest” by delivering a speech that was different from the draft she had submitted.

The lawsuit argued Ms Culver should be awarded at least $5m (£3.7m) for “emotional distress and professional ostracisation” alongside a sum equivalent to 10 years’ worth of earnings.

EY declined to comment. Ms Culver and George Washington University were contacted for comment.

data.title: The robot revolution will turbo-charge growth. But not in Britain
data.description:
“Zelda the welder”, as she has become known at her sheet metal factory in Kent, was an expensive robot. All in, when she was installed five years ago, the bill was £250,000.

But her owner Contracts Engineering, which has since installed a second one, has made that back multiple times over. The firm’s profits before tax have quadrupled.

The robotic welders have meant that the company has been able to beat international competitors. It has expanded its business so much that it has hired two additional human welders to work alongside the machines.

Today, though, Contracts Engineering is in the minority. Its approach is precisely what most factories in the UK are not doing.

When it comes to automation in manufacturing, a toxic cocktail of dismal business investment, a significant skills gap, sky-high energy costs and a fear of taking risks mean the UK is flailing far behind the rest of the world.

Performance is plummeting relative to other countries and factories are under threat if they cannot catch up.

In South Korea, there are 1,012 industrial robots for every 10,000 manufacturing workers. In the UK, the figure is roughly a tenth of this at 111, according to industry body Make UK.

That is roughly half the EU average of 208 and about a quarter of Germany’s figure of 415.

British manufacturing workers have 47pc less machinery, tools and technology than those in similar economies, according to analysis by the Institute for Public Policy Research (IPPR).

This disadvantage is hitting the UK’s global standing. Between 2005 and 2023, the UK plunged from 10th place in the United Nations Industrial Development Organization competitive industrial performance index, which measures a nation’s ability to produce and export manufactured goods, to 19th.

The UK’s share of global manufacturing has slumped from 3.1pc in 2000 to 1.9pc in 2022 while its share of exports has plummeted from 3.7pc to 1.5pc.

The opportunity cost is huge. Make UK estimates that if British manufacturers caught up with the technological advancements of their global competitors, it would add 5pc to the UK economy by 2035 – equivalent to an extra £150bn.

Automating is increasingly essential if UK manufacturers want to compete with overseas companies, says Catherine Barratt, the chief executive of BAMUK, which owns Contracts Engineering.

“The speed of adoption overseas is really high. The UK really needs to adopt automation at a higher pace and at a greater scale.”

Britain is missing out on several core elements of modern manufacturing that are now standard in leading industrial economies, says Faye Skelton, the head of policy at Make UK.

In many countries overseas, integrated smart factory systems such as “manufacturing execution systems”, real time production monitoring and digitally-connected tools that link machines with inventory data and schedules are the norm.

They allow businesses to make their factories far more efficient and respond rapidly to any kind of disruption. In the UK, these systems are largely lacking.

Collaborative robots (“cobots”) that work alongside people, automated guided vehicles and AI-enabled quality inspections and predictive maintenance systems are widespread in countries such as Germany and Japan. UK uptake is patchy.

Where businesses do invest, it is often incremental, buying a single robot rather than a whole factory transformation.

“We haven’t really progressed in terms of digitising our factories,” says Skelton.

“Only one in 10 manufacturers can be described as fully digitised. When you look at our competitors, such as Germany, we are significantly behind.”

The UK has lost out on particular sectors because it has not acted quickly enough or developed the digital technology needed to manufacture a product competitively, says Skelton. Wind turbines are a key example.

“Even though our universities were at the forefront of working out how to develop wind energy, we didn’t have the suitable manufacturers with the same level of tech and robotics in the UK to make it,” says Skelton. Today, turbines are manufactured primarily in China and Denmark.

As the gap between the UK and its competitors gets wider, the problem could become existential.

Pranesh Narayanan, a senior research fellow at IPPR, warns: “At some point, companies will start saying they might just have to shut down their factories because there are all these more advanced competitors in other countries.”

One key issue is the fact that UK business investment is the second lowest in the G7, only marginally ahead of Canada.

UK firms make investments worth just 11.1pc of GDP, according to IPPR. In Japan, by contrast, business investment is equivalent to 18.2pc of GDP.

Significant political instability over the last decade in the UK, which saw Britain leave the EU and multiple changes in governments and industrial policies, has deterred foreign investors.

But another issue is that local banks are reluctant to lend the money for companies to invest in new technologies where there aren’t years of evidence of reliable returns.

“The appetite is low for investment from banks. We hear it a lot from SMEs and it does sound like more of a UK problem,” says Skelton.

PP Control and Automation, a factory near Birmingham that builds robotics such as mechanical milking machines as well as the electrical panels used by other manufacturers to make other types of automation equipment, is booming.

Pinaki Banerjee, the chief executive, expects the company’s revenues will surge by 50pc this year as demand rises for automation equipment in the UK.

Yet even this is not enough to persuade him to continue focusing on Britain.

PP Control and Automation has no plans to invest in the UK in the years ahead. Instead, it is going to spend £30m buying up factories in Italy, India and the US over the next three years.

The crux of the issue is the lack of investment in the UK, says Banerjee. If more factories were planning to ramp up automation, PP would be looking at buying more assets in the UK.

“We’re backed by private equity, I have to also keep an eye on how I can maximise my valuation to my shareholders,” Banerjee says. “If I just expand in the UK, I don’t think I’ll get a bump on my valuation.”

Another big problem is high energy prices. The UK faces some of the steepest electricity costs in Europe, meaning higher costs for automation.

Firms say a national skills shortage is also crucial. Nearly half of manufacturers (46pc) pointed to a lack of skills as their biggest hurdle to automating in a Make UK survey.

The Government’s migration advisory committee suggested in a 2022 report that there could be a link between high levels of immigration during the UK’s EU membership and low investment in automation because it incentivised recruitment over labour-saving capital investments.

In the years since, immigration soared to unprecedented levels. But industry experts say that the UK’s minimum wage system stopped this post-pandemic flow from making labour cheaper and it did not fill the major skills shortage in the manufacturing sector, which currently has around 50,000 unfilled long-term vacancies.

Whatever the hold ups, the outlook is only getting worse.

Today, businesses are grappling with high taxes following Rachel Reeves’s increase in employers’ National Insurance payments and April’s business rates rise. They are facing higher employment costs following Labour’s Employment Rights Act 2025 and soaring energy costs as the war in Iran drives up oil prices.

All of this means the need for companies to make efficiency gains is becoming even more crucial. But Skelton says it is also making it harder for many to find the money to do it: “What our members are telling us is that they don’t have the cash to invest.”

data.title: Britain faces food shortages because of Iran war
data.description:
Britain is facing a summer of food shortages because of the Iran war. A shortage of carbon dioxide could mean chicken, pork and fizzy drinks are in short supply if the conflict continues, secret government contingency planning for a “worst-case scenario” reveals.

Senior officials from departments including No 10, the Treasury, and the Ministry of Defence rehearsed scenarios examining the potential impact of the war on British industry in an event codenamed Exercise Turnstone.

The contingency plans, seen by The Times, were based on a scenario where the Strait of Hormuz has not reopened and no deal to end the war has been reached by mid-June. Farmers were warned of potential disruption and hospitality businesses, already squeezed by Labour’s tax rises, could be hit first by a shortage of CO2.

This is because CO2 is used in the process of slaughtering pigs and chickens and making drinks fizzy. It also improves the shelf life of foods such as salads, packaged meats and cakes. 

Officials also believe a collapse in CO2 supplies could endanger lives by making dry ice used to cool blood supplies, organs and vaccines scarcer. Under a reasonable worst-case scenario, CO2 supplies would fall to just 18 per cent of current levels, and factories would be ordered to stop other manufacturing to boost supplies by up to 100 per cent.

Emergency legislation to compel factories to co-operate has been discussed, with suppliers being compensated for stopping production of their main products.

The worst-case scenario involved an important UK plant suffering a mechanical error, while high gas costs led to lower production of ammonia and fertiliser, which produces CO2 as a by-product across Europe.

Critical food shortages are not expected, but shoppers could see fewer variety of goods on supermarket shelves.

Britain is expected to be hit hard by the economic fallout from the war.

On Wednesday, the International Monetary Fund (IMF) forecast that Britain is expected to suffer the biggest hit to growth of any G7 economy.

In a triple blow for Rachel Reeves, the IMF cut its UK growth forecasts for this year and next, warned of the highest inflation in the G7 and predicted one of the smallest increases in living standards in the world.

Higher fuel prices and energy bills are forecast to cost households £480 this year, according to the Resolution Foundation, with middle-class families being hit hardest.

On Thursday, Peter Kyle, the Business Secretary, confirmed planning for food shortages was under way and said Sir Keir Starmer was “leading from the front”.

Mr Kyle told Times Radio: “Of course we don’t comment on leaks. But this now is out there. But I want to reassure people that we have been acting since the very start of this conflict.”

He added: “We are looking at a variety of scenarios... When people read what is a leak and it is difficult for me because these leaks are very unhelpful, but when people read it they need to be reassured that we are doing this planning and we are doing this kind of scenario planning.

“I can tell you because I’m in these meetings that the Prime Minister has been there since the start of this war and he is going through and driving deep dives into resilience in lots of areas in our economy. You have a Government that is acting with creativity and boldness.”

Mr Kyle insisted food shortages were currently “not a concern” and that he would be “upfront” with the public if that were to change.

A government spokesman said: “We took decisive action last month to shore up the UK’s critical supplies of CO2 by temporarily restarting the Ensus bioethanol plant in Teesside and are continuing to work closely with business groups to tackle the impacts of events in the Middle East.

“Reasonable worst-case scenarios are a planning tool used by experts and are not a prediction of future events.”

data.title: Channel 4 programme chief quits amid shake-up at broadcaster
data.description:
Channel 4’s veteran head of programmes is to step down amid a leadership overhaul at the youth-focused broadcaster.

Ian Katz, the chief content officer, will leave in the autumn after nearly nine years in the role, making him the longest-serving programme boss in the channel’s history.

Mr Katz, a former BBC Newsnight editor and senior journalist at The Guardian, joined Channel 4 in 2018. He is credited with bringing shows such as Taskmaster to the channel, while he also oversaw the Russell T Davies drama It’s A Sin and documentaries including Russell Brand: In Plain Sight.

The 58-year-old was also responsible for driving Channel 4’s expansion on YouTube as the broadcaster tries to navigate a decline in traditional TV viewing and exodus of young audiences to streaming rivals.

However, Channel 4 has faced criticism in recent years that it has lost its edge and now relies too heavily on old formats such as Gogglebox and The Great British Bake Off. The channel was launched under Margaret Thatcher in 1982 with a remit to make challenging and provocative shows.

Mr Katz’s departure comes a month after Priya Dogra took over as chief executive of Channel 4. Ms Dogra was selected to replace Alex Mahon, who left last year after successfully fending off attempts to privatise the broadcaster.

The former Sky and Warner Bros executive will now seek to shape her new leadership team after a period of uncertainty for Channel 4, which lost both its chairman and chief executive last year.

Sir Ian Cheshire, the previous chairman, stood down at the end of his term last April and was appointed this month as the new chairman of regulator Ofcom.

Jonathan Allan, Channel 4’s former chief operating officer, who was in the running for the top job, also stepped down earlier this year.

Ms Dogra said: “Ian has been an outstanding creative leader for Channel 4 over nearly nine years – the channel’s longest-serving head of programming.

“He has overseen an era of creative renewal, delivering bold and distinctive public service programming with intellectual rigour, good humour and an unmistakably Channel 4 glint in the eye.”

The leadership shake-up comes as Channel 4 seeks to carve out its role in the streaming era. The broadcaster is spending less on making programmes as it grapples with declining advertising revenues from its traditional TV business.

Channel 4 has been striking closer partnerships with its rivals as public service broadcasters seek to build scale to fight back against the likes of Netflix. But it is also contending with a wider reshaping of the TV landscape, with Sky locked in talks to buy ITV’s broadcasting business.

Mr Katz said: “It’s been a privilege beyond words to lead Channel 4’s talented and passionate commissioning team through such a transformative period.

“I’m incredibly proud of what we’ve achieved, from pioneering the evolution of a commercial public service broadcaster into a digital streaming business to backing groundbreaking programmes and talent that have brought a bit of joy to audiences’ lives.

“Above all, I’m proud that Channel 4 has continued to be as disruptive and impertinent as ever, asking the questions and covering the stories that other broadcasters and streamers won’t.”

Mr Katz will remain in his role until October to commission and oversee the channel’s slate of programming for 2026.

data.title: Britain has record taxes yet Labour’s mismanagement has left us broke
data.description:
The Governor of the Bank of England has warned that the world is facing a “very big energy shock” that will push up prices.

A statement of the bleeding obvious, as Basil Fawlty might say? Perhaps, but it serves to highlight just how insufficient the response has been to the economic fallout from Donald Trump’s misadventures in the Middle East.

The Government is so far out of its depth that armbands and a rubber ring may have to become compulsory items at future Cabinet meetings.

The latest iteration of the energy support scheme for industry is merely further evidence that ministers either still don’t get it or aren’t willing to provide meaningful support.

The decision to rethink the British Industrial Competitiveness Scheme (BICS), which provides a 25pc cut in bills for so-called energy-intensive users, was supposed to demonstrate that someone in Government finally had grasped the severity of the situation facing manufacturers.

Instead, the opposite is true. Sure, expanding the breadth of it to include over 10,000 factories rather than the 7,000 first proposed is meaningful, but not if support isn’t brought forward. For reasons no one in power has been able to provide a compelling explanation for, those that qualify for relief still won’t be able to access it until April 2027.

The mind truly boggles. Without real help now, Britain risks losing some of the most critical parts of the supply chain forever, undermining our already fragile national sovereignty further and making us even more reliant on volatile foreign suppliers for materials that underpin scores of vital products.

The American owner of the UK’s last producer of a critical chemical called aniline has warned that it would close the site in Wilton, Teesside, if energy prices remained at their current levels for the next three months.

It means the amended BICS scheme, which Peter Kyle wasted little time in championing on national radio on Thursday, won’t make the slightest jot of difference to the fate of at least one major factory. The future of many more will be hanging in the balance.

The Business Secretary had the audacity to claim the changes to BICS showed Labour was “a government of action” that would “always do what’s needed” in times of global instability. Twaddle.

The only sensible conclusion is that Labour’s woeful mismanagement of the economy, under which welfare is being repeatedly prioritised over everything else including even our national security, has rendered Britain so broke that it could not afford to respond in times of genuine crisis.

This, of course, shouldn’t be the case. As the International Monetary Fund (IMF) pointed out this week, Rachel Reeves has raised taxes at the fastest pace in the world.

The Chancellor’s repeated raids on households and businesses would push the UK’s tax burden to a peacetime high of 42pc of GDP by the start of the next decade, the IMF’s figures showed. This equates to an extra £130bn of taxes since Labour took power, or a staggering £4,500 more per household.

But because of this Government’s obsession with welfare and the NHS, there is no money for energy or defence – or much else, for that matter. As Anna Leach, the chief economist at the Institute of Directors, points out, the UK is “uniquely vulnerable” to events in the Middle East because of “financing conditions, high initial starting point for inflation, uncompetitive energy costs and low fiscal space”.

It explains why the IMF expects growth in Britain this year to be slower, and inflation higher than any other major advanced economy.

The problem is spending, or rather where that spending is being directed. The intervention of Lord Robertson – a Labour grandee, former secretary-general of Nato, as well as the author of the Government’s recent strategic defence review – is telling.

Perhaps it will prove to be decisive in ensuring that some of the ever-expanding welfare budget is redirected to other departments. When serious figures like Robertson are starting to openly question the benefits bill, it is surely time for a complete rethink of where resources are being channelled. As he points out, Britain’s welfare budget is now five times the amount we spend on defence at a time when national security has never been more vital.

Perhaps naive ministers have allowed the latest GDP figures to fool them into thinking the economy could weather the incoming storm.

No doubt February’s numbers showing a 0.5pc rise in GDP are a pleasant surprise, but any growth had almost certainly already been extinguished by events in the Middle East – Kyle himself acknowledged this.

It is why the failure to provide meaningful and immediate support for heavy industry makes no sense. Left to fend for themselves in the face of even higher energy and raw material costs than they were already grappling with thanks to the war in Ukraine, and endless government-imposed green levies, manufacturers will be forced to curtail investment, cut back on hiring, and possibly even reduce output. Under those circumstances, growth becomes little more than an aspiration.

Yet, at the same time, ministers are drawing up plans to invoke emergency legislation to force many of those very same factories to double CO2 production to avoid shortages that will ripple through the economy. Forget a fizzy drinks crisis, a CO2 scarcity could quickly become a threat to life by affecting supplies of vaccines, blood and vital medicines.

Given this, you would think ministers would be bending over backwards to provide help. Instead, the best they could come up with was a rehashed scheme that still wouldn’t be available for another year – proof that the disconnect at the top has reached epic new proportions.

On Thursday, Sir Keir Starmer told tech bosses hauled into No 10 to discuss child safety: “The status quo simply cannot be allowed to stand.” With local elections around the corner, the same could be said of Labour’s time in power.

data.title: Founder of Chinese property giant pleads guilty to fraud
data.description:
The founder of the failed Chinese property giant Evergrande is facing the threat of life in prison after pleading guilty to fraud and bribery.

Hui Ka Yan, also known as Xu Jiayin and once one of China’s richest men with a fortune of $45bn (£36bn), made the plea during a court hearing in Shenzhen earlier this week.

It marks a significant fall from grace for the businessman, who turned Evergrande into China’s biggest property developer before it collapsed under the weight of huge debts in 2021.

Evergrande’s unravelling has led to a raft of claims against Hui, including embezzlement of assets and illegal fundraising. The offences carry a maximum penalty of life imprisonment and confiscation of property.

The court in Shenzhen said it would announce its verdict on the case at a later date, claiming on Tuesday that Hui had “pleaded guilty and expressed remorse” during proceedings.

His trial took place after he was detained in China in Sept 2023 on suspicion of committing crimes. Hui, a former steelworker, has not been seen publicly since.

At its peak, Evergrande had a stock market valuation of more than £37bn, working on around 1,300 projects in 280 cities across China.

However, large debts crippled the company in 2021, sending the company’s value plummeting and sparking China’s property downturn, which has weighed on the economy for years.

The China Securities Regulatory Commission found in 2024 that Evergrande had inflated earnings and committed securities fraud.

The commission found that revenues were overstated by more than 560bn yuan (£60.6bn) by recognising sales in advance.

It fined Hui 47m yuan (£5.1m) and handed him a lifetime ban from participating in China’s stock markets.

The indebted property giant was fined 4.18bn yuan (£451.4m) before being ordered to liquidate by a Hong Kong court in 2024. The company was removed from the Hong Kong stock exchange last year.

The collapse of the company has fuelled a property crisis across China, as home sales slowed and construction companies scaled back developments.

Beijing has sought to prop up the property market by providing incentives to prospective home buyers.

However, the economy has since been dealt a fresh blow by the Iran war, straining China’s energy supplies and piling pressure on the country’s manufacturing sector.
