data.title: West Midlands Police makes £1m by ‘harassing’ lorry drivers
data.description:
West Midlands Police has come under fire for making more than £1m by “harassing” lorry drivers and forcing them to pay for escorts.

The force has increased its income from road haulage companies 70-fold over the past five years from £15,000 to more than £1m, according to data obtained by the Construction Plant-hire Association (CPA).

Police have powers to require an escort for unusually large or heavy lorries which pass through their area while making deliveries, including those made to HS2 sites.

Lord Attlee, who went undercover to investigate the force, compared the tactics used by West Midlands Police to “ones that you would expect to see used by a corrupt police force in a slowly developing country”.

A qualified lorry driver himself from his days in the Royal Electrical and Mechanical Engineers, the Tory peer travelled in the cab of a lorry on a route known to be targeted regularly by West Midlands Police while posing as a driving assessor from the haulage firm.

He told Parliament that he witnessed officers “harassing” drivers by “deliberately misquoting or misapplying regulations, and prohibiting vehicle movements when there is no power to do so”.

“Such excessive and overzealous checks are interfering with the smooth running of the heavy haulage industry, which is racking up significant extra costs in all the wasted time spent responding to these demands,” he added.

If officers decide the load needs to be escorted by them, there is nothing to prevent them refusing permission until the lorry company agrees to pay the force sums measured in thousands of pounds.

Lord Attlee also told The Telegraph that he believed West Midlands Police had been charging haulage companies its standard six-hour overtime rate several times in one day.

This means the same group of police officers may have billed those companies several times over for the same amount of work, he said.

‘Disruption and stress across the industry’

The boss of one CPA member company, who spoke on condition of anonymity for fear of police retaliation, said: “Operators are also being pushed to request police escorts when they are not needed. If an escort is requested, the cost is around £2,500 just to travel through the West Midlands area.

“This is creating a huge administrative burden and will cause serious disruption and stress across the industry,” added the executive, who explained that some deliveries were only worth £2,000 – meaning police demands for money more than doubled the price paid by the customer.

Steven Mulholland, chief executive of the CPA, said: “This isn’t about safety – it’s about misinterpretation and incentives that are putting HS2 and major infrastructure projects at risk.

“You simply cannot deliver HS2 or major rail and construction projects if the machinery needed to build them is held up by regional policy drift.

“We need an urgent return to a single, nationally consistent interpretation of the rules, so compliant movements are treated the same way in every part of the country.”

A West Midlands Police spokesman said that the force tries to “foster positive relationships with haulage operators”, doing so “without unnecessary penalties”.

“Demand for abnormal loads across the region is increasing, fuelled by a thriving industry and major infrastructure projects such as HS2 and developments around M42 Junction 5A,” the spokesman said.

“Last year, we assessed 171,565 notifications. Of these, 13,354 notifications were reviewed as they potentially required police support due to the route, size or nature of the load.

“A total of 706 were escorted by our officers, which equates to 0.41 per cent of movement notifications.

“We only ever seek details that are relevant and outlined in legislation, and vehicle movements are only ever prohibited when they are assessed as unlawful.”

data.title: How Sadiq Khan made London into a taxi driver’s worst nightmare
data.description:
It should be a straightforward journey that needn’t take too long – Liverpool Street to London Bridge station, two areas heavily populated with white-collar workers heading from the commuter belts into the capital for their careers.

However, those opting to drive the short one-and-a-half miles find themselves forced to go a more roundabout way – through Whitechapel, in order to avoid the bus gate at Bishopsgate, which prevents motorists (yet allows buses and bikes) from accessing the road.

So, instead of taking around five minutes in the car, it takes between 20 and 30 minutes. Realistically, it would be quicker for drivers to swap four wheels for two legs.

Bishopsgate is just one of the many roads in the capital that have become increasingly unwelcoming to motorists, with a recent survey conducted by TomTom, the satnav company, finding that London is the slowest-driving capital city in the world.

It takes an average of three minutes and 38 seconds to travel one measly kilometre (0.6 miles) in the centre of the city. The analysis also found drivers lost an average of 141 hours stuck in rush hour traffic in 2025.

This glacial pace is annoying when you are trying to get somewhere in a hurry, to attend an important meeting, perhaps, or to make it to the theatre before curtain call. However, for the black cab drivers of London, increasingly congested, slow and difficult-to-navigate roads are leading to a significant loss of earnings, and even putting their livelihoods – and the sight of the London icon – at risk of extinction.

As of late 2025, the number of licensed drivers had fallen to around 14,800, down from more than 22,500 a decade earlier.

For Steve Cox, who became a black cab driver in 2022, navigating the streets of London has become increasingly difficult. He cites low-traffic neighbourhoods (LTNs) – areas where roads are closed to traffic to allow cyclists and pedestrians to take priority – as one of the main contributors to the capital’s slow roads.

“What used to be a 15-minute journey can now take you up to an hour,” the 54-year-old explains. “Tottenham Court Road is severely restricted for motorists between 8am and 7pm, which are some of your peak hours as a black cab.

“If you want to go from one side of Tottenham Court Road to the other to drop a passenger, we have to do two different squares and go round several back roads. It’s just ridiculous.

“It’s another 15 to 20 minutes on the journey time, and probably another £15-£20 for the customer. I’m embarrassed to charge them. It’s unfair and leads to complaints that aren’t down to me – and results in fewer people trying to flag us down.”

While LTNs were initially implemented in London in the 1970s (although under a different name) to filter traffic in the Hackney area, they were implemented en masse in March 2020 under Mayor of London Sir Sadiq Khan’s plans to improve road safety, decrease air and noise pollution, and encourage “active travel” such as walking and cycling.

Now, it is thought four per cent of London’s nine-million-strong population lives in an LTN.

These have made navigating around the capital increasingly problematic; approximately 95 per cent of black cab drivers report that LTNs and poor road conditions make picking up and dropping off passengers difficult.

Cabbies also cite London’s new speed restrictions as a reason why the capital often remains at a standstill. According to Transport for London (TfL), more than half of London’s roads now have a 20mph limit. This includes all roads within London’s Congestion Charge zone, as well as major roads, such as the A107 corridor and the White Hart Roundabout.

While this was implemented to try and cut deaths and serious injuries, City Hall was previously told this change was “devastating to the taxi industry” by Steve McNamara, general secretary of the Licensed Taxi Drivers Association in 2024.

“I understand the need for 20mph zones around schools,” says Frank O’Beirne, who has been a black cab driver for 14 years. “However, places like Park Lane, which have been reduced from three lanes of traffic to just one in order for there to be a bus and cycling lane, is just madness – especially now we’re stuck only being able to go 20mph there.”

The 50-year-old adds: “While our meters do go up in traffic, it’s only incremental – we need to be moving to actually have decent fares. Customers get so fed up in traffic they just jump out.”

Sadiq Khan’s green crusade for London has reached a nadir with the highly criticised and controversial ultra-low emission zone (Ulez). Since 2018, all newly-licenced black cabs need to be “zero-emission capable”, while the maximum operating age for older, more polluting diesel taxis has been reduced from 15 years to 12.

Cox says some older cabbies have been forced into an early retirement. “I know men in their 60s who have had their cab for 10 years and just didn’t see the point of carrying on investing so much money in a new car, and have gone and got jobs in Tesco instead.

“That and the stress of trying to earn enough money to keep you afloat is enough to push someone into another job. I don’t blame people for saying: ‘I don’t need this anymore.’”

It’s of little surprise, then, that a quarter of all black cabs on the roads before the pandemic are no longer in operation. Renting a traditional black cab, or Hackney carriage, is not cheap. Cox pays £360 a week to rent his vehicle, while others pay £400 and beyond. That doesn’t include the additional outgoings for cabbies, such as the mandatory two MOTs a year; while cab drivers don’t pay this, it does result in a loss of earnings as you can’t work while your vehicle is being serviced.

Cox says he needs to earn more than £3,000 a month just to break even on the investments he’s made to become a black cab driver. “It’s a stressful trade,” he says. “It’s made even more stressful by sudden roadworks, or ongoing protests by groups such as Palestine Action blocking roads. I can lose up to £300 a day in earnings when protests take place.”

Those still working as black cab drivers are now finding alternative methods to keep themselves afloat. O’Beirne works as a tour guide during slow periods. “I prefer doing out-of-town stuff now, because London just drives me mad,” he says. “Places like Tottenham Court Road, Mayfair, the City are just no-go areas. It’s a nightmare to navigate.

“Corporate gigs tend to pay better – I get a lot of American customers, who tend to be big tippers. But doing these gigs means you give up some freedom of setting your own hours, which is partly the beauty of doing this job.”

So, what could help London’s black cab drivers stay on the roads? For Cox, cutting, or at least reducing, some of the restrictions of LTNs on Hackney carriages would help with picking up and dropping off customers.

“If we really want to ease congestion, we could put a cap on private-hire drivers,” he adds. “They can operate without any restriction on the size of their fleet. I’ve heard of private-hire drivers getting licensed in Wolverhampton and then driving down here to get more work.”

Currently, private-hire vehicles vastly outstrip black cabs; TfL estimates more than 106,000 of these cabs are on the road.

Meanwhile, Steve Kenton, who has been a cabbie for 35 years, believes City Hall should listen more to black cab drivers. “We have some wonderful and intelligent people who choose to become black cab drivers,” he says. “We spend three to four years perfecting the Knowledge of London so we know the proper routes around the city. We attract individuals from every demographic. Our views and concerns deserve to be treated with more respect.”

“Fundamentally, if our wheels aren’t moving, we aren’t making money,” O’Beirne agrees. “We’re the only fully accessible transport network in London. Not everyone can ride a bike.”

data.title: Sainsbury’s threatens to prosecute shoplifters caught swapping eggs
data.description:
Sainsbury’s has threatened shoplifters with police action if they are caught swapping premium eggs into cheaper boxes.

The supermarket giant has placed signs in stores telling customers that egg-swapping is “regarded as theft”, claiming that any footage of shoplifting will be shared with police.

In a picture posted on social media by Steve Dresser, a retail analyst, Sainsbury’s appeared to have put the signs under Burford Brown eggs, which are 75pc more expensive than the supermarket’s standard own-brand eggs.

A six-pack of Burford Browns eggs sells for £3.20 at Sainsbury’s, compared with £1.80 for its standard own-brand eggs.

Sainsbury’s told customers: “Footage of shoplifting will be passed to the police. We prosecute all shoplifters.”

The warning comes after shoppers complained they were returning home from supermarkets to find their expensive boxes of eggs had been filled with cheaper alternatives.

Videos emerged on social media earlier this year in which customers said they had been victims of egg theft.

Some claimed they had also realised “large” eggs had been swapped for “medium” ones after arriving home from the supermarkets.

Anita Wong, a shopper who sounded the alarm about egg-swapping in February, said she had been told by sales assistants that this was “not uncommon”.

Egg prices have risen from £2.48 for a dozen in early 2022 compared with £3.31 today, according to the Office for National Statistics, with the sector hit by higher costs.

However, supermarkets have suggested that cost of living pressures are not the biggest driver of higher shoplifting levels, claiming that organised criminals are hitting their stores periodically.

The Telegraph revealed last weekend that Asda was considering placing products such as razors and perfume in vending machines, so thieves could not target such products.

Some bosses claim retailers are not receiving enough support from police. Writing in The Telegraph earlier this month, Thinus Keeve, Marks & Spencer’s retail director, urged the Mayor of London to “prioritise effective policing”.

He said attacks were becoming “more brazen, more organised and more aggressive” across Britain’s high streets every day.

A Sainsbury’s spokesman said: “We have a range of security measures in our stores and if our managers notice specific items are becoming a target for theft, they may take additional measures in response.”

data.title: Thames Water to escape new penalties until 2030 under rescue deal
data.description:
Thames Water is set to escape new fines until 2030 under a deal agreed between creditors and the industry watchdog to save the stricken company.

Ofwat, which regulates the sector, is planning to shield the struggling utility from financial penalties linked to poor performance in return for a commitment from creditors to invest cash in the business for the next four years.

The controversial plan would carve out Thames from a penalty scheme run by Ofwat which levies fines if water companies fall behind on key metrics such as leaks and sewer flooding.

Thames is struggling under a £20bn debt pile and will run out of money again in October without the new creditor agreement.

However, the penalties deal has angered campaigners, who say the watchdog is failing in its duty to hold water companies to account.

Feargal Sharkey, the clean water campaigner, accused Ofwat of shirking its responsibility to regulate the industry.

“Ofwat has proved yet again it is incapable, unwilling to and simply does not want to properly regulate the greed, the abuse of customers and the financial engineering that is destroying this industry and the environment,” he said.

The plan, which has been put forward by creditors including US hedge funds Elliott Management and Silver Point, first went to Ofwat for approval in June 2025.

The creditors, known as London & Valley Water, provided the utility with £3bn in emergency funding last year.

As part of the deal, some creditors like Elliott would accept a 30pc write-down in their debt while more junior creditors would lose all their money.

The creditors defended the agreement and said it would mean “clear accountability” if things went wrong.

A spokesman said: “All outstanding fines will be paid. Regulators will have enhanced transparency and Thames Water will have clear accountability for reducing pollution and improving environmental outcomes against stretching performance targets.”

Utilities such as Thames Water have to pay penalties when they fail to meet performance targets set by Ofwat for the year.

Under the new proposals, first reported by The Financial Times, these penalties would be replaced with a formal commitment to invest in Thames Water.

This would allow Ofwat to continue with investigations and penalties but any cash raised from them would be spent on fixing issues with the business rather than returned to the Treasury.

The utility could still face separate fines from other regulators like the Environment Agency and would still have to reckon with possible legal cases. All outstanding fines would also have to be paid.

The Government is reportedly keen to keep Thames Water out of temporary nationalisation but the utility has only been kept afloat so far by the £3bn emergency loan, which is expected to cost the utility an extra £800m in interest payments.

Thames Water said that if Ofwat agreed a deal, creditors had offered the company an additional £6.6bn of debt at a lower rate of interest than the emergency loan, as well as £3.35bn of equity.

A spokesman for Thames Water said: “Thames Water remains focused on securing a market-led solution which delivers improvements for customers and the environment as soon as practicable, whilst making progress with our operational and financial turnaround plan.”

An Ofwat spokesperson said: “We continue to engage with London & Valley Water, and are reviewing their plans carefully to assess whether they deliver a turnaround in the company’s operational performance and strengthen its financial resilience to the benefit of customers and the environment.”

data.title: British potteries are in trouble. Here are the everyday ceramics to buy and use
data.description:
News of Denby Pottery calling in the administrators has brought the challenges faced by the British ceramics industry into sharp focus. Rising costs surrounding employment and energy have left the 217-year-old business, which produces all of its collections from its site in Derbyshire, operating at a loss. It is looking for a new buyer and has reportedly cut its 500-plus workforce by 80.

Denby isn’t the only one feeling the pinch. Mike Raybould, the chief executive of Portmeirion Group, says: “Energy remains one of the most significant pressures on our industry. Ceramics production is inherently energy-intensive, so sustained increases in energy prices have a direct impact on the cost of manufacturing in the UK.” Portmeirion, which was founded in the late 1960s, produces 100,000 kiln-fired pieces a week from its factory in Stoke-on-Trent and is stocked in around 60 countries. “The ‘made in the UK’ backstamp carries a huge amount of value in the global market,” adds Raybould.

Public support for Denby has been mobilised with a #SaveDenby campaign, but it will take more than just a hashtag to save the business. Raybould notes that the ceramics sector has called on the Government to be eligible for energy support schemes such as the British Industry Supercharger, which benefits steel, metal and chemical producers.

Then there is the issue around skills and recruitment. Emma Bridgewater, which also produces its ranges in The Potteries, has an ageing workforce. “So many of our highly skilled makers are coming up for retirement. The industry hasn’t had an entry programme or apprenticeship programme since the 1980s,” says a spokesperson. That will change next month, when six new recruits join the inaugural Craft Apprenticeship Scheme, which covers every element of the ceramic manufacturing process.

Emma Bridgewater, which was founded in 1986, has amassed a 100,000-strong base of ardent fans. “All of our pottery is hand-signed, and some customers will collect a particular decorator’s work and come to meet them at the factory, so there’s a real connection,” adds the spokesman. “By investing in buying British pottery to eat your breakfast off, you’re supporting an industry-wide regeneration of skills and expertise in giving young people in a city an opportunity to develop a career. It really reaches deep.”

On that note, here are some of the best buys to elevate the everyday in the kitchen, all proudly made in the UK.

data.title: Bank to quit Britain in protest against ‘unfair’ £750m car finance bill
data.description:
A bank has said it will quit the UK in protest after an “unfair” scheme that will see its car finance compensation payouts soar to £750m.

FirstRand, which owns roughly a tenth of the UK car finance sector through MotoNovo, has vowed to abandon its UK operations as result of the City watchdog’s mass redress scheme for mis-sold car loans.

In a notice to shareholders, the South African bank said it would leave its motor finance operations in Britain because it no longer has the “risk appetite” for the UK.

The Financial Conduct Authority (FCA) finalised details of the scheme at the end of March, which FirstRand said were “disproportionate and unfair”.

The terms of the scheme mean FirstRand now expects its compensation bill to hit £750m, up from its previous estimate of £510m.

The firm’s announcement shows how the fallout from the car finance scandal is rocking the sector as millions of borrowers prepare to claw back compensation.

Around 12 million drivers will be entitled for an average payout of £829 under the FCA’s scheme, which will cost the car finance industry as much as £9.1bn.

This compensation is for customers who were sold car finance agreements in which dealers received a fee from lenders based on the interest rate charged on the loans.

The FCA, which banned these agreements in 2021, said these fees were rarely disclosed to customers and provided an incentive for dealers to charge higher interest rates than necessary.

Lenders including FirstRand had lobbied heavily for the compensation scheme to be watered down. The FCA did make some changes to the scheme following feedback from industry, but the South African group said that this was outweighed by other “problematic” amendments.

FirstRand’s higher payout estimate came as a result of the watchdog’s decision to broaden the scope of what it considered to be an “unfair” loan and changes to the amount of interest on unfair loans that a company would have to pay back to customers.

Back in October, the FCA had said it expected average payouts to total a lower sum of £700.

The South African bank said that it reserved its legal rights to challenge the scheme and that it believed that the FCA’s terms were “deeply flawed” and diverged “significantly and inappropriately” from a previous Supreme Court ruling.

The total cost for FirstRand would far exceed the £275m in profits that it has made on its motor finance activities in the UK over the past decade.

An FCA spokesman said: “Our scheme provides certainty and is the most cost efficient and orderly way to deal with liabilities that exist, no matter what. We welcome lenders, First Rand included, making financial provisions so they can deliver it.

“The market remains resilient – a record £41bn was lent on motor finance in 2025 and February’s new car sales were the highest in 22 years. With reforms now in place, we do not see further major redress events on the horizon.”

data.title: South East Water boss waives bonus after ‘getting it wrong’ on supply crisis
data.description:
The boss of South East Water has admitted that he “got it wrong” during outages in Tunbridge Wells as he gave up his bonus over the disruption.

Dave Hinton, the chief executive of the embattled utility company, said he had made mistakes during the incident, which left thousands of homes without water for weeks. It resulted in schools in Kent closing and elderly residents queuing for two hours for bottled water.

Mr Hinton said he had “got it wrong” over how he dealt with the public. Chris Train, the company’s chairman, confirmed that Mr Hinton would not be taking his £115,000 bonus for this year.

He received a £400,000 basic salary and a £115,000 bonus last year, as well as being paid a further £50,000 for working overtime.

The chief executive was notably absent during the crisis, which began in November 2025. About 24,000 homes and businesses across the South East lost drinking water for two weeks.

Mr Hinton refused to speak to the media during the outages and attacked Mike Martin, a local MP, for having “politicised the issue”.

‘We accept that we failed’

However, appearing before MPs on the Environment Committee on Tuesday, Mr Hinton admitted the company’s response had been “disappointing” and said mistakes were made in how quickly he communicated with the public.

He said: “That is very much a lesson that we have learnt for the playbook of how we handle future events.”

Mr Hinton said South East Water “should have reacted on those earlier warnings quicker, and our problem-solving around it when we got into that situation was too slow and unstructured”.

Mr Train, the non-executive chairman of the utility company, said: “We accept that we failed in our primary duty and we could have done better.”

The admission follows an earlier hearing when Mr Hinton had sought to defend himself before the Environment Committee. During the first appearance, he told MPs that the outage, which was caused by the failure of a water treatment plant, was not foreseeable.

He rated himself eight out of 10 for his response, claiming that “lifestyle changes”, including more people working from home, were partly to blame for the incident.

‘Cat with nine live’

The earlier hearing came the same day as fresh disruption hit the Tunbridge Wells area. On Jan 7, the taps ran dry again in Tunbridge Wells because of thawing pipes cracking. It affected 25,000 homes in the area.

On Tuesday, Mr Train would not be drawn on whether there would be any failure sufficient that he would ask Mr Hinton to step down.

Henry Tufnell, an MP who sits on the Environment Committee, told The Telegraph: “Hinton’s capacity to survive is remarkable. He’s like a cat with nine lives.”

Mr Tufnell added: “We used to pride ourselves on being able to turn on the tap [to have] high-quality drinking water. To have lost that and to have lost that trust and confidence is just shocking and quite damning.”

data.title: Reeves hands Rolls-Royce £600m to build mini-nukes in Britain
data.description:
Rolls-Royce has been handed a £600m loan from Rachel Reeves to help accelerate the development of Britain’s first mini-nuclear power plant.

The Chancellor’s National Wealth Fund confirmed details of the borrowing facility on Monday, which will be used to build three small modular reactors (SMRs) at Wylfa on the Welsh island of Anglesey.

The manufacturing giant was selected as the Government’s preferred partner for the project last year.

However, design work will now begin after Rolls-Royce struck an agreement with Great British Energy – Nuclear (GBEN), the quango set up to manage the mini-nuclear programme.

GBEN said the contract would unlock the next stage of the project, which aims to help commercialise nascent SMR technology.

It will provide Rolls-Royce with funds to complete detailed design work, including regulatory applications and planning approvals.

The £600m loan is in addition to £2.6bn that Ms Reeves allocated to the scheme during last year’s spending review.

Construction of the power plant itself is not expected to begin until the early 2030s, with the facility set to come online in the mid-2030s. But GBEN is understood to be carrying out ground preparation works now.

The scheme is eventually expected to create 3,000 local jobs in Wylfa, Anglesey, as well as another 5,000 supply chain jobs across the country.

Ms Reeves said: “This investment, along with vital financing from the National Wealth Fund, will strengthen our energy security, create skilled jobs and help to build a new generation of homegrown nuclear technology that will power our economy for decades to come.”

Ed Miliband, the Energy Secretary, added: “At a time of global instability, this is a major milestone for Britain’s energy security.

“We are backing a British company to deliver our first small modular reactors – creating a generation of good jobs, driving growth and providing clean, home-grown power for decades to come.”

GBEN said the contract with Rolls-Royce stipulates that the company must hit various “milestones”, although it did not immediately clarify what those were.

The three mini reactors are expected to have a combined capacity of about 1.4 gigawatts, enough to power three million homes for more than 60 years.

SMRs will be constructed from “modules” built in factories and then transported to sites for assembly.

The idea is meant to ensure the plants can be produced efficiently at scale and more quickly than larger nuclear projects.

In the longer run, it is hoped their lower price tags will also make them more attractive to private investors, who have long viewed bigger plants as too expensive and risky to back without government support.

Major technology companies, including Google, Amazon and Facebook-owner Meta, are investing in potential SMR technologies in the hope they could eventually be used to supply power-hungry data centres needed to develop AI.

Rolls-Royce is also building six SMRs for the Czech government.

Simon Bowen, the chairman of GBEN, said: “This is an immense moment for the UK nuclear programme, our organisation and the industry as a whole.

“Today represents a true recognition of the efforts made across GBEN, Rolls-Royce SMR and Government to get to this point, and I’m hugely proud of the team for reaching this critical milestone.”

Chris Cholerton, the chief executive of Rolls-Royce SMR, added: “This contract unlocks the delivery of our first three units and brings certainty to the UK SMR programme.”

data.title: British banks to be given access to AI ‘too dangerous to release’
data.description:
British banks will be given access to the most advanced artificial intelligence hacking bot from Anthropic after it was deemed too dangerous to release to the public.

The Silicon Valley AI lab confirmed UK financial institutions would be added to its Project Glasswing programme, through which companies are given early access to Anthropic’s Claude Mythos AI tool to stress-test their cyber defences.

Pip White, Anthropic’s UK chief, said the scheme would expand to British businesses “in the very near term, in the next week”.

She told Bloomberg Television: “As you would expect, the engagement that I have had from UK CEOs in the last week has been significant.”

A source familiar with the programme said they expected several UK businesses to sign up to the second phase of the project.

Banks and technology companies have been racing to shore up their cybersecurity systems after Anthropic revealed the capabilities of its Mythos bot. The AI software could autonomously find previously unknown bugs in software that could expose companies to devastating cyberattacks.

Anthropic said its AI tool had found flaws in every major technology operating system and internet browser faster than would be possible for a human hacker.

The $380bn (£281bn) company has delayed the launch of the tool to the public, because of its powerful cyber capabilities. It has so far released Mythos to a select group of tech giants, including Apple and Microsoft, as well as US banks such as JP Morgan, so that they can strengthen their security.

The Telegraph revealed last week that the Bank of England was preparing to warn City chiefs about the powerful new AI technology in upcoming security briefings.

The UK’s AI Security Institute, the Government’s testing lab, confirmed this week it had trialled the new technology, warning it was “a step up over previous frontier models”. The AI can link together cyberattacks that require multiple steps, similar to a human hacker, in a job that would typically take professionals “days of work”.

Ms White said Anthropic had delayed the release of Claude Mythos and put its “own limitations around this product because we know how powerful it can be”.

“If we have to slow down the release for the safe and responsible development of AI and that has a commercial impact, we will do that.”

The disclosure came as Anthropic confirmed it would be reinforcing its UK presence, opening a larger London office early next year with space for 800 people.

Anthropic has about 200 staff in London. The AI lab recently raised $30bn in new funding to expand and has been linked with an initial public offering as soon as this year.

The London expansion was revealed as Anthropic grappled with a rift with the White House. The US department of defense labelled Anthropic a “supply chain risk” after it refused to approve its technology for use in fully autonomous weapons.

Anthropic has sued the White House over the blacklisting, which would force US government departments to stop using its AI tools. The case is ongoing. UK government officials have attempted to woo Anthropic to expand its London presence amid the split with Donald Trump’s administration.

Ms White said: “London is already one of our most important research and commercial hubs outside the US, and our expansion in the knowledge quarter gives us the room to grow into.”

Separately, the Government confirmed on Thursday that a new £500m taxpayer-backed AI fund has made its first investment in a start-up that is developing powerful semiconductor technology. Callosum is seeking to compete with tech giant Nvidia.

The Sovereign AI Unit, chaired by technology investor James Wise, was launched at an event on Thursday evening. The fund will back early-stage British AI businesses to accelerate their growth.

Kanishka Narayan, the AI minister, told The Telegraph that the investments would help to build “deep relationships” with AI founders “to get companies to stay here over time” rather than relocate to Silicon Valley.

Mr Narayan said the Government was seeking to be a “much more active, strategic state” by becoming directly involved in early-stage start-ups. He said this would mean “rolling our sleeves up, helping these companies become a massive set of British AI successes”.

data.title: Labour under fire for leaving builders in the dark about rewilding rules
data.description:
Projects such as railways, power stations and reservoirs risk delays because Labour has kept industry in the dark about new “rewilding” rules about to come into force.

Ministers have failed to provide details of how a new system requiring major developers to offset the damage they do to nature will work.

From next month, developers of large infrastructure projects will be required to make up for any nature they destroy by increasing the quality of natural habitats elsewhere, such as woodlands, by at least 10pc. The rules amount to a legal requirement for rewilding habitat for wildlife.

The rules are scheduled to come into force within weeks. However, builders and industry representatives say they have been left in the dark about how it will work.

Julia Baker, of Mott MacDonald, an engineering consultancy, said: “A mandatory policy for biodiversity net gain (BNG) will create a level playing field, which is welcomed. But the only thing we know is that it will be live in May, and it’s April.

“We have such long waiting times for development consent orders (DCOs) – you need to turn the wheel at least a year before you’re going to hit that corner.

“The frustration is that we really want to make it work, but with less than a month, the only thing that we can do is focus on compliance rather than all the benefits that BNG can generate.”

The Biodiversity Net Gain (BNG) Federation, a trade body representing companies delivering nature improvement under the BNG regime, has written to the Chancellor and the Housing Secretary demanding the immediate publication of guidance.

In the letter, the federation warned developers risk being stranded in a “regulatory vacuum” with less than a month before the deadline.

“NSIP developers tell us that their progress is being hindered,” wrote the trade body. “They cannot prepare without knowing what compliance will require.

“Without answers, the market remains on pause.”

They warned that Nationally Significant Infrastructure Projects (NSIPs) could face delays. These cover the biggest and most complex developments across the country, such as airport expansions, reservoirs and solar and wind farms.

Biodiversity rules were introduced in 2024 for smaller projects, but NSIPs have been subject to a separate timetable given their scale and complexity.

The warning comes at an uncomfortable moment for Rachel Reeves, with the speeding up of major infrastructure projects central to her growth agenda.

This included a vow last year to cut red tape so developers could “stop worrying about bats and newts” and unlock a construction boom.

Ms Baker said she was concerned that the rules were designed for smaller housing developments, rather than large projects.

“It’s 10pc, so if your baseline is one hectare or your baseline is 100 hectares, it really makes a difference,” she said.

“When you’re working on a 200km pipeline, you can spend a lot of time on just survey work, rather than investing that money in nature restoration.”

A government spokesman said: “We are committed to biodiversity net gain, as it plays a vital role in protecting nature while building the homes this country needs.

“We’ve heard clearly from developers, local authorities, and ecologists that the system needs to work better for some of the smallest developments, which is why we have announced plans for a new 0.2 hectare area exemption.

“Government will publish full responses to last year’s consultations shortly, setting out further detail, including how BNG will operate for Nationally Significant Infrastructure Projects.”

data.title: Why housebuilders have given up on London
data.description:
London is not building. In the first quarter of this year, 23 of the capital’s 33 boroughs started work on precisely zero new homes. How has a city, notorious for rising rents and housing shortages, ended up in this position?

London’s social housing waiting lists are at their highest level in more than a decade, with 336,000 households registered – a quarter of the national total.

The average first-time buyer in the capital faces a price tag of £500,000, while private rents have risen two thirds in the past 15 years to an average of £2,271 a month. This is 65pc higher than the national average, according to the Office for National Statistics.

And yet the industry that could boost the supply of housing and ease the pressure has all but downed tools.

Several forces are fuelling the problem: a labyrinthine and laughably complex planning system, a building safety regulator that spent years delaying applications, tax changes that have decimated investor returns, and the end of a government scheme to help first-time buyers get on the ladder.

Private sector housing starts in the capital have plummeted 84pc since 2015, according to data compiled by consultant Molior London. Just 5,547 private homes were started in London last year, against a target of 88,000, and compared to almost 34,000 in 2015.

London is supposed to deliver 440,000 of the Government’s proposed 1.5 million new homes this Parliament. At current rates of delivery, that target looks fanciful.

A Kafkaesque planning nightmare

London’s planning environment is the most challenging in the country. Of the 10 local authorities in England with the worst approval rates for residential planning applications, seven are in London.

The capital operates under not one, but three layers of planning oversight: national policy, the London Plan, and 33 separate local authorities, each with its own requirements, its own politics and its own interpretation of what a new development should deliver.

Paul Rickard, chief executive of Pocket Living, an affordable housing developer active in the capital, describes the level of red tape faced by schemes his company submitted recently.

By the time the application hit the planning portal, it ran to 89 documents, cost £1m and required 27 consultants. A recent project in Hackney generated 9,000 pages of planning documents.

Rickard argues that planning requirements should be tailored much more closely to the size and nature of projects to avoid unnecessary costs and bureaucracy.

“If you’re doing a six-storey building in central London, why do you need an aviation study?” he asks. “There is no point in spending the money. Planes are not going to crash into this building.”

For a small developer, this is not a straightforward hurdle to clear. A failed application can run to seven figures, and a quarter of smaller schemes must go through two or three planning applications.

“How many small developers can afford a million quid and then go, ‘well, that’s okay, I’ll just try again?’” Rickard asks.

Throw in vocal Nimby movements across the capital, and the result is a politically difficult climate to build new homes in. Council majorities in London are narrow – sometimes built on a 19pc share of the vote – and opposition to development from existing residents tends to be louder than support from those who would benefit from new homes being built.

A new regulator and a fresh wave of delays

Into an already Kafkaesque environment, the Building Safety Regulator (BSR) arrived in 2022. While its purpose – to prevent another Grenfell – was impossible to dispute, its execution has left the sector reeling with costly delays.

At its worst, the BSR was rejecting more than two thirds of pre-construction applications and taking an average of 36 weeks to process those it approved, against a target of 12 weeks.

Of 193 new-build high-rise applications submitted since the regulator’s founding, just 15 had successfully cleared the process by the time of publication of a Telegraph Money report in October 2025.

For London, where the density of homes means that the BSR’s scope is particularly broad, the effect on new starts has been severe.

However, since The Telegraph’s damning report on the BSR, there has been progress. New leadership has overhauled internal processes and Rickard now regularly hears of approvals coming through in 12 to 14 weeks. Developers have begun adjusting their financial assumptions accordingly.

“A year ago, they might have said, ‘I need to budget 12 months for [one step of the process] because I just do not know,’” he says. “They no longer need to do that.”

But three years of backlog cannot be undone in months, and Kate Butler, policy director at the British Property Federation, points out that despite improved approval times, there remains an underlying uncertainty among investors and developers.

A regulator that has demonstrated it can take a year or more to respond – even if it now does so faster – leaves lingering doubt for investment decisions.

An unattractive environment for investors

Tax changes and regulation have made matters worse. Previously, an investor buying a block of 10 flats was able to pay stamp duty based on the average price of each unit, rather than the total value of the entire building, thanks to multiple dwellings relief.

But in 2024, the Treasury scrapped this, and now a developer buying a £5m block of flats is taxed as if they are buying one £5m mansion, pushing them above the highest stamp duty threshold. This single change wrecked the viability of many London housing projects, wiping billions from the sector overnight.

The Renters’ Rights Act and the abolition of Section 21 “no-fault” evictions have also introduced fresh uncertainty about underwriting rental income, as tenants gain the right to challenge increases through a tribunal system that is already under strain.

“How you’re able to underwrite or predict any rental growth that you’re going to get is another [challenge],” says Butler.

Underlying all of this is a land market that refuses to correct.

In theory, when the cost of building goes up, landowners should drop their asking prices to keep development viable. In practice, those who don’t need to sell simply don’t – they hold on for better prices, or pass the land down to the next generation.

Pocket Living ran the numbers on what land would need to cost today for it to be able to build on the same financial terms as in 2020. Accounting for doubled finance costs, rising build prices and new regulatory levies, the answer was a 78pc fall in land values.

In reality, Rickard says that prices have dropped by around 40-50pc. While this is a meaningful correction, it is still far short of what developers need to make the sums work.

Inadequate support for first-time buyers

For the first time in 60 years, there is no government first-time-buyer support scheme. This is yet another obstacle for developers because it is much more difficult for aspiring buyers to purchase homes.

Mortgage rates have also jumped in recent years, making it even harder to borrow for a home purchase, and London rents have risen to levels at which saving a deposit is, for many working Londoners, simply out of reach.

Mortgage income multiples, set in the aftermath of the 2008 financial crisis, were not designed for a housing market in which rents in many parts of London exceed equivalent mortgage payments.

“We have people going from paying £1,500 a month in rent, all in, to owning their home for £1,400 a month,” says Rickard. “It is not necessarily that people cannot afford to buy. It is that they cannot access buying. That is a subtle but very important difference.”

Without buyers, developers cannot sell, and without sales, there is little incentive to build. Developers overwhelmingly argue that if first-time-buyer support were restored, construction activity would swiftly increase.

Pro-development Government fuels optimism

It is worth noting that the current Government did not create this crisis. Much of what is broken was done before it took office, and both Butler and Rickard are broadly supportive of Labour’s ambitions: major planning reforms, pro-development messaging and an ambitious new homes target.

“They are the most pro-development and open government for well over a decade,” says Rickard.

But a housing crisis of such a scale requires more than goodwill and posturing; Rickard would like to see a more aggressive removal of the countless layers of cost and bureaucracy.

“We need to be bolder, and we need to do it more quickly,” he adds.

When asked if Labour is on track to hit its new homes target, Butler is diplomatic.

“I think it’s optimistic and ambitious,” she says. “It’s hard to tell how close they’ll get or if they will hit it in this Parliament. But I do think there are things that they can do, and if they do them with urgency, they will see a big increase in development.”

data.title: I visited Britain’s most battered train bridge. No wonder the railways are in chaos
data.description:
For people living near Watling Street in Hinckley, it’s not unusual to be woken in the night by a thunderous bang as another lorry comes to grief at the local railway bridge.

This small Leicestershire town is home to Britain’s most battered bridge. Surrounded by depots of enormous vehicles, it was struck at least 22 times by lorries and buses throughout 2024 and 2025.

Across the country, a railway bridge is hit by a road vehicle every five hours, with about 1,700 strikes on bridges recorded by Network Rail between April 2024 and March 2025.

The result was 120 days of delays to train journeys last year and a £23m bill for taxpayers, with each strike costing about £13,000.

Some 43 per cent of lorry drivers admit to not measuring their vehicle before setting off and 52 per cent do not take low bridges into account.

Outside Hinckley on the A5, heavy goods vehicles of all types, from concrete mixers to delivery vans, push their luck at speed, zooming eye-wateringly close to the underbelly of the bridge that bears the scars of past collisions.

Each time one crunches underneath the steelwork, mayhem ensues, with long traffic jams, train delays and debris littering the road, according to residents.

“It’s a nightmare,” one neighbour says. “It’s frightening, like a bang or an explosion. It happens all the time, at least once a week.”

The bridge’s luminous yellow-striped warning signs are visibly scarred by scrapes, while the supporting walls are missing large chunks of brick.

In the lay-by, splinters and vehicle parts lie scattered through the undergrowth and residents of a nearby caravan park report tables, chairs and even mattresses flying out of lorries.

A mother with two young daughters sighs as she recalls how the bangs shake the caravan almost weekly.

Chantelle Callton, 35, tells The Telegraph: “Oh my God, it shakes. It’s like a bomb’s gone off. Everybody speeds down here, so it’s very dangerous.”

The bridge is so notorious that she was warned about it more than six years ago before she moved there.

“If we go a week when it’s not been hit, we’ll say: ‘We’re due one soon,’” she adds.

Dianna Smith, 60, who runs the caravan site with her husband, says her son and his friend had to help a trapped driver out of a lorry in the night. “It’s a ticking time-bomb until something bad happens,” she adds.

“They get that much speed up and don’t realise they’re too high. One lorry’s top was pulled back like a tin opener. They have to stop the trains and it messes everything up.”

Betty Watton, 44, sitting outside with her two chihuahuas, lives closest to the bridge and says drivers often flee the scene.

She says: “One lorry tore the whole top off and just left it in the road, rolled up like a big piece of scrap. One time paint tins spilt and paint went everywhere, but it’s mainly parcels.”

At a nearby depot filled with HGVs and lorries, workers clad in orange hi-vis laugh knowingly at the mention of the bridge.

They refuse to speak, but one jokes to another: “How many times have you hit it?”

Vehicles over a certain height at the Greenway Environmental depot have to take lengthy detours to avoid the bridge, including driving on to the motorway, the assistant manager says.

Another worker suggests it might not be the most strategic location for a lorry depot.

“Earlier this year, one was full of papers, cardboard and packaging. When that got ripped apart, it peeled the lid back and paper and card flew everywhere, blocking the street off for quite a while,” he says.

“There are a lot of industrial sites and major warehouses here. The kind of places that have drivers that are coming here for the very first time. They follow their sat nav, but still...

“There are a lot of light-up signs the whole way saying: ‘High vehicle. Turn back.’ But it’s become quite an issue that lorry drivers use their phones while driving.”

Mountpark, which is funding the project and working with National Highways, has planned to lower the road under the bridge since 2023, but critics of the scheme fear it would be complex and lead to flooding.

Karen, the landlady of a nearby pub, says road works will only add to the traffic jams blighting her business.

With the construction of a new supermarket depot around the bridge, she fears that the problem will not be going away anytime soon.

data.title: Can investors still bank on the big banks?
data.description:
Questor, The Telegraph’s investing column, takes a weekly view of the markets – what is moving them, what lies ahead and how all of this could affect your portfolios and financial goals.

If there was one sector which investment banks (“sell side”) and institutional fund managers (“buy side”) agreed was a smart investment this year, it was the banks.

While there were notable, contrarian exceptions, the tone of most commentary on the big lenders was positive.

It is proving a more volatile year than anticipated – not least thanks to the war in the Middle East – but even in this context, investors could be forgiven for being disappointed with the sector.

After a multi-year bull run, the FTSE 350 Banks index is down in the year to date. At the time of writing, it sits just 16th out of the 38 industrial groupings that comprise the FTSE 350.

In view of the old market saying – “a ‘bad’ stock in a ‘good’ sector will outperform a ‘good’ stock in a ‘bad’ sector” – it is worth reviewing the bull and bear case for the banks.

Good times

Analysts expect banks to generate almost a quarter of the FTSE 100’s forecast aggregate pre-tax profit for 2026 and pay out a fifth of its dividends. Lenders matter to the UK equity market as much as the supply of appropriately priced credit helps to grease the gears of the nation’s economic engine.

In this respect, it is easy to see why the banks’ shares had done so well.

First, the big lenders have earned their way back into investors’ affections. Their combined pre-tax profits came to £50.7bn in 2025, a record high, and analysts have pencilled in further increases for 2026 and 2027.

Second, the big banks have also paid their way back into investors’ affections with bumper cash returns in the form of both dividends and share buybacks.

The total return via the combined mechanisms in 2025 was £31bn, the second-highest sum ever from the big five (Barclays, HSBC, Lloyds, NatWest and Standard Chartered) and equivalent to a cash yield of 7.5pc based on their current stock market valuations.

Finally, at the start of the year at least, the macroeconomic environment looked benign. 

Interest rates were declining slowly, structural hedges locked in healthy net interest margins while loan losses were muted, regulatory fines were limited and – for those with investment banking operations – financial markets were buoyant.

That combination underpinned analysts’ forecasts for higher profits and dividends in 2026 and 2027.

Hard times

This year’s drop in the FTSE 350 Banks index might be a blip, but there are concerns investors need to consider as they assess the risk-reward profile of the banks’ shares.

First, the stocks are no longer as cheap as they were, thanks to the fact that they have done so well.

Each of Barclays, HSBC, Lloyds, NatWest and Standard Chartered began this decade trading at big discounts to tangible net asset value whereas, at their highs earlier this year, they all traded at a premium to book value.

In addition, only NatWest’s forecast dividend yield currently exceeds the 10-year gilt yield.

Second, HSBC has brought its buyback programme to a halt while it digests its $13.6bn (£10.1bn) purchase of the 37pc stake in Hong Kong’s Hang Seng Bank that it did not already own. 

NatWest has also declared a pause after its current programme following its £2.7bn swoop for Evelyn Partners.

That may mean total buybacks from the banks decline for the second year in a row in 2026 and the markets are proving sensitive to this shift in momentum, not least as they may prefer the safety of buybacks to the risks associated with acquisitions.

Finally, the macroeconomic environment looks less certain, mainly thanks to the war in the Middle East.

Interest rates may stay higher for longer to support net interest margins but any economic slowdown because of higher energy prices could lead to an increase in loan losses that offsets the extra interest income.

In addition, banking stocks are weak in the US and Europe – albeit again after a long, storming run.

Some of this may be because of fears over the lenders’ exposures to private credit and private equity – either through their loan books or their investment banking operations – where they have them.

The lenders continue to rebut such suggestions and assert that a couple of high-profile corporate failures in the US are part and parcel of the business of high-yield lending.

Some of it may just be wider worries about inflation, interest rates and above all growth, should the Middle East war last for longer than hoped and oil and gas prices stay elevated as a result, to the detriment of consumers’ cash flows and corporate profit margins.

A speedy resolution in the Strait of Hormuz could at least remove one worry and give the banks the chance to prove that they can be reliable providers of profits and dividends, two decades on from the first rumblings of the Global Financial Crisis.

data.title: Want your family to access your money as soon as you die? Choose these banks
data.description:
When someone dies, their bank account is typically frozen as soon as the provider has been notified. This is to make sure the money doesn’t get into the wrong person’s hands, but it can also leave relatives unable to pay for immediate expenses.

While many banks allow money to be released for payments such as funeral costs and inheritance tax, how quickly you can access the remaining funds depends on the provider.

Some banks will release relatively large sums without requiring a grant of probate, while others set far lower limits. The government-backed National Savings & Investments (NS&I), for example, will only release up to £5,000, whereas many high street banks allow up to 10 times that.

These thresholds apply to the total amount held with each provider and can make a big difference to how easily relatives can cover costs.

Here, Telegraph Money compares the thresholds set by major banks, and explains how the process works.

How much money will banks release before probate is required?

The table below outlines how much money some of the major banks will release before requiring a grant of probate.

The most flexible providers are those with £50,000 thresholds, such as Barclays, Nationwide, NatWest and Santander.

At the other end of the scale, NS&I has one of the lowest limits at £5,000, while digital banks such as Monzo and Starling also set relatively low thresholds.

What is the process when someone dies?

When someone dies, their bank should be notified as soon as possible – usually by a next-of-kin, the executor named in the will (if there is one), or another personal representative.

You can contact each bank separately, either online, over the phone or in-branch, or sign up to the free Death Notification Service which notifies participating financial institutions in one go.

Banks will typically ask for a copy of the death certificate and proof of identity for the executor or administrator, such as a passport or driving licence. The account will then be frozen, meaning most payments will be stopped.

Whether you can release any funds after this, and how quickly the account can be closed, will depend on the provider’s rules and how much money the account holds.

David McGuire, of law firm Weightmans, said: “Many people assume that there is a fixed rule about how much money can be released after someone dies without the need to apply for probate.

“Typically, and historically, this was considered to be a concession for estates worth less than £5,000. However, in reality, there is no universal threshold. Each bank takes a pragmatic approach and sets its own limit through internal guidelines.”

This can make it particularly complicated if there are multiple accounts and providers involved. According to Mr McGuire, it is not uncommon for one bank to release funds relatively quickly, while another takes a more cautious approach and requires probate before doing so. The process is also different for joint and sole accounts.

Mr McGuire said: “Joint accounts are usually more straightforward, with funds passing automatically to the surviving account holder, meaning those funds can often be accessed without delay. Sole accounts, however, are where the discrepancies can arise.”

If funds can be released without a grant of probate, the personal representative or executor will need to sign an indemnity form and provide ID.

What happens if grant of probate is required?

Probate is the legal process of dealing with someone’s estate after they die, including confirming who has the authority to manage their finances and distribute their assets according to their will (or the law if there is no will).

This often involves applying for a grant of probate, which many banks require before they agree to release larger sums of money. To do this, the executor of the will must apply to the Probate Registry. If there is no will, a close relative can apply for Letters of Administration.

The application process typically involves completing forms, valuing the estate, and calculating and paying inheritance tax. While it is possible to do this yourself, some people choose to use a solicitor to manage the process.

Applying for probate can take several weeks or even months, during which time access to funds may be limited.

Olivia Meekin, of law firm Bishop & Sewell, said: “This can cause financial difficulties for the personal representatives, who often have to continue paying the running costs of the deceased’s property (such as insurance, service charges and utility bills).”

After probate has been received, the bank will release the remaining balance to the personal representatives named on the grant of probate. The account can then be closed.

Do banks offer additional bereavement support?

Additional support will vary depending on the bank, but many offer dedicated bereavement teams and services to help executors or next of kin. You will usually find a bereavement section on each bank’s website to outline the steps you must take, and what the process will involve.

A Barclays spokesman said: “Where it helps reduce administrative burden, customers can also submit documents online, including completing and signing the settlement form digitally.

“We can also support with practical matters, such as releasing funds to help cover funeral costs and other priority bills, before accounts are fully settled, where appropriate.”

Meanwhile, HSBC says it offers end-to-end case support, guiding executors or personal representatives through each stage of the process and helping them understand what’s needed as part of estate management.

A spokesman for the bank added: “This includes explaining next steps, helping you gather any required information, and supporting you through the practical requirements involved in managing the deceased’s affairs with HSBC.”

Some banks can also help set up an executor’s account to help administer the deceased’s estate. But, like probate thresholds, the level of support can vary significantly between providers.

data.title: Labour donor in line for €1bn payday from Autoglass listing
data.description:
One of the Labour Party’s biggest donors is to make at least €1bn (£870m) from a deal to list the owner of Autoglass.

Gary Lubner, a South African businessman who has donated millions of pounds to Sir Keir Starmer’s party, is poised to cash in on the planned float of Belron, the car windscreen repair giant.

Formerly chief executive of Belron, Mr Lubner still owns a minority stake of around 3.3pc in the business, which is aiming to secure a valuation of between €30bn and €35bn once it lists on the Amsterdam stock exchange.

The deal could prove to be one of Europe’s biggest listings in years, as first reported by the Financial Times.

Mr Lubner, who owns his stake in the conglomerate through holding company Atessa, has emerged as one of Labour’s biggest donors in recent years.

Both Atessa and Belron management are said to own a combined 9.6pc of the business.

He has given £10m to the party since 2022, according to figures from the Electoral Commission.

Belron’s origins in South Africa

Belron is headquartered in Egham, Surrey, and owns an array of the world’s biggest glass repair companies, including Autoglass in the UK, European giant Carglass and US firm Safelite.

It traces its origins to a company that was started by Mr Lubner’s grandfather, Morris Lubner, in South Africa in the early 20th century.

The Lubner family has since been heavily involved with Belron, leading it to become one of the world’s biggest glass repair businesses, generating more than €6bn of sales each year.

Mr Lubner took over as Belron’s chief executive in 2000, succeeding his father, Ronnie Lubner, who had led the firm throughout the 1990s and fuelled its expansion into Britain.

He later helped grow Belron’s sales sevenfold through a series of acquisitions, before stepping down as chief executive in 2023.

Carlos Brito, the former chief executive of drinks AB InBev, has served as Belron’s boss ever since.

Mr Lubner was born in Johannesburg, South Africa, in 1959 and studied in Cape Town before moving to Britain in the 1980s to complete a Master of Business Administration (MBA) degree at London Business School.

The businessman first pledged his support to the Labour Party in 2021 following a meeting with Rachel Reeves.

In an interview with the Financial Times, Mr Lubner said he backed Labour after becoming “disenchanted” with the Conservatives. He has also described Brexit as “the biggest own-goal ever”.

Mr Lubner’s son, Jack Lubner, was elected as the national chairman of Young Labour, the Labour Party’s youth wing, in 2024.

No London listing

However, his ties to Labour were not enough to sway Belron to list in London, as the business chose Amsterdam over both the UK and the US.

Mr Lubner has long been a notable supporter of various liberal charities and has said he plans to give “the vast majority” of his wealth away to progressive causes.

In 2023, he launched his own charity, This Day, which seeks to support young people and asylum seekers in both Britain and South Africa.

Meanwhile, the majority owner of Belron is now D’Ieteren Group, a Belgian conglomerate, which bought into the company in 1999.

Clayton, Dubilier & Rice, an American private equity firm, is Belron’s second-biggest shareholder, with around a 20pc stake in the company.

D’Ieteren Group and Labour declined to comment. Belron was contacted for comment.

data.title: Town centres face losing their car parks after operator’s collapse
data.description:
Scores of car parks across Britain face closure following the sudden collapse of National Car Parks (NCP), in a development that threatens to deal a hammer blow to town centres.

Administrators for NCP, the country’s biggest operator, are threatening to abandon sites if landlords refuse to accept drastically lower rents.

Up to 20 car parks have already been shut and councils are now braced for dozens more closures across NCP’s remaining 318 sites.

Closures threaten not only to leave landlords out of pocket but also deal a blow to councils, which risk missing out on millions of pounds in business rates. An unoccupied building owned by a company in administration is typically exempt from business rates.

The closure of NCP car parks would also be a blow for town centres. Many are the main sites for people visiting shopping centres, meaning visitor numbers could suffer.

Insolvency experts at PwC and CBRE are currently scrambling to safeguard NCP’s future after it collapsed into administration this month.

This first involved stripping out loss-making sites across the company’s estate before then demanding lower rents from its landlords. Administrators are now seeking a buyer for the business.

However, the process has already been frustrated by tensions with landlords who are furious at administrators’ threats to close sites if terms cannot be agreed. In some cases, landlords are being asked to reduce rents by more than 50pc.

As well as seeking significantly cheaper rates, administrators have also demanded a four-week rent waiver to help put the company on a stable footing.

Landlords have been given just days to agree to new terms on sites occupied by NCP, increasing the likelihood of widespread closures.

One landlord said they were given 24 hours’ notice of the new offer and told to accept or face immediate closure.

“We view these last-minute bullying tactics as the lowest form of negotiation and are not prepared to do business with people who behave in this manner,” they said.

City lawyers are already in contact with landlords in the event of potential legal action, with some considering whether to take sites back from NCP.

It is understood that the sites already closed have effectively been abandoned, including those in Birmingham, Leicester, London and Luton.

One landlord said they were not even told the site had been closed.

It has sparked alarm among city councils over fears that empty car parks will become a magnet for anti-social behaviour.

Some local officials are already emailing landlords to ask what security measures will be put in place to keep sites safe.

The importance of car parks to local economies has also prompted speculation that some councils may have to foot the bill for reopening sites to support businesses. Such a move would increase pressure on already cash-strapped local authorities.

The developments will trigger scrutiny of NCP’s former Japanese owner, Park24. The private equity firm blamed a combination of Covid, high rents and elevated energy costs for NCP’s downfall, while PwC said the company came unstuck as “significant rent payments” fell due.

However, others have pointed to spiralling borrowings across the business, which rose to £350m by the end of 2025. Some City sources said these debts were largely to blame for the company’s collapse.

NCP charged high prices and had limited overheads, suggesting it should have been financially stable without debt. In central London, NCP charges motorists as much as £60 a day for parking.

Following their appointment, PwC said the business had “deteriorated over a number of years post Covid-19 as demand for parking has not recovered to historic levels, particularly across city-centre and commuter locations”.

Administrators said last night they were still working hard to sell some or all of the business, which was founded in 1931 by Colonel Frederick Lucas before merging with rival Central Car Parks in 1959.

A spokesman for the administrators said: “A small number of commercially unviable sites have closed this week and while no other closures are currently planned, the administrators are engaging with key stakeholders, including landlords of some sites, to reach agreements that will allow for their continued operation.”

data.title: ‘My husband is in prison but BT won’t let me cancel his TNT Sports subscription’
data.description:
Has a company treated you unfairly? Ruth Emery, our consumer champion, is here to help. To contact Ruth, click here*

Dear Ruth,

My husband was sentenced to prison in 2024. His crime was committed before I met him, and I knew nothing about it until his arrest. It has been devastating for me.

Since then, I’ve had problems cancelling the TNT Sports package on our BT account. It’s in his name, although I am a named contact and I pay the bills.

BT said that “named contacts” could not change subscriptions but that it would accept a power of attorney. My husband set up an ordinary power of attorney before he went to prison, but BT told me it would only accept a “lasting” power of attorney (LPA).

As a retired lawyer specialising in powers of attorney, I know the “ordinary” type (also known as “general”) should be adequate for any business matters, particularly as it is only needed during the time he is in prison and he is not suffering from any lack of capacity. However, BT doesn’t accept them. I made a complaint, but it was not upheld.

The only way my husband could make an LPA is for a lawyer to be authorised to go into the prison to see him and get the paperwork signed. That would be very expensive and would take many months.

Foolishly, I tried again to reach a solution with BT recently. The £22.31 monthly charge that I’m paying for a service I’m not using was going to increase by £2 a month at the end of March. I spent one hour and 45 minutes on the phone to BT, and was told the only way I could get rid of the sports package was for them to cancel my husband’s account, at a cancellation cost of almost £600, and open an account in my own name.

Very stupidly, I agreed to do this, but then realised he would lose his email account, which is very important as all his legal and business communications come to it. Prisoners can’t access email, but I am monitoring the account with his permission.

So, I tried to undo the cancellation to put everything back where it was, even though it will involve high monthly costs. The person I spoke to managed to cancel the new account in my name, and I was told my husband’s account would be reinstated.

Then, someone from BT’s “retention team” phoned asking to speak to my husband. I explained again why this wasn’t possible. The caller said he could say nothing to me because I was not a named contact on the account, even though everyone else at BT so far has confirmed that I am.

The worry about this is making me ill. I need a telephone and broadband service and the email address is vital. It is horrible having to go through the trauma of talking about a husband in prison every time I try to speak to BT. Every call is such a nightmare that I have to spend days psyching myself up to undergo the ordeal, and every time things just get worse, not better.

Please could you help me?

SJ, Gloucestershire

Dear reader,

I can only imagine the pain of seeing your husband imprisoned, and then having to repeatedly explain his absence to unsympathetic companies like BT.

Firms have previously been criticised for the way they treat relatives of deceased customers (and many have now improved their processes), but it seems that other scenarios, such as dealing with the loved ones of incarcerated customers, are still causing misery.

I hope this column highlights the importance of companies having a framework in place when these distressing situations arise, so that compassionate and practical solutions can be found swiftly.

You deliberately arranged an ordinary power of attorney for your husband. This is similar to an LPA, but crucially, it is for a temporary period. Someone might want to give a friend or family member an ordinary power of attorney to manage their affairs if, for example, they are physically injured (but still mentally sound), are abroad for a long period of time or are in prison.

An LPA, on the other hand, can be used when someone loses the mental capacity to make their own decisions. You didn’t want to create this power of attorney as it might make it difficult for your husband to resume dealing with his affairs when he’s released in a few years’ time.

You told me: “I think because LPAs now have such a high profile, staff are often not trained to recognise and deal with ordinary powers of attorney. BT has not suggested the power of attorney is invalid, only that it chooses not to deal with them.”

You refer to your actions as “foolish” and “stupid”, but you’re being too hard on yourself.

Prisoners can only call specific numbers belonging to friends and family, and not organisations like BT, so it really is up to you to cancel this TV package – and you have been battling for 18 months to make the telecoms giant listen to you. Occasionally, it suggested a solution, and understandably, you followed its advice.

I was furious with BT on your behalf, as I have been with other companies that have failed to put things right despite the customer complaining for well over a year.

(Yes, I’m talking about Barclays and the Post Office, which I detailed in a previous column. And British Gas in another.)

Thankfully, a day after I intervened, BT called you to apologise, and said it would cancel TNT Sports. You were told that a cancellation with 30 days’ notice could have been placed by yourself, and the firm would look at retraining its staff.

As a gesture of goodwill, it said it would refund six months of the sports charges. I urged BT to reconsider, given you have been trying to cancel it for longer than this. It agreed to a refund of charges for a full year and credited your account with £270. After more nagging by me, BT also sent you flowers to apologise.

A BT spokesman said: “We are very sorry that [your reader] had difficulty cancelling TNT Sports on her husband’s account. To acknowledge her experience, we have provided her with a goodwill gesture, and the complaint has been resolved.”

– Ruth

Consumer Champion Katie Morley is currently on maternity leave. Read her previous investigations here.

*Please note that by submitting your content to us, you are consenting to The Telegraph’s processing of your personal data where required by law. For further details, please see our Privacy Notice.
