data.title: AstraZeneca to invest £11bn in China after snubbing Britain
data.description:
AstraZeneca has unveiled plans to invest $15bn (£11bn) in China just months after dropping hundreds of millions of pounds of investment in Britain.

The Cambridge-based company announced the investment, which will be used to expand its medical manufacturing and research capabilities, during Sir Keir Starmer’s visit to Beijing. The Prime Minister hailed the financing as a deal that would help to support British jobs back home.

But the announcement follows a period of frosty relations between the Government and AstraZeneca which has seen the drugmaker threaten to halt investment in Britain in a row over NHS pricing.

The pharmaceutical giant, which is Britain’s biggest listed company, has abandoned plans for a £200m expansion of its Cambridge research site and £450m overhaul of its Liverpool vaccine factory.

The deal may also raise eyebrows given that five of AstraZeneca’s staff were detained by authorities in China in 2024 amid claims of potential illegal activity. The president of its China division was subsequently placed under investigation by local police, with no further details provided.

Astra is the largest drugs company in China, which is the company’s second-biggest market after the US.

Separately, AstraZeneca also announced on Friday that it had struck a deal worth as much as $18.2bn with China’s CSPC Pharmaceuticals.

Under the tie-up, AstraZeneca will have the global rights to sell CSPC’s obesity drugs outside China.

CSPC is currently developing once-monthly weight-loss jabs. The biggest weight-loss treatments currently require people to inject themselves once a week.

In total, if all of CSPC’s eight obesity drugs make it to market, AstraZeneca said the deal could be worth as much as $18.2bn – including future sales of the weight-loss treatments.

Sir Pascal Soriot, the company’s chief executive, is one of a clutch of Western business leaders who are occasionally summoned for meetings with Xi Jinping, the Chinese premier.

Sir Pascal said on Thursday: “Today’s landmark investment of $15bn begins an exciting next chapter for AstraZeneca in China, which has become a critical contributor to scientific innovation, advanced manufacturing and global public health.”

The “landmark” move was recognition of China’s growing scientific capabilities in areas that could help unlock new treatments for cancer, haematological conditions and autoimmune diseases, among others. It will be focused on sites in Bejing, Shanghai, Wuxi, Taizhou and Qingdao.

Sir Keir said: “Unlocking opportunities for British businesses across the globe and delivering for working people back home is always the driving force behind my international engagements.

“AstraZeneca’s expansion and leadership in China will help the British manufacturer continue to grow – supporting thousands of UK jobs.”

Astra has been at loggerheads with the Government over how much the NHS pays for medicines.

The company and its rivals have argued that the way the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (Nice) evaluates “value for money” on medicines is flawed and deprives patients of potential breakthrough treatments.

For instance, Nice previously rejected Enhertu, Astra’s breast cancer drug.

Wes Streeting, the Health Secretary, walked away from talks with drugmakers last summer after failing to strike an agreement.

But in December, ministers announced a new deal with the US that saw Nice agree to pay up to 25pc more for treatments, a move aimed at protecting the UK life sciences industry.

data.title: Tesco cannot guarantee shoppers will be shielded from food shortages
data.description:
The boss of Tesco has said he cannot guarantee that shoppers will not experience food shortages this summer.

Ken Murphy, the chief executive of Britain’s biggest supermarket, said it “would be wrong” for him to guarantee that customers will not see supply issues as a result of the situation in the Middle East.

“Can I provide guarantees? Of course, not,” he told The Telegraph.

“Given how quickly the situation globally changes and the volatility of the actors involved in this conflict, I think [it] would be wrong for me to give you any guarantee.

“I’m giving you a commitment that Tesco will do its very best to minimise the impact on customers, and I think that we’ve demonstrated that commitment over the last five years.”

Mr Murphy was speaking after it emerged the Government has drawn up contingency plans for potential food shortages if the war in Iran continued.

The Times revealed that officials have conducted an analysis for a “reasonable worst-case scenario” where carbon dioxide would be in short supply, which could result in shortages of chicken and pork.

The analysis is based on the Strait of Hormuz not being reopened by summer and no permanent peace deal.

Peter Kyle, the Business Secretary, confirmed planning for possible food shortages was under way on Thursday and said Sir Keir Starmer was “leading from the front”.

Mr Kyle told Times Radio: “Of course, we don’t comment on leaks. But this is now out there. But I want to reassure people that we have been acting since the very start of this conflict.”

He added: “We are looking at a variety of scenarios... When people read what is a leak, and it is difficult for me because these leaks are very unhelpful, but when people read it, they need to be reassured that we are doing this planning and we are doing this kind of scenario planning.

“I can tell you because I’m in these meetings that the Prime Minister has been there since the start of this war and he is going through and driving deep dives into resilience in lots of areas in our economy. You have a Government that is acting with creativity and boldness.”

Mr Kyle insisted food shortages were currently “not a concern” and said he would be “upfront” with the public if that were to change.

Mr Murphy said the Government was doing “the right thing” in drawing up contingency plans, adding: “I think the Government is planning and scenario-managing worst-case scenarios, and then saying, how would we cope in that scenario?”

He said Tesco was not currently experiencing any supply issues, with none of its farmers or growers having flagged problems.

Mr Murphy said: “We are constantly working on various scenarios ourselves internally, as you would expect us to do. None of our suppliers have flagged any issues to us at this point. I think that’s an important point.”

He said he welcomed the Government decision last month to reopen a Teesside carbon dioxide production facility, saying this was “really good news”.

The National Pig Association, which represents farmers and processors of the animals, said it was “concerned” about the impact of the war in the Middle East.

“If the production and therefore availability of CO2 is restricted, we would expect this to impact supply,” a spokesman said, calling for the meat industry to be prioritised for supply of the gas.

data.title: Banks deepen links with private credit amid crisis fears
data.description:
Banks are deepening their links to the murky world of private credit amid growing alarm about risks posed by the sector.

High street lenders struck deals with the so-called “shadow banks” at a record pace last year, The Telegraph can reveal.

Banks lined up 27 so-called “significant risk transfer” (SRTs), where private credit funds are paid to bear the loss from certain bank loans if borrowers default.

It marked the highest number of SRTs in the last five years, up from 24 deals in 2024.

The deepening links come amid growing alarm about the private credit industry, often referred to as “shadow banking”.

In the private credit industry, loans come not from banks but from sources such as hedge funds and financiers. It has grown rapidly since the financial crisis because it is more loosely regulated than bank lending.

Andrew Bailey, the governor of the Bank of England, has compared the risks in the sector to the subprime mortgage market before the 2008 financial crash. Problems in that relatively obscure corner of finance triggered a global crisis.

Unlike mainstream lenders, shadow banks are largely unregulated. The sector has grown rapidly since the financial crisis and is now a $3tn (£2.2tn) industry.

However, a series of high-profile failures of shadow bank-backed businesses and a suspected fraud in the UK have raised concerns about loose lending standards and poor due diligence. That has triggered scrutiny of links between banks and private credit funds.

Prof Raghavendra Rau, of Cambridge Judge Business School, said: “How much exposure the regulated banks have to lightly regulated non-bank financial institutions is opaque.

“And worse, because private credit lacks public market discipline, the losses can be magnified, meaning unexpected shocks could cascade through the financial system rapidly before regulators even see them coming.”

SRT deals are the main link between high street banks and private credit. They have become wildly popular in Europe, with Barclays and Santander amongst the biggest players.

In a typical SRT deal, a bank pays a private credit fund to assume the risk of default for a group of mortgages or company loans.

The arrangement allows banks to hold less cash in reserve to guard against loans going bad, freeing it up for fee-earning activity.

In the UK, each SRT must be approved by the Prudential Regulation Authority (PRA), the banking watchdog. According to a Freedom of Information request, banks sent 27 deals for approval by the regulator in 2025. In 2024, 24 deals were sent for approval and 19 were notified to the PRA in 2023.

Some regulators have grown increasingly concerned about the practice, amid broader scrutiny of shadow banks.

In a report released by the International Monetary Fund last year, the global watchdog warned that the rapid growth of SRTs could “increase vulnerabilities” to financial stability, “including higher leverage in the financial system”.

It also warned that risks were exacerbated by the “private and opaque nature” of the transactions themselves.

In some cases, private credit funds borrow money from other banks to fund SRT deals. Some fear that this is setting up the risk of a house-of-cards-style collapse that could ricochet across the banking industry if a significant number of loans start to go bad.

Jamie Dimon, the chief executive of JP Morgan, has said that there could be more “cockroaches” in the private credit sector after the collapse of Tricolor, an auto lender, and First Brands Group, a car-parts supplier. Both were heavy users of private credit.

In the UK, the sector has been shaken by the collapse of Market Financial Solutions (MFS), a private credit fund in the property industry that has been mired in allegations of fraud. Lawyers representing Paresh Raja, the company’s founder, admitted to mistakes but denied any intention to defraud.

The Prudential Regulation Authority was contacted for comment.

data.title: Hope for run-down Boots as billionaire owner’s wife exits
data.description:
The British high street is experiencing its biggest cull in years.

Already reeling from a series of costly tax raids at the hands of Rachel Reeves, tough Christmas trading has proven to be the nail in the coffin for a string of struggling household names, including Claire’s, Russell & Bromley and LK Bennett. More high-profile casualties are expected in the coming weeks.

Amid the continued hollowing out of town centres, one high street stalwart remains: Boots.

Outposts of the lipstick-to-prescriptions business can still be found across the country, but many are in a sad state. After its exit from the stock market in 2007, Boots has spent the last two decades being starved of investment.

With serial dealmaker Stefano Pessina leading the charge, Boots has been allowed to dwindle – first under the ownership of private equity’s original Barbarians at the Gate, KKR, then as a forgotten overseas outpost of American pharmacy giant Walgreens.

Now Boots is in the hands of another private equity house, Sycamore Partners, after the New York-based outfit took Walgreens off the public markets last summer. Employees must fear yet more decline.

However, a shake-up at the very top of the company may signal a revival for one of Britain’s best-loved and most important retailers.

After leading the pharmacy giant for almost a decade, Boots chief executive Ornella Barra is stepping down to become chair. She replaces Pessina, who is also her business partner and husband, and is believed to still own half of the business at the ripe age of 84.

At the same time, managing director Anthony Hemmerdinger is being handed a much bigger role running Boots, including oversight of the No7 beauty brand – long regarded as one of several jewels in the stable.

The management reshuffle is viewed internally, and in the City, as another major step towards setting Boots free from its ownership purgatory and returning it to the UK stock market.

City sources said a float is still the main long-term ambition of the owners, and more likely to occur next year than in 2026.

A former executive said a spin-off of Boots “was always the plan” and the boardroom changes were “a step towards that”. The break-up of Walgreens into five standalone entities – Boots among them – shortly after its £8bn takeover last year was seen as the first real step.

The prospect of Boots being groomed for a spectacular public markets comeback under new leadership will raise hopes of a long overdue makeover of its unloved shops. Shoppers will be hoping that it also means a halt to any further closures.

The sale to Sycamore came after Boots axed 300 stores across the UK, drastically reducing its footprint from 2,200 to around 1,900 branches. Boots also shut 48 opticians in 2020, and had kickstarted a store closure plan the previous year that ended with more than 200 outlets being given the chop.

The status of Boots as more than just your typical bog-standard retailer means its future garners more attention than others.

Armed with Britain’s best-selling skincare brand No 7, the chain believes it can continue to compete in the booming beauty market despite the excitement surrounding the arrival of LVMH-backed French phenomenon Sephora in Britain, as well as other upstarts.

Amid the glamour of expensive lipstick and eyelash-thickening mascara, it is easy to forget that Boots is an integral part of the country’s healthcare system. Without its sprawling network of pharmacists, thousands of people would struggle to get their vital medicines and daily pills.

Its last round of store closures was accompanied by an apologetic statement explaining that the decision was “not taken lightly” and that in all cases there was an alternative store less than three miles away.

Yet that didn’t prevent an outcry in some affected towns, with locals complaining about being forced to endure long queues at other pharmacies, which in some cases had led to delays in getting their prescriptions.

Boots bosses have long rejected the portrayal of a business stuck in retreat as lazy and outdated.

“The Boots Group is in great shape, both financially and operationally, with Boots UK having grown market share for 19 consecutive quarters,” Barra said as she stepped aside.

But that fact masks the two-speed nature of the business. A former executive said there are around 500 Boots shops that are “the powerhouse of the business” – the vast majority of which have already been revamped.

However, the rest are much smaller shops, which are vital to the surrounding communities but cost “between £50,000 and £100,000” to refurbish with no resulting “uplift” in turnover, making it hard to justify the outlay.

“Pharmacies are really difficult to make money in now, because of what the Government pays per prescription,” another ex-director said. In November, the Department of Health and Social Care announced that NHS prescription charges would be frozen for the second year running.

 Clive Black, analyst at Shore Capital, adds: “There has been immense profiling of a small number of new and modernised stores, gaining lots of capital expenditure and senior management attention, to show the newfound vitality of Boots.

“But the reality is that many Boots are drab, under-invested, and needing of an injection of elevated execution that British shoppers will notice across the board.”

Those who know the business well say Boots branches need to become even bigger healthcare hubs, so that they can alleviate pressure on local GPs by providing more of the services that you would usually go to your doctor for.

The chain already does private online doctor consultations, vaccinations and blood pressure checks, as well as specialised treatments for skin and hair loss. However, it is eyeing a larger role in diagnostics with “at-home” testing kits for conditions such as high cholesterol, vitamin deficiencies and food intolerances. These will be offered under the mantra of standing “shoulder to shoulder with the NHS”.

If the move is successful, it would justify spending more capital on its smaller stores, many of which struggle to turn a profit.

The former executive says: “The mantra…was always ‘We’re going to have a great Boots in every major town, and none that we’re ashamed of.’ I think that will continue.”

data.title: BP hails ‘exceptional’ oil trading during Iran war
data.description:
BP has hailed an “exceptional” performance at the start of the year after cashing in on surging oil prices during the Iran war.

The energy giant said it expected trading revenues to jump during the first three months of 2026 owing to “heightened volatility in crude oil, natural gas and refined products prices”.

This represents a significant rebound for BP after a weak performance in the last quarter of 2025, demonstrating how the energy shock unleashed by the closure of the Strait of Hormuz is benefitting the world’s biggest oil and gas giants.

At least a fifth of the world’s oil and gas supplies rely on the crucial trade artery.

The International Energy Agency (IEA) said global oil demand dropped sharply following the outbreak of the Iran war amid soaring prices and a shortage of supplies from the Middle East.

Figures from the IEA’s monthly oil report showed that global demand was down 3.4pc in March. It is expected to decline a further 1.1pc in April to 100.4 million barrels a day, which would be the lowest level in three years.

The IEA warned that “demand destruction will spread as scarcity and higher prices persist”.

BP highlighted the impact of surging oil and gas prices in its latest update, noting that the average price of a barrel of Brent crude hit $81 in the first quarter, up from $63 in the final three months of the year.

The oil price peaked at nearly $120 last month, although it is now hovering around $100 after the US and Iran struck a two-week ceasefire last week.

Blow to financial results

BP said the rapid changes in market conditions were “expected to impact financial results”. It added that oil production and operations would be “slightly lower compared to the fourth quarter 2025” after its operations in the Middle East were affected by the conflict.

Around a fifth of BP’s overall oil production is generated in the Middle East, although it does not operate any refineries in the region.

Other oil companies have been caught up in the conflict after their refineries were damaged by drone attacks.

The conflict represents the first challenge for Meg O’Neill, who took over as BP’s chief executive earlier this month.

She became BP’s third boss in just two and a half years following the departure of Bernard Looney after failing to disclose romantic affairs with colleagues, while her predecessor Murray Auchincloss was ousted late last year.

Ms O’Neill told staff that BP will be simplified into two main business units, moving the company back to the way it was structured before its ill-fated attempt to invest in green energy in 2020.

She said that one division of the group will focus on oil and gas production, while another will cover BP’s refining, distribution and retail activities.

In line with its current strategy, Ms O’Neill said BP would continue to redirect billions of pounds from green energy projects towards oil and gas in an attempt to increase profitability.

Shell said last week that it expected its oil trading business to report a boost in earnings following the rapid increase in oil prices.

data.title: Supermarkets ‘taking the mickey’ on olive oil prices, says Filippo Berio
data.description:
The boss of Filippo Berio has accused supermarkets of “taking the mickey” out of customers with their sky-high olive oil prices.

Walter Zanre told Sky News that wholesale olive oil prices had fallen in recent months but said supermarkets were refusing to follow suit.

“We brought prices down twice last year and it’s not all been passed on to the consumer, which is a huge frustration,” he said.

It follows a surge in the price of olive oil since 2022, after adverse weather in olive-growing countries led to poor harvests.

Spain, which produces almost half of the world’s olive oil, was hit by droughts and heatwaves in 2023 and 2024. The weather caused global olive oil production to fall by almost one million tonnes.

It caused higher prices for many well-known olive oils, including Filippo Berio, which uses a blend of oils from across Europe despite being an Italian brand.

The average price of a 500ml bottle of Filippo Berio has risen from £3.75 in 2022 to £7.50 now, according to the tracker Trolley.co.uk.

Tesco’s own-brand olive oil stands at £5.50 for 500ml. This is up from £5.20 this time last year, Trolley.co.uk data shows. The same-size bottle of Nicolas Alziari olive oil, at Waitrose, sells for £22.50.

The boss of Filippo Berio argued that prices should have come down rapidly for customers after stronger production more recently meant there was better availability of oil.

According to the International Council of Olive Growers, the wholesale Italian olive oil price was down 30pc in March compared to last year’s figures.

‘Expand their margins’

Mr Zanre, who has worked for the olive oil brand for more than 25 years, said producers had reduced the price at which they sell to supermarkets, but that this was not being seen on the shelves.

He said: “We can’t dictate retail prices. For me, it’s immensely frustrating that supermarkets have taken the opportunity to expand their margins.”

Mr Zanre accused supermarkets of profiteering from strong demand for olive oil, suggesting grocery bosses had probably been surprised at how resilient olive oil sales had been in recent years, even though it was more expensive, and so had decided not to drop prices.

His claims prompted a backlash from retail leaders on Tuesday, who argued that they passed on reductions where they could.

Andrew Opie, the director of food and sustainability at the British Retail Consortium, said: “Retailers work hard to pass on cost savings to customers wherever possible and, as confirmed by the Competition and Markets Authority, operate on very tight margins, reflecting a market driven by savvy customers.

“Olive oil, like many everyday products, is something shoppers can compare across brands and retailers to take advantage of promotions or switch to alternatives that suit their budget.”

The attack against supermarkets comes amid fears that prices for olive oil could soon start rising again.

The war in Iran, and consequent fuel crisis from the closure of the Strait of Hormuz, is expected to hit olive growers because they need diesel for their tractors as well as to fuel water pumps which irrigate the olive groves. It means production costs will probably rise this season.

It will add to pain for shoppers, who have been told to brace for higher prices across supermarket shelves. The Food & Drink Federation warned earlier this month that food inflation could hit 10pc by the end of the year, up from early forecasts of 3.2pc.

It said the war was having a “direct and immediate impact” on costs for UK food and drink manufacturers.

data.title: Morrisons to sell dozens of pharmacies in scramble to cut costs
data.description:
Morrisons has put dozens of pharmacies up for sale in a fresh effort to cut costs.

The private equity-owned supermarket has launched a sale process after concluding that many of its in-store pharmacies are not financially viable.

As a result of the review, property agents have been tasked with selling some of the pharmacies on a store-by-store basis, rather than the entire portfolio.

Those affected are likely to remain open in stores, but under the brand of any eventual buyer.

The move to sell dozens of pharmacies comes after Morrisons’ losses plunged to £381m last year, fuelled by steep borrowing costs.

Rising debt pressures have led bosses to reduce borrowings, which fell to £3.1bn from £3.5bn last summer.

The sale of pharmacies is the latest attempt to strip back the business and focus increasingly on traditional retail.

A separate restructuring last year triggered the closure of four pharmacies, alongside 52 cafés, 17 convenience stores, 35 meat counters and 13 florists.

This led to more than 300 jobs being put at risk of redundancy, as bosses identified areas “where the costs of operations are significantly out of line with usage, volumes, or the value that customers place on them”.

Rami Baitieh, chief executive of Morrisons, said the cuts were a “necessary part of our plans to renew and reinvigorate Morrisons”.

Morrisons is pursuing a turnaround strategy after Mr Baitieh said the business had been going through a “challenging period”.

The retail boss blamed weaker consumer spending among poorer shoppers, many of whom have been affected by price rises.

This has led to Morrisons struggling to boost its share of the grocery market, which is at 8.4pc.

That is up narrowly from 8.5pc this time last year, although it is well below the 9.9pc it achieved in October 2021, when it was bought by private equity firm Clayton Dubilier & Rice in a £10bn deal.

This has prompted speculation that Morrisons could lose its position as Britain’s fifth-largest supermarket to Lidl, which now boasts 7.7pc of the grocery market.

Morrisons is not alone in seeking to sell off pharmacies, as the sector has been battered by rising costs in recent years.

Around 1,400 pharmacies have shut in England since 2017, according to the National Pharmacy Association, leaving many households unable to access medicine in their areas.

This has been blamed on rising wage costs and a shortfall in how much the NHS pays pharmacies.

Boots has also closed hundreds of pharmacies in recent years, while LloydsPharmacy, which had 138 sites, collapsed in 2023.

Rachel Reeves has piled more pressure on pharmacies over the past year by increasing business rates and the minimum wage.

Supermarkets have also come under pressure from rising taxes under Labour, most notably by the Chancellor’s decision to increase employer National Insurance rates.

Speaking last month, Mr Baitieh pleaded with the Government to avoid hitting retailers with any extra costs.

He said: “I would say, please, no more. This is very important for the market. We expect inflation to go up.”

Morrisons declined to comment.

data.title: Denmark’s fat jab champion is slimming down the whole economy
data.description:
Novo Nordisk, Denmark’s fat jab pharmaceutical giant, reached an unwelcome milestone last week.

After a dizzying 76pc plunge in its share price over the past 18 months, a torrid week of trading meant it finally gave up the last of its market gains recorded since June 2021 when it released pioneering weight-loss drug Wegovy.

It follows years in which Novo Nordisk has been arguably Denmark’s greatest global success story. Its first-mover success with Wegovy and Ozempic turned it into Europe’s largest company, with the $100bn (£74bn) weight-loss drugs market at its feet.

The tide has since turned. This week, Novo admitted that its newest drugs were showing less promise in trials than those of its American rival Eli Lilly. It has also yielded to Donald Trump’s demand for hefty price cuts in the hitherto lucrative US market.

Novo is now in a metaphorical weight-loss programme of its own. It has shed its long-serving chief executive, cut 11pc of its workforce and lost hordes of foreign shareholders.

This is not a typical tale of corporate tumult. Novo is such a colossus in Denmark that its ructions can shake the country’s whole economy.

Yet Mette Frederiksen, the country’s Left-wing prime minister, chose this moment to take the country to an early election, set for March 24.

Since taking office in 2019, Frederiksen has steered Denmark through the turbulence of the pandemic, the Ukraine war and Trump’s threats to seize Greenland.

She will be hoping that Novo’s travails do not derail her bid for a third straight election victory.

It is something of a gamble. Novo’s fate is inextricably linked to that of the economy and thus to her own political prospects.

The company sold 309bn krone (£36bn) of drugs last year, which is more than 10pc of Denmark’s annual $430bn GDP. It employs one in every 100 Danes.

As Novo’s crisis deepened, Denmark’s economy – which was one of the fastest-growing in Europe – slowed dramatically at the end of last year. The country recorded 2.2pc growth in the third quarter, falling to just 0.2pc in the fourth.

Per Hansen, an economist at bank Nordnet, says: “One of the engines that has been driving the Danish economy over the past two to three years has been Novo Nordisk and the pharma industry.”

Las Olsen, the chief economist at Danske Bank, says that if you remove the pharmaceuticals sector, Denmark’s growth rate would be more like 1pc or 1.5pc.

“Which is fine, of course – but not the stellar growth rate that we have seen in the headline numbers,” he says. “I think we will grow more in line with European economies, perhaps a little less.”

These aren’t views that will cheer up the gloomy Danes.

Measures of Danish consumer confidence have languished in deeply negative territory ever since Russia invaded Ukraine.

“It’s a conundrum,” says Helge Pedersen, the chief economist at Nordea Bank. “House prices are at a record high, there’s almost no unemployment – everything is good but consumers are depressed.”

He says ordinary Danes are still preoccupied with inflation, even though the official rate is just 0.8pc.

“People are still worried about inflation, in particular food prices. We all discuss meat prices, they’re so high, coffee prices, chocolate prices. But that’s perceived inflation, which is higher than reality.”

Frederiksen and her centre-Left coalition partners have already started delving into the public coffers to soothe voters’ disgruntlement.

The Danish government recently cut the tax on electricity bills to almost zero. It also increased spending on childcare and welfare support and removed taxes on coffee, chocolate and sugar.

It has plenty of fiscal firepower. It was running a budget surplus estimated at more than 2pc of GDP in 2025 and its debt-to-GDP ratio was 30pc last year, one third that of Britain.

That allowed Denmark to ramp up defence spending last year by more than $4bn, which – along with higher healthcare expenditure – may prop the economy through any pharma slowdown.

The defence spending is a response to the Russia threat and also to pressure from Trump. He has called for Europe to fund its own defence and he is looking to wrestle at least de facto control over the Danish territory of Greenland.

Hansen says the Greenland issue may also have cast a pall over consumer confidence.

“Danish people are afraid that the new world order that is initiated by Mr Trump is something we have not dealt with since World War Two. That is really what is scaring the Danes,” he says.

Paradoxically, though, it may be helping the tough-talking and experienced Frederiksen. Since Trump’s Greenland manoeuvring became more explicit and tangible, her Social Democratic Party’s previously fading popularity has rebounded.

Geopolitical uncertainty and cost of living pressures are common across Europe. But in Denmark, there could be another more idiosyncratic source of economic anxiety.

Around 10pc of Danes hold shares in Novo. Many more will be exposed to its precipitous share-price decline via their private pensions.

Hansen, though, says Danish investors have been strangely patriotic: as foreigners have fled, Danes have been buying up the stock.

“The number of Danish private clients with us that hold one or more Novo Nordisk shares keeps climbing,” he says. “People still think there is a difference between what Novo Nordisk will achieve longer term and its current valuation in the stock market.”

Denmark’s economists, like its investors, also seem relatively sanguine about their country’s economic champion. Olsen says he was more worried during Novo’s breakneck growth phase than he is now.

“The fear was that it might erode the competitiveness of the rest of the economy. The large amount of hiring at Novo Nordisk could have led to very high base growth, that would then leave the rest of the economy uncompetitive if something was to go wrong,” he says.

“But what we have seen so far is that manufacturing wages growth in Denmark has, if anything, been lower over the last four or five years than we see in competitor countries. There is a competitive economy outside of Novo Nordisk.”

Pedersen agrees that the Danish economy is diversified enough to weather the Novo storm: “But of course you should keep an eye on it because the numbers are still so big on the macro level.

“Politicians do need to be aware that we have two Denmarks – one with and one without the pharmaceutical industry.”

Right now, the politician with the most riding on the Danish pharmaceuticals industry is Frederiksen.

As she called the election, she talked mostly about Greenland and wealth taxes.

Novo Nordisk’s decline might have garnered global attention with the company no longer deemed Denmark’s greatest success story.

Yet for Frederiksen, the hope is that this will simply be background noise as she bids for a third straight election victory – instead of a shot in the arm for her political rivals.

data.title: Axel Springer wins approval for Telegraph takeover
data.description:
The incoming owner of The Telegraph has revealed ambitions to make it “a truly international brand” after his £575m takeover was waved through.

Mathias Döpfner, the chief executive and controlling shareholder of the German news publisher Axel Springer, assured staff that The Telegraph will retain its “British identity” and that after almost three years of ownership limbo, it “should now take off”.

He welcomed the decision by Lisa Nandy, the Culture Secretary, to approve the sale without further delays.

Mr Döpfner said: “We are pleased to have received UK government approval to proceed with this acquisition. After a long period of uncertainty, we are able to confirm that we will invest significantly in The Telegraph’s editorial excellence and international growth.”

It came after Ms Nandy told MPs that she had given permission for the Abu Dhabi-backed investor RedBird IMI to sell security in The Telegraph to Axel Springer. The company also owns the specialist titles Politico and Business Insider, as well as scores of print and online publications across Europe.

Mr Döpfner said: “The Telegraph will join a global network committed to independent reporting while retaining its distinct editorial voice and British identity.”

In a memo he told staff he would personally take charge of The Telegraph on Axel Springer’s executive board, calling it “a great privilege – and an even greater responsibility”.

Mr Döpfner added: “What connects us with The Telegraph is not only a shared commitment to freedom and centrist conservative values, but also a distinctive blend of tradition and progress. Both of us have consistently approached technological change and the opportunities of the media industry with a forward-looking mindset.”

The security was set up in 2023 to be immediately converted to full ownership. It means that Axel Springer could be in effective control of The Telegraph within days.

Mr Döpfner, 63, and a journalist by background, sought to reassure staff and readers about Axel Springer’s values and commitment to editorial independence.

He said: “Protecting the newsroom by a clear separation between commercial and editorial decisions – this principle is deeply embedded in our corporate DNA. Decisions about what is published rest with the editors-in-chief. That’s non-negotiable.

“Does that mean that anything goes? No. Our values define the framework. As a journalist and a committed democrat, I hold strong personal views, which I express openly in my writing and in discussion.”

Mr Döpfner added: “However, they do not constitute instructions for our journalists – and everyone who works with us understands this. 

“I value debate in the spirit of pluralism and freedom of expression – especially with our editors-in-chief, all of whom are independent and strong-minded. And sometimes we agree to disagree.”

The decision by Ms Nandy to allow the transfer of security in The Telegraph came alongside a preliminary decision not to trigger a regulatory investigation that would have imposed further months of delay.

She said she was “not minded to” involve the Competition and Markets Authority or Ofcom. The regulators used to scrutinise media takeovers where there are competition or public interest concerns.

Axel Springer is Europe’s largest news publisher, with decades of heritage in independent journalism via German major titles including the tabloid Bild and broadsheet Die Welt. 

Its English-language publications Politico and Business Insider have small market shares in the UK, and lawyers for the company have argued that they do not threaten competition or media plurality.

Ms Nandy did not announce a final decision and reserved the right to change her mind if new information comes to light.

But she said: “I am pleased to be able to take these positive steps, which give greater certainty to The Telegraph and its staff.”

Axel Springer also requires regulatory approval in Ireland and Austria, which may take some weeks. However, the British decision is the critical one and lays the ground for Axel Springer to take effective charge of The Telegraph very soon.

The news was greeted with relief in The Telegraph newsroom. It will all but end a period of damaging uncertainty which began on June 7, 2023, when the Barclay family’s unpaid debts caught up with them.

Lloyds Banking Group seized control of The Telegraph because of £1.2bn in overdue lending to its proprietors, who also owned the parcel courier Yodel and online retailer Very.

The bank planned an auction of The Telegraph, which was derailed by a deal between the Barclay family and the rulers of the United Arab Emirates. The Gulf state paid off its debts in exchange for security over The Telegraph via RedBird IMI, which it majority funded.

Its plan to take full ownership was blocked following a campaign against the takeover by The Telegraph and a cross-party outcry, which led to a ban on foreign state ownership of newspapers.

Without it, The Telegraph would have covered the Iran conflict under the ownership of the UAE. In recent weeks, its authorities have clamped down on free speech, including jailing British visitors for social media posts.

Baroness Stowell of Beeston, a Conservative peer who played a key role in legislating against UAE ownership, said: “Finally, The Telegraph’s ownership has been settled and it is just such great news.

“Here’s to a great future and continued press freedom.”

The decision to block RedBird IMI set off a diplomatic row and a torturous form of corporate captivity, as a series of onward sales were agreed and aborted.

They included one deal with a previously unknown New York publisher and one with the US private equity firm RedBird, which was the UAE’s junior partner. It planned to allow the Gulf state to retain a 15pc stake after Labour relaxed the ban on foreign-state shareholdings. The Telegraph also campaigned against those plans.

When they collapsed in October, Lord Rothermere, the owner of The Daily Mail, agreed to buy The Telegraph in a deal that was set to face lengthy regulatory scrutiny. 

Then, finally, last month, Axel Springer swooped in with a knockout bid of £575m, which appears set to glide through the political and regulatory gauntlet.

The price represents a profit on the £500m paid by RedBird IMI and exceeds most analysts’ estimates of The Telegraph’s value in a rapidly shifting news landscape.

Mr Döpfner arrives on Fleet Street as an established international force. He was appointed chief executive of Axel Springer in 2002 and previously bid for The Telegraph when it was last sold, in 2004.

The company, founded in post-war Berlin, has successfully expanded under his leadership and has said it aims to become the free world’s leading news publisher. Its corporate “constitution” promotes freedom, free speech, free markets, the rule of law and democracy.

Mr Döpfner told The Telegraph staff that he suspected that the company’s eponymous founder “deep down, he would have liked to have been English”.

“In a sense, he now is – four decades after his death,” he added.

data.title: Ovo Energy sues TalkTalk over botched takeover
data.description:
Ovo Energy has sued troubled broadband giant TalkTalk over a botched deal to buy up customers.

The energy company has launched a legal claim in a dispute about the sale of broadband customers to TalkTalk, as Ovo scrambles to secure a sale.

Ovo sold SSE Energy Services’s phone and broadband business in 2022, which saw 135,000 customers transferred to TalkTalk.

The company had acquired the telecoms business following its takeover of SSE Energy Services, a gas and electricity provider, two years previously.

To complete the deal, TalkTalk paid an initial sum upfront and agreed to make further contributions once certain milestones were met.

However, the deal soured after swathes of customers left TalkTalk, rendering the takeover largely pointless. TalkTalk has since refused to pay the outstanding sum to Ovo, prompting the company to launch a legal claim.

The lawsuit underscores efforts by Ovo to settle its affairs as bankers scramble to complete a sale.

Ovo, which is Britain’s fourth-largest gas and electricity supplier with four million customers, has been attempting to raise £300m in fresh investment for months in an effort to shore up its finances.

The company is facing uncertainty over its future after failing to meet financial stress tests introduced by regulator Ofgem in the wake of the 2022 energy crisis, which caused dozens of suppliers to collapse.

Bankers at Rothschild have been appointed to oversee the fundraising process, with a sale of the business also under consideration. It is thought that any deal will not include Ovo’s technology arm Kaluza.

Ovo has also begun cutting tens of millions of pounds in costs under a new business plan aimed at securing its survival.

Despite warnings about its ability to stay afloat and posting losses of £135m in 2024, Ovo still paid out £27m that year to a company owned by Stephen Fitzpatrick, its founder.

Mr Fitzpatrick, who founded Ovo in 2009, has extracted tens of millions of pounds through a licensing deal to use the brand. The energy company last year bought the brand outright in a £150m deal.

Mr Fitzpatrick’s other ventures include Kensington Roof Gardens, a private members’ club in west London once owned by Sir Richard Branson.

The legal claim will create a problem for TalkTalk, which is struggling under its £1.4bn debt burden.

Surging interest costs have pushed the company to heavy losses and caused it to fall behind on payments to suppliers including Openreach, BT’s network division.

The troubles have prompted shareholders, including Ares Management and Sir Charles Dunstone, the executive chairman of the broadband giant, to provide repeated cash injections to keep the company afloat. The latest £115m lifeline in March marked the third such deal since the end of 2024.

TalkTalk is also seeking a buyer for part or whole of the business. Larger rival Virgin Media O2 had been considering a deal for its consumer division but has now walked away.

Ovo and TalkTalk declined to comment.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: JCB threatens to quit Britain over inheritance tax raid
data.description:
The heir to JCB’s multibillion-pound manufacturing fortune has threatened to quit Britain in protest against Rachel Reeves’s inheritance tax raid.

Jo Bamford, son of Lord Bamford, the JCB chairman, warned the Staffordshire-based business could easily relocate to America to avoid paying 20pc tax on assets worth more than £2.5m.

“The family tax is a real problem,” Mr Bamford told City AM, warning that JCB “could quite easily become an American business”.

“I love being in Britain,” he said. “I love being here. I love our factories. But I would say to a political party of any stripe, there’s only so much you can ultimately do.”

Founded in 1945, JCB is one of Britain’s biggest family-owned manufacturing companies. It has 11 factories in the UK and employs more than 8,000 workers.

However, Mr Bamford said JCB could consider its future in the UK because Labour is “hunting down” family businesses through its inheritance tax raid.

He said: “You want us, as a family, to invest here in Britain.

“You know, we have businesses everywhere around the world. We have them in India and China and Brazil.”

Mr Bamford added: “I’m here because I’m British, and I’m here and I employ people in Britain because I like British people and I like being in my part of the community.”

The levy was first announced by the Chancellor in late 2024, removing family business owners’ ability to pass down assets through generations without having to pay inheritance tax.

As of April, full tax relief will only apply to the first £2.5m of a business’s assets upon the owner’s death, with everything above that threshold subject to a 20pc tax.

This threshold had initially been set at £1m, but was increased after a backlash from rural voters.

Many business owners are concerned that they will be forced to sell off assets or reduce investment to pay the tax.

This has prompted a string of high-profile entrepreneurs to consider leaving Britain as part of a broader wealth exodus, driven also by Ms Reeves’s decision to abolish non-dom status.

Some have already left since Labour came to power, including Alan Howard, a billionaire hedge fund manager, John Fredriksen, a shipping magnate, and Lakshmi Mittal, a steel tycoon.

Speaking to The Telegraph last year, Sir Rocco Forte, the hotelier, said the inheritance tax changes were “one of the main causes for the exodus”.

A Treasury spokesman said: “We’ve listened and raised the relief threshold to £2.5m to protect more small family businesses, while ensuring the largest make a fair contribution so we can deliver support for families and businesses, including cutting the cost of living.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: The affordable homes rule fuelling Britain’s housing crisis
data.description:
It may have been designed with the best of intentions, but a decades-old policy to boost social housing has been blamed for strangling Britain’s property market.

Housebuilders are required by the Government to contribute affordable homes to secure planning consent for new projects.

Such homes are built for lower-income families and priced below local market rates, meaning less profit for the housebuilder. 

Some view the rule, called section 106  of the Town and Country Planning Act, as a vital safety net to offer low-income households a route into the property market. Others fear it is killing off housebuilding across Britain when it is needed most.

According to the most pessimistic estimates, affordable housing requirements have delayed more than 45,000 homes across the country.

Meanwhile, the Adam Smith Institute, a think tank, estimates that section 106 has cost builders £1.3bn in the past year alone, based on the grants they are losing out on and the cost of building the homes.

That is the equivalent of £13,000 for every open-market home built by a private developer.

Andrew Dawber, a founding partner of social housing developer Civitas Investment Management and a senior fellow at the think tank, says the system has become a brake on development of all kinds.

“Very few houses are getting started, of all descriptions: open market houses, affordable houses,” he adds.

Scrutiny of social housing delivery comes at a crucial time for the country as Labour appears increasingly unlikely to deliver on its pledge to build 1.5 million homes by 2030.

Despite this, the Government has so far held firm on retaining section 106.

A spokesman for the Ministry of Housing, Communities and Local Government said: “We need more affordable homes and section 106 agreements help to get spades in the ground on housing developments, delivering more than 20,000 new affordable homes last year.

“We are introducing reforms to improve the section 106 system so it is simpler and more transparent and we can get on and build the homes and infrastructure this country desperately needs.”

Its stance has prompted a renewed dispute over whether the policy is doing more harm than good.

Sir James Cleverly, the shadow housing secretary, says: “Excessive section 106 requirements, as we have seen in London under Labour, make development unviable.

“Angela Rayner’s legacy means similar demands are being imposed across England. It all means less regeneration and less affordable housing as nothing ends up being built.”

When councils approve new private housing, it is usually granted on condition of a section 106 deal, requiring builders to provide a certain number of affordable homes on their developments. These are then typically sold to housing associations to operate.

However, small housebuilders in particular are struggling to get affordable homes built because they can’t find housing associations willing to take them on.

While housing associations are non-profit organisations, their finances are under pressure from capped social rents, limited government grants and rising maintenance costs.

One developer, who does not wish to be named, tells The Telegraph that his business has secured permission to build homes in the South West, a quarter of them affordable. The builder has approached 25 housing associations but has not received any responses.

This has left the whole site on hold despite years of pouring money into the planning process, leaving tradesmen without work and potential buyers in limbo.

James Hodgkinson, a researcher at the Adam Smith Institute, says: “It’s a system that paralyses development and critically, nobody wins.”

The boss of another medium-sized housebuilder, who does not wish to be named, describes how his team has had to start several sites without a housing association on board, posing a significant financial threat.

“We are getting extremely low offers when we are able to secure a housing association offer,” he says. “This is further denting margins and putting some developments in a loss-making position.

He claims this, alongside tax rises and growing policy costs, is pushing smaller housebuilders to “the verge of collapse”.

A failing model

Introduced in 1990 in the final year of Margaret Thatcher’s administration, section 106 rules evolved over the years and were significantly expanded by Tony Blair’s government.

At that point, section 106 was viewed as an attractive way to compel the private sector to put more cash into affordable homes.

By the time the Conservative-Liberal Democrat coalition came into power in 2010, the rule had become firmly embedded in the planning process.

What had once accounted for under 10pc of affordable housing delivery had grown to represent nearly half of all affordable homes built in the UK by the early 2020s, says Dawber.

The shift represented a fundamental change in housing policy, moving from direct investment to a system that relies more heavily on the health of the private market.

But that dependence, critics argue, has become the problem. With the open market buckling under mounting taxes, high interest rates and soaring build costs in recent years, section 106 is also suffering.

Government data published in November show that in 2024-25, 36pc of all new affordable homes delivered in England were bankrolled by section 106, down from 45pc in the previous year and a peak of 51pc in 2019-20.

This marked its lowest share since 2014-15.

How to fix it

The Adam Smith Institute believes the rule should be scrapped and replaced with grant funding through Homes England, the Government’s central housing agency.

“Section 106 is a lever that could be pulled quite easily and there’s a big amount of housing that would be unlocked,” says Hodgkinson.

Emma Ramell, at the Home Builders Federation, says the system is under serious strain but stops short of endorsing abolition.

That is despite acknowledging that “the model has increasingly failed” in recent years, which has led to developments stalling and “much-needed affordable homes sitting empty”.

Ian McDermott, the chief executive of Peabody and chairman of G15, which acts on behalf of London housing associations, acknowledges that the system “isn’t currently working” but rejects the idea of scrapping it entirely.

“The suggestion that section 106 is a tax on development misses both its purpose and its value,” he says, arguing that the policy must be made to “work better” rather than scrapped.

For McDermott, the system is faltering because housing associations are asked to buy homes after key decisions are made, increasing costs down the line.

He believes closer collaboration between developers and housing associations would ensure such homes are fit for purpose.

But Hodgkinson argues a return to the grant model would encourage more private capital into the affordable homes market.

“Incentivise developers to build affordable homes where they are needed and where they are most cost-efficient through flat grants,” he says.

“We also want to drastically expand the eligibility criteria because developers can and will probably want to build these affordable homes, but private equity or housing associations might also wish to get in on the action.”

Labour has shown little appetite for the type of structural overhaul the think tank is proposing, with a stated focus on long-term reform for the section 106 market.

But with housing starts at a fraction of what is needed and Labour’s 1.5 million homes pledge looking further out of reach, the pressure to find more levers to pull will not dissipate any time soon.

data.title: Britain’s biggest housebuilder may replace bricks with wood as prices soar
data.description:
Britain’s biggest housebuilder could turn to wood instead of bricks for new homes as the war in Iran pushes up construction costs.

Barratt Redrow, which built more than 16,000 new homes last year, said it was considering using more timber if bricks and other heavy materials became too expensive.

Builders across the country brace for a surge in costs triggered by the war in Iran. John Newcomb, the chief executive of the Builders Merchants Federation, said he was hearing of price increases of up to 20pc on some materials.

Bricks are fired in energy-intensive kilns, often powered by gas, which leaves them vulnerable to an Iran-driven price shock. European gas prices have nearly trebled since the conflict began. Steel and cement are also expected to increase in price.

Barratt Redrow said it expects build-cost inflation to average 2pc in the 12 months to June but warned that “higher energy costs are likely to be reflected in increased building material costs” in the following year.

“Our ability to switch from traditional to timber frame construction ... provides us with further flexibility should we see significant movements in our traditional construction input costs,” the company stated on Wednesday.

The use of timber frames in construction is thought to date back to the late Middle Ages, reaching a peak in the early 17th century. Timber structures fell out of fashion in the UK after the Great Fire of London of 1666. Brick was the dominant material used in the Victorian era – a period of significant housing expansion in industrial cities.

Barratt Redrow owns a timber-frame business called Oregon, which would allow it to scale up production. Using timber to build homes requires fewer bricks and metals.

In the same update, Barratt Redrow slashed its target for buying land for housebuilding projects. The builder said it expects to approve between 7,000 and 9,000 plots for purchase this year, down from previous guidance of 10,000 and 12,000. This will save up to £200m of cash.

“With a less certain backdrop, given recent geopolitical events and their likely impact on mortgage rates and build-cost inflation, we are being even more selective,” the housebuilder said.

“The ongoing conflict in the Middle East is contributing to increased economic uncertainty, including the potential for a more prolonged higher interest rate environment and renewed cost pressures.”

The announcement comes a week after rival Berkeley Group halted new land purchase and scaled back investment in its building projects, saying it was becoming impossible to build in London because of onerous regulations and soaring costs.

data.title: Losses hit £53m at The Sun as it battles falling tabloid sales
data.description:
The Sun has reported a £53m loss as Rupert Murdoch’s newspaper battles falling tabloid sales.

Newly filed accounts for News Group Newspapers (NGN), which publishes The Sun and Sun on Sunday, show pre-tax losses widened from £18m in the year to July 2025.

It came as turnover fell by 7pc to £273m, which bosses blamed on a drop in print sales and algorithm changes that dented online readership.

The figures underscore the challenge facing The Sun, which has been grappling with how to restore profitability across its advertising-funded business model.

The Sun introduced a premium model last year, offering readers access to exclusive stories and columns by star writers such as Jeremy Clarkson for £2 a month.

The move, which mirrored a similar partial paywall launched by MailOnline, was aimed at reducing the title’s reliance on dwindling ad revenues.

However, the rollout of AI tools in Google’s search engine is posing a fresh threat to free-to-access online journalism, with a number of publishers already reporting sharp reductions in traffic.

The tabloid has also sought to offset declining readership by expanding in the US, while it has also made redundancies to cut costs.

The publisher employed 530 jobs at the end of the last financial year, down from 552 a year previously.

The Sun reported increased legal costs related to the phone-hacking scandal at The News of the World, which more than doubled last year to £36.7m.

This is thought to include a £10m settlement with the Duke of Sussex, which was struck with NGN last year. The publisher issued an apology for “serious intrusion” by The Sun and phone hacking by private investigators working for the News of the World.

The troubles at The Sun stand in contrast to Mr Murdoch’s broadsheet titles, which operate under a subscription model.

Revenues at The Times and Sunday Times increased to £390m, driven by growth in subscribers and advertising. The newspaper has been seeking to boost reader numbers by reducing the total number of stories it publishes, in a strategy it claims should result in “better stories”.

Decline of TalkTV

Separate accounts showed Mr Murdoch had lost more than £150m on his failed news channel TalkTV in a rare setback for the media mogul.

TalkTV, the opinionated broadcaster that was pulled off air two years ago, posted an operating loss of £8.7m in the year to July 2025.

It takes cumulative losses since the channel’s launch in 2022 to more than £150m. Mr Murdoch’s News Corp also booked a £25m writedown on its investments in the UK, which it said was in response to challenging market conditions affecting its broadcasting operations.

A £154m loan to TalkTV has been waived and the channel’s parent company has now ceased trading.

The demise of TalkTV marks a rare defeat for the nonagenarian tycoon, who last year handed control of his news empire to his eldest son, Lachlan Murdoch, after a bitter succession battle.

The deal will secure the Right-wing stance of Mr Murdoch’s outlets, which include Fox and the Wall Street Journal as well as his UK newspapers. Lachlan is closely aligned with his father’s politics, whereas siblings James, Elisabeth and Prudence are viewed as more liberal.

Mr Murdoch was separately handed a boost this week as a US judge dismissed Donald Trump’s $10bn (£7.4bn) lawsuit against The Wall Street Journal for publishing a lewd birthday note sent by the president to Jeffrey Epstein.

A spokesman for News UK said: “News UK is a portfolio of assets, each at a different level of digital maturity. 

“Those brands are managed as a whole and our investments are focused on formats which will deliver revenues over the long term to create a sustainable future for all of our journalism.”

data.title: Labour blames OpenAI’s cash struggles for data centre cancellation
data.description:
A Labour minister has claimed OpenAI’s “challenging” financial situation is behind the decision to halt data centre investments in the UK, rejecting claims that high energy costs are to blame.

Kanishka Narayan, the AI minister, said that the ChatGPT developer dropped out of the project, called Stargate UK, because of its “internal financing context”.

This marks a split with OpenAI, which singled out the cost of energy when pulling the plug on an AI data centre in the North East last week.

The AI lab said it would revisit the plans, announced in September, when “the right conditions, such as regulation and the cost of energy, enable long-term infrastructure investment”.

However, Mr Narayan has now appeared to reject OpenAI’s comments.

Speaking on The Rest is Money podcast, he said there had been “no change to energy pricing, and there has been no change to the regulatory environment”.

“What has changed is, as is well publicised, OpenAI’s internal financing context has become more challenging and they have made similar calls in the United States with their programme there as well,” he said.

Britain has the highest industrial electricity prices in the developed world, although UK energy costs have fallen slightly since OpenAI unveiled plans for Stargate UK.

Data centres, and particularly AI systems, are energy-intensive, making them vulnerable to high electricity costs.

OpenAI confirmed this month that it has raised $122bn (£97bn) in new funding and the business is believed to be targeting a $1tn public float as soon as this year.

But it also burned through an estimated $14bn in 2025. This has led the company to cut back on costly projects, such as scrapping Sora, its expensive AI video app.

OpenAI, which is valued at $830bn, revealed its $500bn Stargate data centre investment plan at a White House press conference in January last year.

The project has since been beset by delays, while OpenAI has retreated from some of its other data centre ventures.

In Norway, OpenAI pulled out of another data project this week. It confirmed it would no longer rent AI computing power directly from the 100pc clean energy data site and instead lease capacity from Microsoft.

Since December, OpenAI’s international expansion efforts have been led by George Osborne, the former Conservative chancellor.

In the US, OpenAI also cancelled an expansion plan for its primary data facility in Abilene, Texas.

OpenAI declined to comment.
