data.title: The collapse of Starmer’s housebuilding revolution
data.description:
For Weston Homes, a mid-sized property developer in London’s commuter belt, Sir Keir Starmer’s election victory was supposed to be a godsend.

The Labour leader had promised to “bulldoze” England’s planning system and unlock a housebuilding revolution, staking his reputation on building an unprecedented 1.5 million homes by 2030.

But almost two years on, something has gone badly wrong. Before Sir Keir was elected, Weston Homes was building 1,000 new homes per year. This has now plunged to 600.

“Our output was probably last that low at the back end of the global financial crisis,” said Bob Weston, the company’s chairman.

“The UK might not be in a technical economic recession, but we are, without question, in a housing recession.

“Everybody is scaling back. Construction industry jobs are falling like flies. Pretty much every construction site in the UK is on go-slow.”

Weston says the outlook for housebuilders is the worst he has seen in his 50-year career.

At a national level, the data tells an alarming tale.

Despite Labour’s promises, the number of new homes built last year fell to a decade-low.

Even more worryingly, the number of homes securing planning consent tumbled by 27pc to levels last seen in the early 2010s.

Campaign group Britain Remade estimates that, based on the current pipeline and average build times, England is on track to deliver just 706,000 new homes over this parliament.

This represents just 47pc of Labour’s target and 5pc less than the number of new homes delivered under the previous government.

In London, analysts expect the lowest annual building rates in the post-Second World War era.

A range of problems is underlying this latest crunch, including time.

Although Labour has made a series of structural planning reforms widely commended by industry, including scope to unlock swathes of land, it will be years before the benefits are realised.

It is also the sad reality that planning was only ever part of the problem.

Elsewhere, developers are burdened by red tape and too many people still cannot afford to buy new-build homes.

The war in Iran has also now unleashed more turmoil, as soaring energy prices and elevated borrowing costs are bound to increase construction costs and dent demand.

Ultimately, it appears Sir Keir’s housebuilding pledge is dead in the water.

Housing boom on paper only

To its credit, Labour acted swiftly to tackle the planning system.

Within days of the election in July 2024, Rachel Reeves, the Chancellor, announced that mandatory housing targets would be reimposed on local authorities.

By the end of the month, Labour had published a draft national planning policy framework (NPPF) that increased the number of homes councils were expected to approve.

It also created a “grey belt” designation to unlock development on protected green belt land. Data company LandTech estimated that this could open up space for up to four million new homes.

A new draft NPPF published last December also outlined plans to encourage development in the “train belt”.

The idea being that if developers submit plans to build projects of a certain density within 800 metres of major railway stations, they should be approved by default. Lichfields planning consultants expect that this will unlock land to develop 633,000 homes.

This radical policy shift has been effective.

Local authorities are allocating sites for housing quicker, while also prompting conversations with housebuilders about increasing development, says Mark Adams, the executive chairman of Pennyfarthing, a housebuilder in the South of England.

Because planning applications are now much more likely to get waved through, Britain’s builders are also submitting more of them.

A senior source at a major housebuilder says their firm has doubled planning applications made on sites in their long-term land portfolio as a result of these reforms.

“Two years ago, we were progressing with planning applications for about 33pc of our portfolio,” they said. “Now it’s about 66pc. So that’s a 100pc increase.

“In the quarter of a century I’ve been doing this, there’s been nothing like as positive a planning system as we’ve got now.”

But this does not mean more homes are actually getting built, because council planners remain woefully under-resourced. That is alongside frequent opposition from local Nimbys (not in my back yard).

This has sparked a planning backlog as applications for major sites are taking two or three years to be approved.

The number of homes gaining full planning permission fell in every single quarter last year, according to Savills.

Across 2025, just 180,000 properties were approved for development. Not only was this the lowest level in more than 20 years, but it was also down by a quarter from the year before Labour won the election.

In London, the number of homes getting planning consent more than halved year on year.

Stuck in the doldrums

According to Simon Coop, of Lichfields planning consultants, there has been no real change in the way that local authorities approach planning decisions.

“Nearly two years into the parliamentary term, you’re getting the sense that nothing’s changing,” he says.

Ironically, the new surge in planning applications may have made things worse, says Greg Hill, of The Hill Group, a housebuilder in London and the South.

“The system remains very challenging, very slow,” he says. “Because there are more applications in the system, it puts more pressure on the system. So there’s planning activity but not housing delivery activity.”

In 2024, Labour announced it would employ 300 new planning officers, but this has so far made little difference.

This week, the British Property Federation (BPF) warned that it will take an additional 3,000 officers to give local authorities the manpower needed to manage their workloads.

Low levels of planning approvals mean just 3.4pc of the population live in local authorities which are on track to meet their new housing targets, according to Britain Remade.

Savills’ analysis shows that in London, which has a housing target of 88,000 per year, the number of homes that secured planning consent last year was little more than 20,000.

The national pipeline is also dismal. In 2025, just 202,800 new homes were completed, the lowest total in any calendar year since 2015, including the pandemic.

This was down 3pc year on year, 18pc less than in 2019 and nearly a third short of the Government’s annual target of building 300,000 homes per year.

Starts on new homes has climbed since Labour took office, but they are still in the doldrums.

Office for National Statistics (ONS) data show that work started on 118,000 new homes in England in the 12 months to September.

Red tape burden

This was up by 30pc compared to the 12 months before the 2024 election. But excluding that period, it was the lowest 12-month total since 2013.

James Moody, at London property developer Seven Capital, says: “Their manifesto pledge has failed. That is the honest answer.”

Even Steve Reed, the Housing Secretary, acknowledged the crisis last month by warning that a lack of new homes meant young people were living with their parents into their 40s.

“It’s not acceptable,” he said. “The opportunities that were open to my generation, when we were younger, are closed off to them.”

One key factor Sir Keir seems to have overlooked is that planning is only part of the problem.

While Labour has done a good job of reducing planning hurdles, it has not addressed the regulatory burdens on construction, which are growing, says Sam Dumitriu, at Britain Remade, an economic think tank.

For decades, housebuilders have had to make large financial contributions to the local areas where they are building.

From 2010, councils have been able to charge developers for new infrastructure under the Community Infrastructure Levy (CIL).

This came on top of pre-existing requirements for housebuilders to make contributions to affordable housing under Section 106 of the Town and Country Planning Act.

When Sir Sadiq Khan was elected Mayor of London in 2016, he set a requirement that 35pc of new housing developments should be “affordable” homes, which are sold at below market rates.

The 2017 Grenfell fire, which killed 72 people, triggered a new wave of building safety regulations.

From April 2022, large builders have had to pay a Residential Property Developer Tax (RPDT) to fund the country’s cladding remediation bill.

In the same year, requirements were introduced for the new Building Safety Regulator to approve plans for high-rise developments.

This process takes an average of nine months to give sign-off and has so far seen 69pc of pre-construction applications get rejected, according to Britain Remade.

From October 2026, developers will also have to pay the building safety levy, another tax to pay for cladding remediation.

At the same time, environmental rules have tightened dramatically.

Self-storage ‘more valuable’

In 2019, the Government introduced “nutrient neutrality” rules that mean housing developments must not increase soil pollutants in protected areas.

The Future Homes Standard, which will come into effect in March 2027, will also require new homes to be fitted with low-carbon forms of heating.

“There isn’t anyone who does what we do who hasn’t got a long list of examples of sites where they’re held up due to red tape, bureaucracy, regulations and other items,” says Hill. “We have a litany of examples.”

Mandatory timelines for decision-making are rarely adhered to, he adds. “We’re perpetually in delay on everything we do.”

A new report by the Home Builders Federation (HBF) being published next week is expected to show that in the last five years, the cost of building a new home has increased by more than £70,000 because of new taxes, regulations and build cost inflation.

Zoopla estimated last year that half of the country was no longer viable to develop.

At Seven Capital’s 100 Kensington development, a 462-home project under construction in west London, the company is paying nearly £70m in developer taxes and community contributions at the site, says Moody.

This is about a third of the project’s total build costs, which are roughly double what they would have been a decade ago, he says.

“The planning tax is about £60m more than we will make, and that is before I pay tax on any profits,” says Moody.

“I’ve been doing this for 30 years and I’ve been through a number of recessions. I think this is the most difficult set of circumstances I’ve ever operated in. Because we live in an environment full of regulators, not facilitators.”

Seven Capital has another 178-home development in Islington, which has been in the planning process since 2021.

Since Seven Capital logged the planning application, inflation and new regulations have increased the cost of building the site by about 30pc, says Moody. Sale prices, however, have not moved.

“It is probably more valuable as a self-storage site than a housing site.”

Jon Stoddart, co-head of planning at CBRE, says: “It’s just a very heavily taxed industry. Whilst the Government has had some really good policy initiatives, there still seems to be a fundamental issue around the viability of development.”

London falling

These issues are most acute in London – partly because the capital has a higher density of tall buildings subject to new building safety regulations, but also because there are not enough buyers who can afford to pay for what the builders are selling.

The average home in the capital costs 10.6 times a typical person’s earnings. Technically, this ratio has eased from its 2021 peak of 12.9, but because interest rates have climbed since Covid, mortgage costs are actually far higher.

Based on current rates, the monthly mortgage bill for a buyer purchasing an average London home with a 10pc deposit is now £3,000, up from £2,000 in 2021. Zoopla calculated last year that a buyer needs to earn more than £100,000 per year to afford to buy a property in the capital.

“The biggest thing for housebuilding at the moment, aside from the planning, is demand,” a source at a major housebuilder says. “People need to be able to afford to buy. That side of the coin has been neglected.”

Squeezed affordability matters even more for developers now because of the loss of the Help to Buy equity loan scheme, which ran from 2013 until 2023.

Nationally, this supported around 40,000 new build sales per year, by providing first-time buyers purchasing new homes with 20pc government-backed equity loans that were interest-free for the first five years.

“First-time buyers are in the worst situation they’ve been in for decades,” says Weston.

Before the pandemic, first-time buyers made up 70pc of Weston Homes’ buyers. Now, they purchase only 30pc of the homes that Weston sells.

The Help-to-Buy crutch was removed at the same time that the Government was pushing a buy-to-let tax crackdown, which has eroded the business case for private rental investment in the capital.

Changes to anti-money laundering rules, falling prices and a new stamp duty surcharge for overseas buyers in 2021 have also hit demand among foreign investors.

New-build developers were heavily reliant on these two buyer groups to purchase new homes off plan, which was a vital source of cash flow during development, says James Barton, joint head of London Land Agency at Knight Frank. Now these buyers have dried up.

“Developers can deal with two or three headwinds, but at the moment they have a perfect storm of eight or nine issues that they can’t control,” says Barton.

London is now at the epicentre of Britain’s housebuilding drought. Private housing starts in London fell by half in 2025 to just 5,500, according to data company Molior. This was 83pc less than in 2015.

Based on the capital’s current pipeline, Molior expects just 14,053 homes will be completed across 2027 and 2028, just 8pc of the Government’s two-year London housing target of 176,000.

This would be the first time since 1946 that London has completed no more than 10,000 homes a year.

And what is in the pipeline is being put on hold. There are currently 893 planning permissions for unbuilt schemes in London, according to Molior. In the last 12 months, construction started on just 73 of these.

“More than 90pc of permissions just aren’t going anywhere,” says Tim Craine, Molior’s founder.

Ben Hopkinson, at the Centre for Policy Studies, says: “It’s very, very bad. London’s facing its strongest headwinds for housebuilding since the Second World War.

“Starts are down that much that, if something drastic doesn’t happen, we’re set for record low levels of housebuilding.”

In October, Sir Sadiq and Reed announced a package of emergency measures to boost London housebuilding, which included reducing the affordable homes requirement from 35pc to 20pc. However, analysts warn it is not enough to shift the dial.

At the start of April, London-focused housebuilder Berkeley Group said it was halting land purchases because of “unprecedented” increases in costs and regulation.

“We have ended up in a situation where the place where we need to build homes the most has become the place where it’s hardest to build homes. It’s a real disaster,” says Dumitriu.

Impact of war in Iran

Now, the war in Iran has made a bad situation even worse.

“Seven weeks ago, things were genuinely improving,” says an industry source. “Mortgage affordability was improving and you were seeing an improvement in sales rates. Then Iran happened.”

Iran’s blockade of the Strait of Hormuz has sent oil prices soaring past $100 per barrel, reviving inflation fears and driving up government borrowing costs. Mortgage rates have jumped as traders price in the prospect of Bank of England interest rate rises.

The average rate on a two-year fixed-rate mortgage has climbed from 4.83pc at the start of March to 5.89pc, according to Moneyfacts. For a buyer taking out a typical £200,000 home loan, this has added £3,024 in interest payments to the cost of the loan over two years.

The blow to buyer demand is significant. Adams, of Pennyfarthing, says the number of buyers visiting the company’s new-build sites has plunged by 50pc since the war began.

“There’s this lack of confidence because of what’s going on in the UK and the wider world,” says Adams. “People can see where mortgage rates are going and they are looking over their shoulder at energy prices and the general cost of living.”

Pennyfarthing has contractors lined up at a site in the South of England, which has planning permission for 340 homes.

However, it has now put the development on hold, in large part resulting from fears over falling demand. “We’ve had it on a rolling review for the last four weeks,” says Adams.

The other big looming threat is rising construction costs, a source at a housebuilder warns: “The longer the war goes on, the worse the impact gets.”

UK construction costs in February were already 42pc higher than in January 2020, after the pandemic supply chain crisis and inflation wave.

Soon, higher oil prices will flow through to higher costs of bricks, steel, concrete and other energy-intensive construction products.

Housebuilders face a “perfect storm” in the second half of this year as they brace for a double whammy of elevated costs and lower demand, warns Noble Francis, chief economist at the Construction Products Association (CPA).

Consequently, the listed UK housebuilder stocks have tanked. Barratt Redrow, Berkeley Group, Vistry and Taylor Wimpey have dropped by 32pc, 14pc, 47pc and 21pc respectively so far this year.

Whitehall sources have also been warning that the war jeopardises the Government’s target to build 1.5 million homes.

For Sir Keir, failing to deliver on housebuilding means not only an acute shortage of homes but also the death of Labour’s promise to secure the highest sustained growth rate in the G7.

New housing creates growth partly through construction sector jobs and profits, but also by reducing rents and enabling the workforce to be able to move to where the best jobs are, which boosts productivity.

“If Labour don’t have that growth story from housing, then where is the growth going to come from?” says Dumitriu.

“A failure to get a grip on this housing issue by the end of this parliament means they’re going to be in real trouble.”

A spokesman for the Housing Department said: “We will build the homes this country needs.

“We’ve overhauled the planning system and invested a record £39bn in social and affordable housing to help developers get spades in the ground and weather any geopolitical pressure.

“This is alongside emergency measures in London to unblock dozens of stalled sites and build thousands more homes.”

data.title: Sadiq Khan could wage war on ‘Chelsea tractor’ owners with new charges
data.description:
Sir Sadiq Khan is considering waging a war on “Chelsea tractor” owners with new charges, a new document suggests.

Transport for London’s (TfL) Vision Zero action plan claimed there was a “pressing need for targeted safety interventions” against larger vehicles.

It stated the size of large SUVs – nicknamed “Chelsea tractors” – made roads “more hazardous for everybody outside the vehicle” because they “significantly increase the severity of collisions”.

The plan is part of Sir Sadiq’s efforts to eliminate deaths and serious injuries on London’s roads by 2041.

There is no specific definition of an SUV, but it is a term given to vehicles that are generally taller, wider, heavier and less fuel-efficient than traditional cars. Critics have pointed out that electric vehicles (EVs) are just as heavy, but would not be affected by the plans.

Thomas Turrell, a member of the London Assembly, said: “This family car tax is a double standard. The mayor will not go after his precious heavy EV cars with this regressive, backwards tax, instead choosing to hammer families.

“His anti-car zealotry will not stop here. If he truly believes vehicles should be taxed for being heavier, he should be consistent rather than money-grabbing.”

Mike Hawes, the chief executive of the Society of Motor Manufacturers and Traders, said all cars sold in the UK “regardless of size” must meet “exacting safety and pedestrian protection standards”.

He added: “Singling out specific cars based on size restricts consumer choice and would unfairly penalise the many drivers who require a larger vehicle for essential mobility.”

No proposals about possible charges for SUVs have been put forward yet.

SUVs account for about a third of all new cars sold in the UK. They have grown in popularity in recent years, with many drivers favouring their higher seating position.

The Vision Zero document claimed “oversized” vehicles such as SUVs made it “harder for drivers to see people walking and cycling nearby”, adding that children were “more easily hidden from a driver’s view”.

It stated that in Britain, SUVs were 14 per cent more likely to kill pedestrians and cyclists than other passenger cars if there was a collision, and 77 per cent more likely to kill children.

Punishing London’s motorists

Sir Sadiq expanded the ultra-low-emission zone to cover the whole of London in August 2023, which requires drivers of vehicles that do not meet emissions standards to pay a daily fee of £12.50.

London also has an £18 congestion charge for driving in a zone in the centre of the capital at certain times.

Sir Sadiq has faced sustained criticism from ordinary Londoners that he ignores their concerns about schemes that punish motorists.

One such scheme was formally approved this week, which involves the construction of a cycle path at the Holland Park roundabout in west London, one of the capital’s busiest road interchanges.

Residents have repeatedly objected to the £6m scheme and have claimed that TfL has cited false statistics generated to justify the works.

Ruth Saunders, a member of the Save Our Streets group based near the roundabout, said: “This scheme is designed to benefit a small number of commuter cyclists travelling through the borough for a few hours each day, to the detriment of local residents and other road users.”

Regarding the potential plans to tax SUVs, a spokesman for Sir Sadiq claimed: “There is growing evidence about the safety risks of large SUVs, which are becoming more common on London’s roads.

“The mayor has asked TfL to undertake detailed analysis of the safety risks posed by large SUVs and their wider impact on London’s roads.

“This could then inform any future policy proposals in London or recommendations to Government or the sector.

“As the numbers of large SUVs increase on London’s roads, it is right that TfL consider their impact on safety and how they affect other road users.”

data.title: Net zero zealots are putting Gen Z off building planes
data.description:
Britain’s aerospace companies are gearing up for their biggest boom in a generation.

Record airliner orders will require plane makers and their suppliers to lift production by a fifth in the next few years, creating well-paid jobs for thousands of workers.

Yet a UK aerospace industry that produced the jet engine, the Harrier jump jet and Concorde is being hampered by a growing recruitment crisis.

Years of demonisation by green campaigners such as Greta Thunberg for its inability to wean itself off fossil fuels are beginning to take their toll as young people turn away from careers in industry.

A lack of inspirational new aircraft and the allure of rival jobs in technology and AI are compounding the situation, leaving even the biggest firms struggling to hire.

Airbus, which employs 15,000 people in the UK, is resorting to converting one of its Beluga wing transporters into a recruitment centre in an effort to bolster its appeal to Gen Z.

The firm will fit the plane with classrooms and a mock-up of its assembly line as it seeks to recruit hundreds of apprentices a year to the Broughton wing plant in North Wales.

Bosses are hoping that Nasa’s Artemis programme to return humans to the Moon by 2028 will also help restore some of the wider industry’s lost glamour.

Richard Gardner, the honorary president of Farnborough Air Sciences Trust, said Gen Z had been turned off aviation by eco-zealots who wrongly condemn the sector for its environmental impact.

He suggested the problem starts in school, where children are increasingly taught about the impacts of climate change.

He said: “We talk about the word brainwashing, which is quite emotive for some people, but that is what it is.

“The teaching unions are very influential in terms of the way that profession is going, and they are pretty green in their tendencies.”

Gardner added: “I think they mostly buy into these green policies, science and economics teachers aside, and that aerospace and defence are therefore not high in their career recommendations.”

He said the recent rise of the Green Party as an electoral force is also a “big worry” for aviation.

Green policies include a commitment to banning domestic flights for journeys that could be made by train, halting airport expansion and imposing a tax on frequent travellers.

Kevin Craven, the chief executive of ADS, the trade group for aerospace, defence and space firms, said that while the industry might not be decarbonising fast enough for some young people, its focus on reducing emissions may be failing to motivate others.

He said: “There is a feeling that aerospace has faded into the background a bit because there are no longer any mountains being climbed.

“Concorde, the Comet, the jet engine and the Harrier were all British firsts that really excited and inspired people.”

Craven added: “All new aircraft are really a cylinder with wings and look approximately the same. There are incremental advances which are huge for sustainability, but it’s hard to see that progression.

“There is also huge competition from other high-tech arenas – particularly AI – and, more generally, the City, where vast swathes of money are available to the best and brightest.”

Mr Craven said it was galling to see tech companies recruiting the most able engineers, even though their electricity consumption is set to double by 2030, creating clear parallels with the net zero challenges facing aviation.

He said: “When you look at cloud computing, on the other side of it is a dirty big data centre requiring huge amounts of power and huge amounts of cooling.”

‘Incredibly competitive world’

Sue Partridge, the UK country manager for Airbus, said the world’s biggest plane maker had turned to initiatives such as the Beluga recruitment project as it prepares to deliver 1,000 jet planes a year as early as 2027, up from 793 in 2025.

Airbus’s Filton, Gloucestershire, wing design and development centre near Bristol faces competition for A-level students and graduate recruits from a broad spectrum of rival employers, she said.

The firm, which has trained 4,000 British recruits in the past decade, also faces a fight to hire 16-year-old apprentices for its assembly line in Broughton, Flintshire, with Merseyside’s carmakers and the cluster of green energy firms on nearby Deeside, Wales.

She said: “It’s an incredibly competitive world out there, and we need to do everything we can to attract talent and secure those skills we are going to need for the future.

“But it is difficult, particularly in newer digital skill areas, where we are competing with Google and companies like that, which was never the case in the past.”

Partridge added: “With the younger cohort, the challenge is attracting them to the industry in the first place and inspiring them so that they want to actually build aeroplanes. They get influenced in their career choice at an early age.

“A few years ago, all the stories were about flight shaming, and aerospace was the bad guy. That’s why we want to get into schools so they really connect with Airbus and aerospace manufacturing.”

The Beluga, named after the white whale whose bloated fuselage it resembles, garners rare attention for a modern plane and would be a formidable tool in encouraging take-up of Stem (science, technology, engineering and mathematics) subjects, Ms Partridge said.

“There are still inspirational stories we can tell, and I think the Beluga is one,” she said. “We know people love the plane, and we can use that as a symbol.

“It’s an iconic aircraft, and if you see one in the sky, for me, it’s just as impactful as Concorde used to be. We already have a Concorde near Filton, South Gloucestershire, and we can use this in the same way.”

Mr Craven said the development of so-called flying taxis, in which Britain is playing a leading role, is one area that should resonate with younger people.

That is largely “because they will be silent, and there won’t be obvious pollutants pouring out of the back”.

However, it is not clear how big the market for the electric vertical take-off craft will be, with some industry observers concerned that they will be limited to whisking the very rich to and from first-class flights, a role hardly likely to endear them to Gen Z.

Industry experts hope that a new era of manned space travel could achieve greater cut-through.

Elon Musk’s SpaceX has made space a “much closer frontier”, while the Artemis missions have the potential to capture hearts and minds just as the Apollo programme did in the 1960s and 1970s, said Mr Craven.

He said: “Aerospace and space are joined at the hip, and whenever we ask an astronaut to an event, there is a frisson of excitement. People want heroes, and that combination of high-tech and courage is the ultimate inspiration.

“I can remember watching the Moon landing on a black and white TV with a 10-inch screen in the middle of Africa. It’s probably why I ended up as an engineer.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: US is best place to invest, says boss of British drugs giant
data.description:
The boss of Britain’s largest drug developer has praised the US as the best place to invest amid fears over the waning appeal of the UK.

Dame Emma Walmsley, the outgoing chief executive of GlaxoSmithKline (GSK), said it was delusional to suggest Britain could become a competitor to the US. She lauded America as key to the group’s future growth.

“It’s more than half of GSK’s global turnover today,” she said of the US. “It’s still the leading market in the world in terms of the launches of new drugs and vaccines. It’s still, probably alongside China … the best market in the world to do business development.

“No one should be deluded that the UK is going to be a massive scale market, a domestic market, but it can be an exporter of innovation in life sciences.”

Dame Emma said GSK, which spends £1bn developing new drugs in the UK every year, is preparing to invest $30bn (£23bn) in the US by the end of this decade.

She is set to leave GSK at the end of the year after eight years in charge.

‘Worst country in Europe’ for drug prices

Her comments are likely to fuel fears about the attractiveness of Britain’s pharma sector after several major drugmakers cancelled UK projects worth billions – amid concerns over NHS drug price rules and pressure from Donald Trump, the US president.

AstraZeneca recently announced it was pausing a planned £200m investment in a Cambridge research facility, while Merck scrapped plans for a £1bn research centre in London and laid off 125 scientists.

In September, Eli Lilly, the American manufacturer of weight loss drug Mounjaro, also said that it was reconsidering plans for Gateway Labs, its London-based hub that provides support for biotech startups.

The UK Government has struggled to revive overseas interest in Britain in recent years, with the number of foreign direct investment projects falling 12pc last year to a record low of 1,375, according to official figures.

High energy costs, recent pressures like the increase in employers’ National Insurance contributions and wider geopolitical uncertainty have all been cited as reasons for declining investment in the UK.

One of the key concerns of Britain’s pharmaceuticals industry has been over NHS drug pricing in the UK. 

After postponing its investment, Dave Ricks, the chief executive of Eli Lilly, said the UK was “probably the worst country in Europe” for drug prices.

The Government agreed to a deal with the US at the start of the month to scrap tariffs on UK drug shipments – in a bid to end the row with Britain’s pharmaceutical industry. 

Dame Emma welcomed the deal as “a step in the right direction” for Britain.

The agreement means the UK will pay more for medicines through the NHS, in return for a guarantee that US import taxes on pharmaceuticals made in Britain will remain at zero for three years.

data.title: Shoplifting surges by 133pc in five years
data.description:
The number of shoplifting offences has risen by 133 per cent in the past five years, official figures show.

The number of thefts reported to police rose from 228,128 in 2020-21 to 530,457 in 2024-25, according to House of Commons Library data analysed by the Liberal Democrats.

However, only 19.83 per cent of offences in 2024-25 resulted in a charge, with wide variations between the 43 police forces in England and Wales.

At the bottom of the table was the Metropolitan Police, with fewer than 7 per cent of recorded cases resulting in a charge.

This was down from 8.64 per cent in 2020-21, when there were 31,008 shoplifting offences reported to the force. Since then, the number of store thefts has tripled to 93,626.

Durham Constabulary brought charges in 32.7 per cent of its shoplifting cases, more than four times the rate of the Met in 2024-25.

It was followed at the top of the table by Humberside (31.9 per cent), Norfolk (30.6 per cent), Cumbria (30.44 per cent) and West Mercia (30.21 per cent).

Marks & Spencer has criticised the failure of the Government and police to get a grip on shoplifting, which the retailer claimed had seen attacks on its stores by thieves become “more brazen, more organised and more aggressive”.

Executives from the store wrote to Sir Sadiq Khan, the Mayor of London, and Shabana Mahmood, the Home Secretary, demanding they do more to address the problem.

The Lib Dems said small businesses “simply cannot afford for this to continue” and called for a return to community policing with dedicated police counters.

‘Our communities deserve better’

Max Wilkinson, the Lib Dems’ home affairs spokesman, said: “Our communities deserve better than the surge of shoplifting that’s ransacking our local high streets. For years, the Conservatives quietly dismantled community policing and this Labour Government has failed to properly restore it.

“Retail workers have, in too many cases, been left to fend for themselves as hundreds of thousands of shoplifters get away scot-free. Our brilliant small businesses simply cannot afford for this to continue.”

He added: “Liberal Democrats have time and again said we need a visible neighbourhood police presence, with staffed police counters in every community and officers on the ground. Only then can we make our high streets safe for shopkeepers and shoppers.”

Labour has committed to ensuring every community has a named contactable officer, and pledged to put 13,000 extra police officers back on the streets by the end of this Parliament.

Its new crime and policing bill also aims to curb rising shoplifting by repealing the “low-value” theft rule, which treats shoplifted items below £200 as minor summary offences. It also introduces a separate offence for assaulting retail workers and new respect orders to ban repeat offenders from town centres.

Earlier this week, the Met’s head of business crime insisted his force was determined to come down hard on shoplifting, having already nearly doubled detection rates, increased arrests of store thieves by 44 per cent and seen a 3.2 per cent drop in offences in London in the past year.

The Lib Dems have also raised concerns that shoplifting is particularly hitting small businesses, which are already under pressure from rising energy bills, business rates and extra costs from the Chancellor’s National Insurance contribution rise.

The party has called for a 5p VAT cut for hospitality businesses and urgent reform of the business rates system to breathe life into high streets.

A Home Office spokesman said: “This Government inherited unacceptable levels of shop theft. Real progress is being made, with the number of people caught and charged rising by 21 per cent.

“Alongside major policing reforms to boost neighbourhood patrols, supported by 13,000 extra neighbourhood officers, we are giving police the powers they need to tackle shop theft, including scrapping the £200 rule that lets too many offenders off the hook.”

data.title: Middle classes risk paying more for their shopping under surge pricing
data.description:
Middle-class households may have to pay more for their shopping as retailers roll out surge pricing, Bank of England economists have warned.

New AI-pricing systems may charge better-off households more in order to subsidise lower prices for poorer families, researchers wrote on the Bank’s blog.

The rise of algorithmic and electronic pricing means retailers are increasingly able to personalise prices for different customers, based on characteristics such as what they can afford or particularly need.

Anthony Savagar, Misa Tanaka and Jagdish Tripathy, all Bank of England economists, wrote: “This may lead to higher price dispersion as individuals with high willingness-to-pay subsidise those with lower willingness-to-pay.”

In practice, this is likely to mean better-off households paying more for items than those who are worse off.

Retailers could charge different prices depending on where a person lives. Research in the US shows that shops there already do this.

Mr Savagar wrote: “With increased availability of big data and computing power, more firms are adopting algorithmic and AI-powered pricing to adjust prices rapidly in response to changing economic conditions over time and across consumers.”

Clare Lombardelli, a senior Bank of England official, warned earlier this month of “concerns about fairness” as surge pricing could soon be coming to supermarkets.

A report in February by think tank Onward found that Britons were already spending £10bn a year on subsidies for people claiming Universal Credit through discounts on services for benefit claimants.

These include reduced broadband tariffs, council tax discounts and prescription charge exemptions, which are subsidised by the people paying the full costs.

Households living on benefits can also get major discounts on experiences such as visits to London Zoo, Westminster Abbey and the Tower of London, where a family of four can get a £107 discount worth 96pc of their ticket price.

The rise of big data and technology such as electronic shelf labels means that retailers are increasingly using AI algorithms to adjust prices rapidly based on supply and demand.

Businesses are also exploring personalised pricing, where they tailor prices to each individual customer, based on their circumstances or interests. In practice, this could mean charging a person more for a band T-shirt if streaming data showed they were one of the group’s biggest fans.

Mr Savagar warned that the rise of electronic and algorithmic systems could also make inflation shocks more intense as prices rise almost immediately.

Price surges could also become steeper because the algorithms could push shops to “raise markups more aggressively when inflation is already high”, he said.

data.title: Labour’s equality ‘obsession’ is driving away best and brightest, says energy boss
data.description:
The boss of one of Britain’s leading start-ups has warned that high taxes and an “obsession” with equality under Labour risk triggering a national brain drain.

Alan Chang, co-founder of Fuse Energy, says attracting the best talent to the UK was already becoming more difficult and that without change more high-earners would leave the country.

He also attacked “excessive” regulation and urged ministers to cut energy bills by scrapping green energy subsidies.

The entrepreneur’s company is one of the fastest-growing energy suppliers, having gone from just 50,000 customers as recently as last year to nearly 300,000 today.

Despite his success, Mr Chang warned that a heavy focus on equality in government and the corporate world was damaging business. He says taxes designed to redistribute wealth were also too high – and risked driving high-earners away as AI transforms the economy.

Speaking to The Telegraph, Mr Chang says: “Government policies are very clear. Benefits are increasingly attractive and taxes are higher.

He says taxes caused “friction” for companies wanting to build, “because it’s increasingly hard to attract people to come to this country. They’re saying, ‘OK, high taxes. Maybe that’s fine, but what do I get in return? If I get excellent public services, maybe it’s OK’.

“But that’s increasingly not the case. We see, for example, engineers who really want to work at Fuse but don’t want to relocate to this country. And I think if this trend of higher taxes and worse public services continues, this is going to get worse.”

The tech entrepreneur – previously a senior executive at digital bank Revolut – has previously described high taxes as “a direct assault on the wealth creators and hard-working contributors who actually pay for the insane welfare bill”.

His comments come as Labour is preparing to introduce a new “socio-economic duty” on public bodies that will require all decisions to aim to reduce inequality in society.

Mr Chang argues that the gap between the most productive workers and the least productive was also set to grow “by orders of magnitude” as a result of AI.

“If you increase the productivity gap, that compensation goes up, the tax also goes up,” he says.

“And unless something changes, the brain drain will get worse.”

‘Get out of the way’

Mr Chang was also highly critical of diversity and inclusion policies championed by organisations such as the Confederation of British Industry, a business lobbying group, which encourage employers to hire more people from different demographics.

By comparison, he says, Fuse had built a diverse workforce anyway by taking a colour-blind approach that focused on skills and outcomes.

“In some corporate environments, people are very obsessive about trying to be ‘inclusive and equal’,” he says.

“There’s not enough focus on results. Ultimately, in a corporate environment, what matters is results.

“Trying to be the best versions of yourself, trying to beat your competitors, trying to do what’s best for the company.

“If you look at Revolut and at Fuse, look at the ethnicity, they are super-diverse. But we didn’t start by saying ‘OK, we need this number of people from ethnicity A, this many of ethnicity B.’

“We focus on finding people with the best skills. It’s a merit-first culture.”

He added: “In the last year or two, I think there has been a shift. More and more people I’m seeing are going back to common sense.”

Mr Chang, who became a millionaire at age 28 thanks to his stake in Revolut, founded Fuse in 2022 with Charles Orr.

The company is positioning itself as a challenger energy supplier that aims to offer cheap deals using cutting-edge software and by building and operating its own solar farms. It is following a path successfully taken previously by Octopus, which is now the UK’s biggest energy provider.

As well as operating its own solar farms, Fuse is also planning to begin selling “plug-in” solar panels later this year that households can easily place on balconies or other outdoor areas.

Mr Chang says the company “doesn’t need government support” and instead urged ministers to “get out of the way”.

“The Government can focus on getting the basics correct, that’s all we need,” he says. “We don’t ask for more.”

However, he warned that a barrage of regulation was making life harder for businesses.

“No one is arguing that all regulation is bad,” he added. “But at the same time, we’re seeing more and more regulations being added every year but very little to none being removed.

“You need more and more people just to monitor the regulations to make sure the company complies with it all. All this adds up and becomes a cost of business. There’s excessive regulation.”

Business groups have raised concerns about the barrage of new rules introduced under Labour through the passage of the Employment Rights Act, which greatly empowers unions and introduces controversial new rights such as day-one sick pay and paternity leave.

‘We don’t need subsidies’

Beyond simply making business more dynamic, Mr Chang said slashing regulation could help to bring down energy bills at a time when the crisis in the Middle East threatens to drive them up.

He said there were “very addressable” ways to bring down bills, including by easing planning rules so that solar farms could be built more cheaply.

Green energy subsidies, including contracts for difference, would be unnecessary if building could be made cheaper, he said.

“If you can solve that, we don’t need subsidies. It’s pretty insane, right? Basically, we’ve made things super expensive to build so you have to add a subsidy on top to incentivise the private sector to build. That’s not a smart way to do it.

“What you want to do is make it low-cost to build – and then delete the subsidies.”

He also called for more North Sea drilling, the construction of new nuclear power plants and the removal of carbon taxes.

“Drilling in the North Sea, that’s probably number one,” he said. “What’s insane is, if you look at the whole carbon cycle of liquefied natural gas imports versus domestic gas production, domestic is 15pc lower carbon emissions.

“So it’s kind of a no-brainer. You get less carbon emissions, you get more jobs, and you get more economic activity, therefore higher tax revenue.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Reeves promises help on soaring factory energy bills – but only in a year’s time
data.description:
British factories face a year-long wait for relief from soaring energy bills despite Rachel Reeves’s suggestion that help is on the way.

The Chancellor promised on Sunday to “set out the next phase of our plans to boost Britain’s competitiveness” in response to high energy costs, which are worsening because of the war in Iran.

But The Telegraph understands that one of the Government’s key measures to cut the country’s crippling industrial energy bills cannot start any earlier than April 2027.

Business groups urged Ms Reeves to move faster, with a wave of inflation already hitting companies. New surveys of confidence and output suggest both have already fallen to lows not seen since the pandemic.

Addressing the “global economic crisis” triggered by war Iran, the Chancellor wrote over the weekend that she would “set out the principles that will guide how we support businesses in the months ahead”.

Ms Reeves is this week expected to outline details of the British Industrial Competitiveness Scheme (BICS), which will see green levies on electricity costs waived for about 7,000 companies in eight key sectors.

However, a government source indicated that the Chancellor would not bring forward the timelines for the programme to launch. It means factories face a year-long wait for relief at a time when energy costs are soaring.

‘Essential’ to move faster

The Confederation of British Industry (CBI) lobby group said it was “essential” to move faster. “It needs to be delivered at pace. Any delay risks undermining business confidence at a time when it is already under strain,” said Tania Kumar, the CBI’s director for energy transition.

In a survey of 3,000 British voters conducted by the Institute for Economic Affairs, 77pc wanted the Government to focus on cutting the cost of energy. Similarly large majorities urged the Government to focus on economic growth, and cut taxes on workers and businesses.

In an article in The Sunday Times, the Chancellor said she did not want to be rushed into potentially expensive or ineffective policies.

Her hesitancy came as Donald Trump on Sunday said the US Navy would block the Strait of Hormuz – a vital transit route at least one fifth of the world’s oil, gas and fertiliser – to any ship that had paid a toll to Iran.

This could extend a conflict that has already squeezed energy supply chains and driven up fuel and electricity costs. Brent crude prices jumped as high as $104 a barrel on Monday morning after closing on Friday at $94.

Ian Stewart, the Deloitte chief economist, said the Iran war was “reshaping business sentiment” after a survey of finance chiefs by the consultancy found confidence at a six-year low. The barometer fell to -57pc in the first three months of this year, from minus 13pc the previous quarter.

Mr Stewart said: “It’s created a shock to CFO confidence, lowering optimism to levels we haven’t seen since the early days of the Covid-19 pandemic.

“Finance leaders are coping with high levels of external uncertainty, and their focus is on managing risks from geopolitics, rising energy prices and higher financing costs.”

A separate survey of 4,000 businesses by accountancy firm BDO found output had dropped to a five-year low.

“The decline in business output and confidence underlines the UK’s exposure to the global energy shock,” said Scott Knight, head of growth at BDO.

“Confidence will not be restored until energy flows stabilise under a lasting ceasefire.”

BDO warned the combination of increasing price pressures and falling demand would prompt businesses to stop hiring workers, with employment intentions at a 15-year low.

Britain’s manufacturers were already labouring under the highest electricity prices in Europe, but these will rise still further if the Iran conflict keeps squeezing oil and gas supplies.

Ms Kumar said the Chancellor was “right to resist the lure of short-term, unaffordable sticking plasters”, but she needed to seize this moment to make Britain more cost-competitive.

The BICS is a seven-year scheme to exempt businesses from paying two green levies and an energy-security charge. This could reduce their electricity costs by up to £40 per megawatt hour.

Scheme ‘does not go far enough’

To be eligible, factories must be in economically critical sectors. These include advanced manufacturing, clean energy, defence, digital technologies and life sciences. Suppliers to these sectors, such as makers of chemicals, steel or critical minerals, can also qualify.

Responding to a consultation on the BICS that ended in January, the Aluminium Federation said its members had “consistently reported that the proposed April 2027 implementation date is far too late”.

The Chemicals Industry Association told the Government that the scheme was not as generous as others in the EU, and “does not go far enough to make the UK a globally competitive manufacturing location”.

Other business groups supported the BICS but warned the government not to fund the scheme by increasing levies on companies that were not part of it.

Elexon, a company that provides data and settlement services to the energy industry, said getting BICS up and running by next April would be “very challenging”.

Its submission said the Government had to amend laws and build new processes and systems, and much of the groundwork for that would need to be finished by early this summer.

The Treasury declined to comment on speculation that Ms Reeves would this week announce an expansion of the BICS.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Prepare for half-term flight cancellations, warn airlines
data.description:
Airlines have warned passengers to expect flight cancellations during the May half-term holiday as the Iran war squeezes fuel supplies.

The International Air Transport Association (IATA), which represents more than 360 airlines, said that flight disruption will spread from Asia to Europe by the end of next month.

The International Energy Agency (IEA) has warned that Europe risks running out of jet fuel within six weeks after the closure of the Strait of Hormuz trapped a fifth of the world’s oil and gas supplies in the Gulf.

Willie Walsh, the director-general of the IATA, described the IEA’s warning as “sobering”.

He said: “We have also estimated that by the end of May, we could start to see some cancellations in Europe for lack of jet fuel. This is already happening in parts of Asia.”

In normal circumstances, airlines must use their allocated slots at least 80pc of the time under “use it or lose it” rules. However, the IATA is seeking a waiver in the event of rationing.

“Along with doing everything possible to secure alternative supply lines, it’s important that authorities have well-communicated and well-coordinated plans in place in case rationing becomes necessary, including for slot relief,” he said.

Two of Europe’s biggest airlines have begun cancelling hundreds of flights in a sign of the potential disruption ahead.

Lufthansa has announced plans to shut down its regional airline by this weekend while the Netherlands-based carrier KLM has axed 160 flights for the coming month.

European jet fuel prices have surged by more than 120pc since the start of the war, according to the IATA. European countries are particularly vulnerable to fuel supply shortages because of their heavy reliance on imports from the Middle East.

Around 40pc of Britain’s supplies came from the Gulf before the Iran conflict started. The UK has since switched to sourcing jet fuel from the US, with record American imports expected this month and next.

Michael O’Leary, the Ryanair boss, has sounded the alarm over flight cancellations if the war continues to drag on. EasyJet has said higher jet fuel prices cost it about £25m last month alone, though bosses played down fears of shortages.

Despite repeated warnings from the industry, ministers continue to maintain that supplies are secure.

Rachel Reeves, the Chancellor, told the BBC this week that the Government was “monitoring the situation very carefully” but was “confident” of current supplies.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: If Labour is serious about growth, it must open up the North Sea
data.description:
One consequence of the current surge in global energy prices is that it has turned the spotlight onto Britain’s policy with regard to its unexploited supplies of fossil fuels. Sixty-five leading climate scientists recently wrote an open letter to the Financial Times arguing against new drilling in the North Sea. They said that this would imperil the fight against climate change while bringing no economic benefit. Do they have a point?

The argument primarily concerns the Jackdaw and Rosebank fields. These two fields are not covered by the ban on new licenses since their applications had already been submitted when Labour took office.

The issue here is not climate science which, despite the claim that everything in this subject is settled, remains controversial. But we don’t need to get into that. Rather, the issue here is about economics.

The climate authors advance several arguments. One is that 90pc of the energy reserves in the North Sea have already been exploited. This is true but so what? Why does this mean that we should leave the remaining 10pc unexploited?

Another is that it is not worth opening up new supplies from the North Sea because this would have little or no effect on the prices of gas and oil here because these are set globally. This is true but allowing extra domestic production would create more jobs in the parts of the country that have suffered from the rundown of the North Sea industry and produce increased tax revenues.

The UK’s trade deficit would be correspondingly reduced. So long as this activity is profitable, it will bring benefit to the UK economy. And the people to judge whether or not it is profitable are the energy companies, which want to have the chance to exploit these two North Sea fields.

Indeed, it would be wrong to use the availability of domestic supplies to keep prices down artificially. Domestic consumers of energy should face world prices for energy. In that way they will be incentivised to minimise their consumption of it. But profitable domestic energy supply can improve UK economic performance such that the Government could give relief to consumers in other ways, rather than artificially keeping the price down.

The great irony about our restrictions on UK fossil fuel production is that it is not unambiguously reducing emissions. We import 35pc of our gas from Norway. The fact that this gas is produced there rather than in the UK does nothing to reduce carbon emissions. Indeed, this imported gas may generate higher emissions than our own domestically produced gas. This is the height of absurdity.

Some defenders of the current policy claim that renewable energy is cheaper. If that were true, than we surely ought to rush headlong towards replacing all fossil fuel energy with renewables. But it isn’t. Admittedly, it is true that the marginal cost of renewable energy is often lower than for fossil fuel produced energy. But this ignores the fact that because of the intermittency of renewables – sometimes the wind does not blow and/or the sun does not shine – there has to be huge and expensive duplicate capacity.

An argument that we hear often is that the UK’s energy policy is setting an example to the world. This betrays an exaggerated view of the UK’s importance in both global energy markets and in the world more generally. Indeed, it harks back to the days of empire. We are now only a medium-sized economy.

Admittedly, there is scheduled to be a major international conference on climate change later this month in Columbia. The UK will be one of about 50 countries represented. Allowing new drilling in the North Sea could potentially cause the UK government considerable embarrassment at this forum.

But so what? What is the market price of embarrassment? The big boys in global energy are the US and China. Needless to say, they are continuing to consume and produce large amounts of energy from fossil fuels. For all the effect our policy has on them, we might as well wave a union jack over the North Sea. (Come to think of it, though, this might cause distress and therefore
requires a trigger warning.)

Allowing drilling in the Jackdaw and Rosebank fields would not in itself transform UK energy supply or the economy more generally. But it would be a start. The Government repeatedly tells us that its overriding ambition is to deliver decent economic growth for the UK. And within this, it sees manufacturing as key. Yet the UK has some of the highest industrial energy prices in the developed world. They have recently been three times America’s. Scarce wonder, then, that UK manufacturing has been in the doldrums.

But the damage does not stop there. The Government is relying on the UK’s prominent position in AI to deliver decent economic growth. Yet AI is a huge consumer of energy. Worryingly, OpenAI has recently announced that it is halting its UK data centre project, citing high energy cost and concerns about regulation.

By wanting to secure cheap energy for the UK, this does not mean that we necessarily reject the whole policy agenda against global warming. The issue is about the pace of change, the cost, and how a medium-sized economy should behave when the big boys are setting the pace. Even climate change sceptics would surely acknowledge that it would be good if we can eventually wean ourselves off reliance on fossil fuels, because so much of the world’s supplies of gas and oil are located in places with hostile governments (Russia) or in areas of instability (the Middle East).

Yet this does not mean that we should impoverish ourselves in the process.

Roger Bootle is senior independent adviser to Capital Economics and a senior fellow at Policy Exchange. roger.bootle@capitaleconomics.com

data.title: Best cash Isas: Today’s latest rates
data.description:
The start of a new tax year is a great time for Isas. As savers get their new Isa allowance, we’re seeing cash Isa providers compete for your cash with increasingly generous rates.

A cash Isa is similar to a savings account, but with a crucial difference – you don’t pay tax on the interest you earn.

It is one of four types of adult individual savings accounts (Isas) – the others are stocks and shares Isas, innovative finance Isas and lifetime Isas.

You can currently save up to £20,000 per tax year, which is known as the Isa allowance. In the 2025 Budget, Chancellor Rachel Reeves announced plans to reduce this to £12,000 from April 2027 for savers under the age of 65; younger savers will have to use an investment Isa if they wish to use the remaining £8,000 of their allowance.

You can either deposit this sum into one account or spread it across several, and you can transfer your Isa savings between providers if you want to take advantage of a better rate elsewhere.

In this guide, Telegraph Money explains how cash Isas work, covering:

  • How we determine the best rates
  • The best cash Isas in April
  • Expert opinion: Things to consider when choosing a cash Isa
  • Why choose a cash Isa?
  • Cash Isa FAQs

How we determine the best rates

The Best Buy tables below show the best savings rates widely available in the market. This means certain accounts are excluded, including those that are available only to local or existing customers.

The data in these tables is provided by Savings Data Limited and is compiled using automated tracker tools and updates from savings providers. Savings Data Limited then manually checks the information and enters it into a database that feeds the live tables, which update daily.

The savings accounts shown are protected by the Financial Services Compensation Scheme (FSCS). The information in this article is intended for information purposes and should not be taken as endorsement or advice.

The best cash Isas in April 2026

Find the best cash Isa for you using the tables below.

Best easy-access cash Isas

These accounts usually allow you to make as many deposits and withdrawals as you like – but this flexibility can come with lower rates. For the savings equivalent, you can see our guide to the best easy-access savings accounts.

Best variable rate cash Isas

Accounts in the table below show the best Isa rates on the market for accounts that offer a variable rate of interest. While this could increase at any time – which can be valuable when rates are on an upward trend – it could also decrease.

Best notice cash Isas

These accounts require you to give a certain amount of notice to your provider when you want to make a withdrawal, indicating the number of days you must wait until your money is released. You can usually make as many withdrawals as you like – but be sure to check the account terms first.

If this way of saving suits you, make sure you check our guide to the best notice accounts for alternative options.

Best fixed-rate cash Isas

These Isas require you to lock your money away for a specified period of time. The rate of interest is guaranteed throughout this period, which can give you peace of mind should other rates take a tumble.

For the savings account equivalent, which you might want to use if you’ve used up your Isa allowance, see our guide to the best fixed-rate bonds.

For other types of tax-free saving, see our guide to the best Junior cash Isas for under-18s. Or, if you’re saving to buy a home or fund your retirement, don’t miss our guide to the best lifetime Isas.

Expert opinion: Things to consider when choosing a cash Isa

Choosing the best cash Isa for you can be tricky, as it depends on why you’re saving and how you want to save – but there should be options to suit everyone.

Caitlyn Eastell, of analyst Moneyfactscompare, said: “For savers that are more tax savvy, cash Isas may be the ideal option as you receive a £20,000 Isa allowance each year and can pay into multiple Isas within the same period providing this limit is not exceeded. Isas aren’t too dissimilar to their traditional savings counterparts with easy access, notice, fixed and regular accounts being an option.

“It is worth considering that for the variable rate options, interest could increase or decrease over time, and those Isas that are fixed for an agreed term will either not allow earlier access or may deduct a certain amount of interest.”

Cash Isas can also be an effective way of saving for the longer term.

Mark Hicks, of Hargreaves Lansdown, said: “Cash Isas have certainly seen a resurgence of late. Looking at what may be coming in Labour’s Budget, and the rhetoric around higher taxes, cash Isas should be the first port of call for all savers at the moment.

“If you’ve got £20,000 or less, definitely go for a cash Isa first and ensure your money is protected from tax. If you’re fortunate enough to have more than £20,000 to save in an Isa, then you can look at other saving accounts as well.”

Why choose a cash Isa?

Cash Isas are beneficial for many savers as they offer an easy way to make sure your savings interest is sheltered from income tax.

Their popularity took a dip when the personal savings allowance (PSA) was introduced in 2016, as it means some savers can earn up to £1,000 in tax-free savings interest each tax year. When interest rates are low, only those with large savings pots or high incomes come into the scope of paying tax on savings interest.

However, savings rates have been high for some time; until recently it was not uncommon to be earning 5pc. Someone with a £1,000 PSA could face a savings tax bill with £20,000 in savings – a problem that doesn’t occur with a cash Isa. What’s more, your growth is protected as it compounds for years into the future, which has helped some savers become Isa millionaires.

The main issue that comes with a cash Isa is the £20,000 Isa limit on how much you can pay into it each tax year. If you want to move a large savings deposit in order to protect your returns from tax, it may need to be moved gradually over several tax years.

In some cases, interest rates offered by cash Isas can be lower than their savings equivalent. Providers are well aware of the extra tax benefit Isas offer, and know they don’t have to be as competitive with rates.

It’s important to shop around and find the most competitive rate you can. Cash Isa rates can be lower than their savings account counterparts, but the tax-free benefits of an Isa will often outweigh the loss of a little interest.

If you’re not sure whether you could be in danger of having to pay tax on your savings interest, try the Telegraph Money savings tax calculator – simply enter your savings, interest rate and tax band and it can tell you whether you’d be better off getting an Isa.

Cash Isa FAQs

There is no limit to the number of cash Isas you can have – but it’s a good idea not to have so many that you lose track of them.

You can hold cash Isas you’ve paid into in past tax years, and – now that Isa rules have changed – you can also open and pay into as many as you like in the same tax year. The key thing is to make sure you don’t pay in more than £20,000 across all Isas in the same tax year.

Yes, you can combine Isas into one account – but in order to do this without affecting the tax status of the money, or your Isa allowance, you must move the cash via an Isa transfer.

There are quite a few rules to bear in mind when you do this – you can find out more in our comprehensive guide to Isa transfers.

This depends on the rate you’re earning and the rate of inflation. At the time of writing, lots of cash Isas can beat inflation, but you might need to shop around for them. It’s a good idea to keep an eye on what’s happening with inflation versus the best Isa rates so you can gauge whether or not you have a competitive deal.

Our inflation calculator shows the effect rising prices can have on your savings.

It’s never too late to put money in an Isa to benefit from its tax-free status. However, if you’re planning to deposit money very close to the end of the tax year on April 5, there’s a chance you could miss the deadline.

If this happens, you can still save the money – it will just be taken from the following year’s allowance instead.

The annual Isa allowance resets at the start of a tax year on April 6. You can choose to put this entire amount into one account, or split it among several.

Mr Hicks said: “Isas were typically seasonal, with a big spike in demand around the end of the tax year. They are much less timely now and every year the cash Isa season gets longer and longer.”

data.title: ‘Not worried’ about shadow banking risk, says JP Morgan boss
data.description:
The boss of JP Morgan has said that he is “not worried” about his own bank’s $50bn (£37bn) exposure to private credit, despite recent warnings over the risks of “shadow banking”.

Jamie Dimon said that JP Morgan was well insulated from any wider ructions in the private credit sector.

The chief executive told analysts: “You’re going to have very large losses in private credit before it looks like banks are going to get hit. 

“It doesn’t mean you won’t feel some stress and strain, and you might have to do something about it, but I’m not particularly worried about it.”

His comments come despite growing alarm over the private credit industry, following a series of high-profile failures of shadow bank-backed businesses.

Private credit is a form of shadow banking, where loans come from non-banking sources such as hedge funds, which are subject to less strict regulations. The sector has grown rapidly since the financial crisis, as tighter regulations on traditional banks pushed riskier forms of finance into unregulated markets.

However, concerns have been mounting over loose lending standards and the opacity of the sector, with Lloyd Blankfein, the former boss of Goldman Sachs, saying earlier this year that he could “smell” a crash on the horizon.

Andrew Bailey called for a review on Tuesday of how private credit and other non-banks are regulated.

The governor of the Bank of England said the current regime needed to “have its tyres kicked”, adding there needed to be clear distinctions between protecting trust in money while avoiding the need to bail out institutions that make risky bets.

Speaking in New York, he said: “It remains an open question to consider where are the limits of what an independent central bank should do in the financial stability space.”

In the past, Mr Dimon has warned about the private credit sector, saying more “cockroaches” could emerge after the collapse of the sub-prime auto lender Tricolor and the car parts supplier First Brands.

Both companies were heavy users of private credit. JP Morgan took a $170m hit from the collapse of Tricolor.

The US banking giant said in its first-quarter results that its overall exposure to the private credit sector was about $50bn.

Mr Dimon had also warned in his annual letter at the start of this month that losses from private credit were likely to be larger than expected, as lending standards in the sector had been “modestly weakening pretty much across the board”.

In a call with analysts on Tuesday, Mr Dimon reiterated the point. However, he said that any losses were unlikely to be systemic given that, at $1.7tn, the private credit market was still small compared with other forms of lending.

“It almost can’t be systemic at that size relative to anything else,” he said.

Mr Dimon was speaking as JP Morgan reported a 13pc increase in net profits to $16.5bn in the first three months of the year. Volatility in markets, fuelled by the war in Iran, led the bank to report its highest-ever trading revenue of $11.6bn, surpassing the bank’s previous record by almost $2bn and marking a 20pc increase on last year.

It follows similar increases in profits at rival Wall Street banks, which are also benefiting from their role financing trades during the Middle East turmoil.

Between them, JP Morgan, Citi and Wells Fargo made more than $25bn in profits during the first three months of the year.
